text: Research Article Recent mass transport deposits in the Gulf of Cagliari Maria Cristina Caradonna a,*, Anna Del Ben a, Gian Andrea Pini a, Riccardo Geletti b, Veronica Frisicchio a a Department of Mathematic, Informatics and Geosciences, Universit`a degli studi di Trieste, Via Weiss, 1, 34128 Trieste, Italy b National Institute of Oceanography and Applied Geophysics – OGS, Borgo Grotta Gigante 42/C, Sgonico, 34010 Trieste, Italy A R T I C L E I N F O Editor: Shu Gao Keywords: Southern Sardinia offshore Mass transport deposits Submarine geomorphology Multi-channel seismic profiles A B S T R A C T Sedimentary deposits due to slope failures and submarine landslides are common on continental margins. The availability of five new high resolution 2D multi-channel seismic profiles, integrated by Multibeam data, pro- vides unprecedented insight into the shallow submarine geomorphology of the Gulf of Cagliari. The study area, located in the southern Sardinia offshore, exhibits several Mass Transport Deposits (MTDs) during the Upper Quaternary. They move from the continental slope and the north-east flank of the Banghittu High. We identified the recent 26 km long MTD-CG1, of which we interpreted the translational domain, charac- terized by weak deformation, and the toe domain, with a predominantly compressional and erosional regime. This erosion is produced locally by the basal shear zone (bsz) which represents the detachment surface of the slide. Both MTD domains are crossed by vertical conduits and fluid migration that produce buried pockmarks. In the translational domain an underlying carbonate buildup offered greater strength to sliding, resulting in an undisturbed sector. The MTD-CG1 is composed of Middle-Upper Pleistocene sediments which, during the Ho- locene, assumed an initial direction towards SE, to become towards the SW in the Carbonara Valley. After the gliding, the MTD was cut by the last incision phase of the

detachment surface of the slide. Both MTD domains are crossed by vertical conduits and fluid migration that produce buried pockmarks. In the translational domain an underlying carbonate buildup offered greater strength to sliding, resulting in an undisturbed sector. The MTD-CG1 is composed of Middle-Upper Pleistocene sediments which, during the Ho- locene, assumed an initial direction towards SE, to become towards the SW in the Carbonara Valley. After the gliding, the MTD was cut by the last incision phase of the canyon system which markedly characterizes the physiography of the gulf. The MTD-CG1 is primarily predisposed to instability by its steep slope gradient and fluid presence, while possible triggers include eustatic sea level changes and seismic activity. 1. Introduction The Mediterranean Sea is highly vulnerable to offshore geological hazards, including seismic activity, sedimentary slope instabilities, and fluid flow processes (Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013; Ercilla et al., 2021). Submarine landslides represent one of the most significant geo- hazards in the region, resulting from a combination of preconditioning factors and triggering mechanisms, which vary depending on the spe- cific geological and environmental contexts. One of the most frequent triggers is a sudden increase in pore pres- sure, which weakens sediment layers and reduces their shear strength (Bromhead and Cooper, 1992; Sultan et al., 2004). While this typically leads to small to localized failures, it can also induce larger regional events when combined with other factors. Seismic disturbances, rapid sediment deposition, and sea level fluctuations also play significant roles, depending on local conditions (Sultan et al., 2004; Canals et al., 2004). In tectonically active regions, for instance, earthquakes can weaken sediments, increasing the likelihood of slope failure (Locat et al., 2002; Masson et al., 2006). Sea level fluctuations, particularly during glacial-interglacial cycles, modify hydrostatic pressure and sedimenta- tion rates, contributing to long-term slope instability

it can also induce larger regional events when combined with other factors. Seismic disturbances, rapid sediment deposition, and sea level fluctuations also play significant roles, depending on local conditions (Sultan et al., 2004; Canals et al., 2004). In tectonically active regions, for instance, earthquakes can weaken sediments, increasing the likelihood of slope failure (Locat et al., 2002; Masson et al., 2006). Sea level fluctuations, particularly during glacial-interglacial cycles, modify hydrostatic pressure and sedimenta- tion rates, contributing to long-term slope instability (Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013; Brooks and Adams, 2020). These processes interact with preconditioning factors, such as steep gradients and weak sediment layers, determining the susceptibility to landslides. While steep slopes increase gravitational stress, failure occurs only when other factors, such as overpressured sediments or tectonic weaknesses, are present (Hampton et al., 1996; Locat et al., 2002). The combined effect of these factors contributes to slope instability on both small and large scales settings. Submarine landslides are widely recognized as a key components of continental margin evolution, recorded both in modern (Moore, 1978; Hampton et al., 1996; Masson et al., 2006; Shanmugam, 2010) and in ancient sedimentary sequences (Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013; Ogata et al., 2019). In the southern Sardinian margin, particularly in the Gulf of Cagliari, during the Plio-Quaternary, the continental slope was affected by sub- marine canyon headwall retreat and unchannelized turbiditic flows, with sediment redistribution observed on the lower slope (Paliaga, 2015). These processes likely operate on both localized and regional scales, contributing to slope instability. In the Sant’Elia-Foxy Canyon system, surface sediments are affected by flow waves, indicating ongoing gravitational instability (Deiana et al., 2017). Although the Gulf of Cagliari is not exposed to significant geological hazards, such as major earthquakes or tsunamis, gravitational instability remains a key process shaping the continental slope. Understanding these processes is crucial for reconstructing the long-term evolution of the margin and assessing potential implications for sedimentary dy- namics and slope stability. This study aims to investigate the geomorphological and geological processes driving gravitational instabilities within the Plio-Quaternary sequence of the Sardinian margin, focusing on the youngest Mass Transport Deposit (MTD) located on the continental slope of the Gulf of Cagliari. By analysing the internal architecture and external morphology of the shallowest MTD in the region, called MTD-CG1, the study explores how pre-existing morphological features have influenced its evolution. A key research question is the relationship between the development of MTD-CG1 and the incision of the submarine canyon system in the Gulf of Cagliari. Specifically, this research examines the spatial distribution, relative age, and geological impact

youngest Mass Transport Deposit (MTD) located on the continental slope of the Gulf of Cagliari. By analysing the internal architecture and external morphology of the shallowest MTD in the region, called MTD-CG1, the study explores how pre-existing morphological features have influenced its evolution. A key research question is the relationship between the development of MTD-CG1 and the incision of the submarine canyon system in the Gulf of Cagliari. Specifically, this research examines the spatial distribution, relative age, and geological impact of MTD-CG1, investigating its interaction with the Sarroch, Sant’Elia-Foxy, and Carbonara canyon systems that cross the gulf. A fundamental question arises: does MTD-CG1 predate or postdate the formation of these canyons? Answering this requires determining whether the canyons incised an already deposited MTD- CG1 or, conversely, whether the mass transport deposit moved within pre-existing canyons. Understanding the influence of the canyon system on the deposition and movement of the MTD provides critical insights into the interplay between sedimentary transport, gravitational failure, and seafloor morphology. This research contributes to a deeper understanding of submarine slope instability and mass transport processes, offering valuable perspectives on the recent geological evolution of the region. 2. Geomorphological setting Sardinia is a geologically complex island with very low seismicity, as only a few low-energy seismic events have been historically recorded, the most notable occurring in 1616 and 1771 (Meletti et al., 2020). The southern continental margin of the island is strongly influenced by tectonic structures, particularly by a major lineament running from N- NW to S-SE, aligned with the Capo S. Elia promontory (Fig. 1). Marine Geology 483 (2025) 107515 2 structure, associated with the Plio-Pleistocene extensional dynamics, delineates the boundary between the Cagliari and Sarrabus shelves and has contributed significantly to the geological evolution of the region (Ulzega et al., 1986). The Gulf of Cagliari, located on the southern continental margin of Sardinia, shows contrasting physiographic and tectonic domains. Structural highs, such as the Banghittu High and Ichnusa Seamount, further shape the submarine topography of the gulf, segmenting its sedimentary systems (Fanucci et al., 1976; Lecca et al., 1998; Meleddu et al., 2016). These features reflect the influence of extensional tectonics during the Plio-Pleistocene, which led to the formation of intrashelf and intraslope basins that serve as key sites of sediment accumulation (Lecca et al., 1988). During the Plio-Quaternary, the depositional systems of the gulf are organized into three main domains: i) the carbonate platform environ- ment, mainly represented by the Banghittu High, consists of residual submarine structures composed predominantly of carbonate-bioclastic deposits (Lecca et al., 1998); ii) slope environments, flanking struc- tural highs such as the Ichnusa Seamount, characterized by hemipelagic deposits and fine-grained turbidites that evidence gravity-driven sedi- ment transport (sensu Bouma et al.,

as key sites of sediment accumulation (Lecca et al., 1988). During the Plio-Quaternary, the depositional systems of the gulf are organized into three main domains: i) the carbonate platform environ- ment, mainly represented by the Banghittu High, consists of residual submarine structures composed predominantly of carbonate-bioclastic deposits (Lecca et al., 1998); ii) slope environments, flanking struc- tural highs such as the Ichnusa Seamount, characterized by hemipelagic deposits and fine-grained turbidites that evidence gravity-driven sedi- ment transport (sensu Bouma et al., 1985); iii) the deep-marine basin in the depocenter, where high-energy turbidites and low-energy hemi- pelagic deposits alternate (Lecca et al., 1998). The Holocene evolution of the Cagliari shelf reveals a typical three- part zonation: siliciclastic coastal sands dominate the inner shelf, bio- clastic sands produced by Posidonia oceanica characterize the mid-shelf, and sandy muds are prevalent on the outer shelf (Lecca et al., 1998). This zonation reflects active sediment transport linking the shelf to the Sardinian Valley through canyons and slope basins (Tamburrino et al., 2019). At the distal margin of the gulf, a progradational wedge on the upper slope, composed of silts and clays, represents reduced tractive energy and Holocene sedimentation processes related to the last glacial regression (Orrù et al., 2011; Sechi et al., 2023). Holocene and Late Pleistocene deposits overlay earlier Pleistocene regression cycles and Neogene sediments, incorporating evidence of sea-level fluctuations such as beach rocks and erosional features (De Muro and Orrù, 1998; Deiana et al., 2021). The western Cagliari platform, which extends about 40 km in width and has a gradient of approximately 3–4◦ in the inner platform (Meleddu et al., 2016), transitions into a slope that descends to depths of about 1260 m, where it is incised by several submarine canyons. In contrast, the eastern Sarrabus platform is narrower, only a few kilometers wide,

incorporating evidence of sea-level fluctuations such as beach rocks and erosional features (De Muro and Orrù, 1998; Deiana et al., 2021). The western Cagliari platform, which extends about 40 km in width and has a gradient of approximately 3–4◦ in the inner platform (Meleddu et al., 2016), transitions into a slope that descends to depths of about 1260 m, where it is incised by several submarine canyons. In contrast, the eastern Sarrabus platform is narrower, only a few kilometers wide, and descends rapidly to depths of about 1000 m (Fig. 2). These canyons, shaped by retrogressive erosion and gravitational instabilities, play a significant role in the region’s sedimentary and morphological dynamics (Paliaga, 2015). Among them, the Sarroch Canyon and the S. Elia Foxy Canyon System cut through the shelf break and merge in the lower slope to form the Carbonara Canyon, near the Sarrabus slope, influencing the overall geomorphology of the area (Ulzega et al., 1986) (Fig. 2). The outer Cagliari shelf and upper continental slope show retro- gressive erosion processes which impact the heads of the canyons. Intrachannel landslides have been documented in these areas (Ulzega et al., 1986; Meleddu et al., 2016), and gravity currents occasionally lead to the deposition of large blocks. Pockmark fields, commonly linked to fluid emissions from creep zones created by landslides, are often observed in similar geological settings (Riboulot et al., 2013; Deville et al., 2020; Riera et al., 2022). In addition, crescent-shaped bedforms, known as cyclic steps, have been identified within the head scarp of the Foxy Canyon (Deiana et al., 2017). These features, created by recurrent erosion and deposition driven by gravity, have wavelengths ranging from tens to a hundred meters and heights of several meters (Meleddu et al., 2016). In terms of sediment accumulation, high-energy events such as large-scale turbidity

observed in similar geological settings (Riboulot et al., 2013; Deville et al., 2020; Riera et al., 2022). In addition, crescent-shaped bedforms, known as cyclic steps, have been identified within the head scarp of the Foxy Canyon (Deiana et al., 2017). These features, created by recurrent erosion and deposition driven by gravity, have wavelengths ranging from tens to a hundred meters and heights of several meters (Meleddu et al., 2016). In terms of sediment accumulation, high-energy events such as large-scale turbidity currents or slope subsidence are ruled out in the region. Instead, sedimentation is primarily attributed to low- intensity turbidity currents or hyperpycnal flows, which are consid- ered the main contributors to sediment transport and deposition in the basin (Paliaga, 2015). 3. Material and methods The main data used in this work were collected in the Gulf of Cagliari as part of the Western Sardinia project in a collaboration between the Istituto Nazionale di Oceanografia e di Geofisica Sperimentale (OGS) and the Universities of Trieste and Cagliari. More specifically, five multichannel seismic reflection profiles (CG lines) and a multibeam echosounder bathymetry (MB) were acquired by the vessel N/R OGS Explora in September 2010 (Zgur et al., 2010). In this work, the CG profiles were integrated with the lower-resolution ES profiles from the ViDEPI project (2009) to provisionally cover the upper slope. The seismic profiles and bathymetric data were imported into a project using Petrel™ seismic interpretation software (Schlumberger ltd). 3.1. Multichannel seismic reflection profiles The acquisition of the CG profiles was optimised to investigate the Quaternary sedimentary package. The seismic streamer was a 1500-m- long cave, with 120-channels and a geophone spacing of 12.5 m. The seismic source consisted of two GI-GUNs with a total volume of 710 cubic inches (11.2 l) with an 8-s recording, for an average shot spacing

The seismic profiles and bathymetric data were imported into a project using Petrel™ seismic interpretation software (Schlumberger ltd). 3.1. Multichannel seismic reflection profiles The acquisition of the CG profiles was optimised to investigate the Quaternary sedimentary package. The seismic streamer was a 1500-m- long cave, with 120-channels and a geophone spacing of 12.5 m. The seismic source consisted of two GI-GUNs with a total volume of 710 cubic inches (11.2 l) with an 8-s recording, for an average shot spacing of 25m and a 30-fold coverage for each common midpoint. Based on the recording parameters used, the maximum available theoretical fre- quency cannot exceed 150 Hz. Based on the criterion that defines the resolution of a seismic source as λ/4 (where λ is the wavelength), it can be deduced that at an average velocity of 2000 m/s, the minimum thickness that can be recorded with this source is about 3 to 4 m. The raw data was processed with the industrial software package Echos™. The gain (compensation for transmission losses, absorption and spherical divergence) was calculated and applied by inverting the amplitude decay curve. The seismic data was then processed to obtain time- migrated sections with the stacking velocity field. Fig. 2. Study area in the Gulf of Cagliari. M.C. Caradonna et al. Bathymetric data The bathymetric data were acquired using the multibeam echosounder Reson Seabat 8111 (100 KHz) hull mounted on R/V OGS Explora (Zgur et al., 2010), which defines a resolution of 50 m along the seismic profiles. They were integrated by the open-access European Marine Observation and Data Network (EMODnet), characterized by 115 m resolution multibeam bathymetry (www.emodnet-bathymetry. eu, 2020) and by the 100 m resolution of the MaGIC (Marine Geo- hazards along the Italian Coasts) data (https://github.com/pcm- dpc/MaGIC). MaGIC

Bathymetric data The bathymetric data were acquired using the multibeam echosounder Reson Seabat 8111 (100 KHz) hull mounted on R/V OGS Explora (Zgur et al., 2010), which defines a resolution of 50 m along the seismic profiles. They were integrated by the open-access European Marine Observation and Data Network (EMODnet), characterized by 115 m resolution multibeam bathymetry (www.emodnet-bathymetry. eu, 2020) and by the 100 m resolution of the MaGIC (Marine Geo- hazards along the Italian Coasts) data (https://github.com/pcm- dpc/MaGIC). MaGIC is a project supported by the Italian Civil Protec- tion Department that aims to investigate geohazards along the Italian continental margins (Chiocci and Ridente, 2011). Data and positions of morpho-bathymetric features of the Gulf of Cagliari, from the MaGIC project, were incorporated into the study (Fig. 2). This allowed the mapping of potentially tsunamigenic land- slides, landslide crowns and creep zones. The identified seismic features were then correlated with the mapped sedimentary processes, facili- tating a better understanding of the relationships between sedimentary orientations and their source deposits. 3.3. MTD characterization The Quaternary strata were analyzed using seismic stratigraphic interpretation, with a focus on seismic facies and on geometry of sedi- mentary features, following the methodologies developed by Mitchum et al. (1977), Mulder and Cochonat (1996), Frey Martinez et al. (2005), Moscardelli et al. (2006), and Moscardelli and Wood (2008). The initial 2D analysis was further refined through the integration of geomorpho- logical elements. Seismic geomorphology techniques (Posamentier and Morris, 2000; Posamentier, 2004; Posamentier et al., 2007) were applied to identify key geological patterns at the map scale, which were subsequently examined in greater detail using the available seismic lines. The characterization of the MTDs was based on a detailed seismic facies analysis, which revealed lithofacies variations within the Upper Quaternary sedimentary unit. These deposits, often associated with gravity-driven downslope

The initial 2D analysis was further refined through the integration of geomorpho- logical elements. Seismic geomorphology techniques (Posamentier and Morris, 2000; Posamentier, 2004; Posamentier et al., 2007) were applied to identify key geological patterns at the map scale, which were subsequently examined in greater detail using the available seismic lines. The characterization of the MTDs was based on a detailed seismic facies analysis, which revealed lithofacies variations within the Upper Quaternary sedimentary unit. These deposits, often associated with gravity-driven downslope processes, were identified by their charac- teristic opaque, chaotic, and discontinuous seismic facies. MTDs, commonly referred to as submarine landslides, occur on submarine slopes and typically involve the downslope movement of materials, sometimes over considerable distances and volumes, into low-gradient areas (Hampton et al., 1996; Owen et al., 2007). Due to their high mobility, MTDs exhibit significant variability in seismic signatures (Mohrig et al., 1998; Gee et al., 1999). To characterize the MTDs, we first analyzed the geomorphological features and internal architecture, following the framework established by Moscardelli and Wood (2008). Seismic reflection data were inter- preted to identify variations in the continuity, amplitude, and configu- ration of the internal reflections. These parameters allowed us the recognition of gravitational deposits and their stratigraphic relation- ships within the depositional succession. MTDs were classified as chaotic or transparent, based on their internal seismic reflection configuration, which varies according to the nature of the starting materials and the distance over which the material has traveled (Masson et al., 2006; Rovere et al., 2014). The basal shear zone (bsz) or basal continuous surface, a key struc- tural feature in MTDs, was identified and analyzed. This surface repre- sents a deformation zone between the mass transport material and the underlying substrate (Frey Martinez et al., 2005; Alves and Lourenço, 2010; Richardson et al., 2011). The

seismic reflection configuration, which varies according to the nature of the starting materials and the distance over which the material has traveled (Masson et al., 2006; Rovere et al., 2014). The basal shear zone (bsz) or basal continuous surface, a key struc- tural feature in MTDs, was identified and analyzed. This surface repre- sents a deformation zone between the mass transport material and the underlying substrate (Frey Martinez et al., 2005; Alves and Lourenço, 2010; Richardson et al., 2011). The bsz is crucial for understanding the dynamics of material flow and the associated kinematic behavior (Alves, 2010; Fossen and Cavalcante, 2017; Cardona et al., 2020; Sobiesiak et al., 2018). The study of the bsz focused on evaluating the factors influencing the transformation of mass flow styles, including material viscosity, water absorption, frictional stress, flow acceleration, and interactions with underlying lithologies (Mohrig et al., 1998, 1999; Elverhøi et al., 2000; Lucente and Pini, 2003; Posamentier and Kolla, 2003; Ilstad et al., 2004; De Blasio et al., 2005; Moscardelli et al., 2006; Posamentier and Walker, 2006; Moscardelli and Wood, 2008; Alves, 2010; De Blasio, 2011; Dey et al., 2016; Otsubo et al., 2018). We applied the model of Lewis (1971) to examine the distribution of deformation along the bsz, categorizing it into three domains: head, translational and toe domains. Although the available seismic lines include both CG and ES profiles, only the CG profiles cross the translational and toe domains. These profiles do not cover the headwall and the upper slope regions. In contrast, the ES profiles, available on the ViDEPI website, have lower resolution, limiting their ability to identify the MTD analyzed in this study. Only the ES-319 profile has been useful for this study. Therefore, our analysis concentrated on the translational and toe domains, where the CG profiles provided sufficient

seismic lines include both CG and ES profiles, only the CG profiles cross the translational and toe domains. These profiles do not cover the headwall and the upper slope regions. In contrast, the ES profiles, available on the ViDEPI website, have lower resolution, limiting their ability to identify the MTD analyzed in this study. Only the ES-319 profile has been useful for this study. Therefore, our analysis concentrated on the translational and toe domains, where the CG profiles provided sufficient detail to observe the key features and structures. The toe domain was characterized by compressive structures and thrust systems, with movements occurring perpendicular to the strike of thrust faults (Prior et al., 1984; Frey Martinez et al., 2005; Bull et al., 2009). The overall morphology of the toe domain often shows a convex descending profile, indicating the downslope termination of the MTD. Given the absence of calibrations and sediment core data in the continental slope, the age of the MTD-CG1 was estimated using a pro- portional depth-age method, which was the only possible approach for dating the deposition. The depths of the MTD-CG1 base and top below the sea bed were compared with the established ages of 0 million years (Ma) for the sea bed and 2.58 Ma for the Quaternary base reflector (International Commission of Stratigraphy, 2023). This method pro- vided an approximation of the MTD-CG1 deposition age, assuming a constant sedimentation rate throughout the Quaternary. Although sediment decompaction was considered, its influence was found to be negligible. In the absence of sediment cores and calibration data, the applied method offers valuable insights into the temporal framework of the MTD-CG1 deposition. 3.4. Seismic attributes To characterize seismic facies and assess their lateral variations, a selection of seismic attributes was applied to reflection seismic data. These attributes facilitated the detection of fluid presence and the identification of structural features within mass transport deposits (MTDs). Given the variety of seismic attributes that can be derived from raw reflection data, it is essential to choose those that best highlight relevant geological features. As noted by Col´eou et al. (2003), “there is no rule for selecting which attribute will best illustrate changes in rock properties.” Consequently, we employed several attributes, each selected for its ability to reveal key sedimentary characteristics. 1) Regular reflection amplitude: this basic attribute is derived from the raw seismic data and serves as a foundation for others. Strong amplitude variations indicate significant impedance contrasts, reflecting differences in velocity and density within the subsurface. These contrasts are particularly sensitive to fluid variations, as different fluids influence seismic wave propagation. 2) Envelope (also known as instantaneous amplitude): representing the amplitude of the oscillatory function, this attribute highlights structural discontinuities, lithological boundaries, faults and depo- sitional changes, providing insights into the lateral variation of sedimentary facies. 3) Local structural dip: serving as an edge-detection method, this attribute identifies rapid changes in local dip, such as at channel edges or faults. Using two-way travel time (TWT) profiles, it offers qualitative insights into structural features, aiding in the identifica- tion of key geological features influencing sedimentary processes. M.C. Results Gravitational seabed instabilities within the Upper Quaternary sedimentary sequence of the slope in the Gulf of Cagliari have been analyzed and mapped, with particular attention to the MTD-CG1 and the Banghittu Landslide (BLs). These features were identified, described, and mapped to reveal their spatial distribution and associated geological characteristics (Fig. 3b, 4). The MTD-CG1 extends approximately 26 km in a NW-SE direction (Fig. 3). The translational domain has an average length of 16 km and an average width of 9 km, with bathymetric slopes ranging between 1.5◦ and 3◦ (Fig. 3a). The toe domain borders to the SE with the Carbonara Canyon and to the north of the Ichnusa High, ex- tends further towards SW, for about 8 km as seen from the bathymetric step. The toe domain, instead, is characterized by gentler bathymetric slopes, ranging between 0.5◦ and 2◦ (Fig. 3a). The rise of fluids within the MTD is observed in both domains (Fig. 3b). In contrast, the Ban- ghittu Landslide (BLs) sliding phenomenon is located on the NE slope of the Banghittu High (Fig. 3), where the bathymetric gradient varies be- tween 5◦ and 9◦ (Fig. 3a). 4.1. In contrast, the Ban- ghittu Landslide (BLs) sliding phenomenon is located on the NE slope of the Banghittu High (Fig. 3), where the bathymetric gradient varies be- tween 5◦ and 9◦ (Fig. 3a). 4.1. The MTD-CG1 The MTD-CG1 is located at depths of 600 to 1000 m below the seabed and is bounded at its base by a slightly inclined seismic marker with negative polarity, corresponding to the bsz. This boundary separates the displaced mass from the underlying material. The thickness of MTD-CG1 varies across its domains and is covered by a thin sedimentary layer whose thickness sometimes approaches the limits of seismic resolution (Fig. 4). The seismic facies of MTD-CG1 predominantly exhibit low- amplitude acoustic reflections, with distinct characters in each morpho-structural domain. 4.1.1. Translational domain In the translational domain the MTD-CG1 is characterized by alter- nating undisturbed and deformed sectors. In the so-called undisturbed sector, parallel reflectors are observed, with a thickness averaging 38 ms TWT (Figs. 5, 6). It is located in the middle slope area, between the Sarroch and S. Elia-Foxy canyons below the MTD, and is marked by an opaque seismic facies and topped by inclined reflector (Figs. 5, 6). This structure, at a depth of about 100 ms TWT below the seabed, exhibits a mound-shaped reflection configuration, lateral seismic facies changes and draping effects of the overlying layers. This undisturbed sector consists of unaffected, undeformed sediments, while the MTD flowed around it without disrupting its internal structure. Thrusts and back-thrusts are located at the flanks of the undisturbed sector, where attribute analysis shows

This structure, at a depth of about 100 ms TWT below the seabed, exhibits a mound-shaped reflection configuration, lateral seismic facies changes and draping effects of the overlying layers. This undisturbed sector consists of unaffected, undeformed sediments, while the MTD flowed around it without disrupting its internal structure. Thrusts and back-thrusts are located at the flanks of the undisturbed sector, where attribute analysis shows the highest dip values (Fig. 5e). These structures converge on the bsz, with the floor detachment acting as a forethrust, marking the main kinematic features of the deposit. The MTD-CG1 is characterized by numerous pockmarks indicating fluid migration, sometimes buried several tens of milliseconds TWT below the seabed. They are concentrated within the MTD (Figs. 5, 6, 7), particularly in the intra and lower slope regions, between the Sarrabus and Banghittu highs. Fluids migrate through vertical or oblique con- duits, intruding into cohesive portions of the mass and generating widespread pockmarks in the Upper Quaternary sedimentary unit (Figs. 5, 6, 7). Envelope attribute analysis (Figs. 5c, 7c) highlights a high contrast in acoustic impedance. The bsz appears as a discontinuous basal surface, affected by the presence of fluids (Figs. 5, 6). The translational domain of MTD-CG1, located between the north- eastern flank of Banghittu to the west and the Sarroch canyon to the northeast, is marked by the presence of pockmarks and fluid migration within the deposit, as well as thrusts and back-thrusts (Fig. 7). The discontinuous thickness of the MTD-CG1 visible in Fig. 7, particularly evident towards the southeast, produces its decreasing to around 20 ms. Toe domain The toe domain of the MTD-CG1, located on the lower slope of the gulf, exhibits chaotic seismic facies, thrust systems, and a southward- inclined bsz (Figs. 8, 9, 10). The thickness of the MTD-CG1 reaches a maximum value of approximately 50 ms TWT (Fig. 8), decreasing towards the south-east sector to around 26 ms TWT (Fig. 9). In this outer sector, some contin- uous internal reflectors with negative polarity display downlapping terminations, indicative of a prograding sedimentary body. Thrust systems are generally rooted in the bsz, but also occur as isolated features (Figs. 8, 9). MTD-CG1 reaches a maximum value of approximately 50 ms TWT (Fig. 8), decreasing towards the south-east sector to around 26 ms TWT (Fig. 9). In this outer sector, some contin- uous internal reflectors with negative polarity display downlapping terminations, indicative of a prograding sedimentary body. Thrust systems are generally rooted in the bsz, but also occur as isolated features (Figs. 8, 9). These structures exhibit both low and high- angle orientations, with the highest dip values identified in the struc- tural attribute analysis (Fig. 8c). The toe domain, extending from the southeastern sector near the Banghittu High to the Carbonara Canyon, exhibits erosive interactions of the bsz with the underlying substrate (Fig. 10d). A distinct bathymetric step (Fig. 10e) defines the southwestern limit of the MTD-CG1, resulting in localized thickening (approximately from 25 to 60 ms TWT) (Fig. 10b). At the foot of the Banghittu High, the MTD-CG1 terminates, with internal SW-verging thrust originating from local compression. To the northeast, the deposit is truncated by the Carbonara Canyon (Fig. 10a). Fluid migration up through the sedimentary sequence is also observed, as emphasized by the envelope attribute (Fig. 10c). Even in this domain, fluid migration occurs through vertical or oblique conduits, facilitating the formation of pockmarks. Beneath the toe domain of the MTD-CG1, an older buried MTD, referred to as MTD-CG4, is found within the sedimentary sequence. This unit is characterized by a transparent and chaotic seismic facies (Fig. 8). 4.2. Other recent mass movement systems On the northeast flank of Banghittu High, the here called Banghittu Landslide (BLs) has been identified (Fig. 11). To the southwest, the headwall is characterized by small tilted blocks sliding towards the deeper sector. The upper layers exhibit a wavy sliding pattern, also affecting the seabed (Fig. 11). Towards the northeast, the BLs becomes discontinuous and thins as it progresses deeper, eventually reaching the toe domain, where material accumulates. A basal detachment surface defines the sliding interface. Beneath the BLs, the older mass transport deposit MTD-CG2 is observed. In its headwall domain, parallel and continuous seismic layers are interrupted by a fault rooted in the bsz (Fig. 11b), which induces the chaotic rearrangement of the sediments. On the steeper sections (Fig. 11b), the body slides down, contributing to the accumulation at the slope base. Seismic imaging shows a deformation of the seabed concordant with the top of the chaotic layers of the MTD. It should also be noted the buried MTD recognized in the profile of Fig. 8 at a depth of about 1400 ms TWT. It reached a thickness of about 70 ms TWT, corresponding to 50–60 m. MTD-CG1 and recent canyons: A relative age assessment The Sarroch Canyon originates near the northern Banghittu High, initially following an east-west orientation. As it progresses downslope, its course shifts to a NW-SE direction before merging with the S. Elia- Foxy Canyon, ultimately forming the Carbonara Canyon. Seismic pro- files (Figs. 4, 11, 12, 14) clearly show that the MTD-CG1 has been incised by canyon formation, with remnants preserved along both canyon flanks, particularly within the translational domain. In the Sarroch Canyon, an earlier phase of incision (C2 in Fig. 12a) was subsequently infilled and overlain by sub-horizontal sediments, which include reworked deposits of the MTD-CG1. The incision phase C1 represents the most recent erosional event, distinctly postdating the MTD-CG1. This phase is also observed in the S. Elia-Foxy and Carbonara Canyons (Fig. 12b, c), confirming the relative age assessment. The seismic section located near the Sarroch Canyon (red outline in Fig. 11a) shows continuous sediment deposition throughout the Qua- ternary, providing a suitable reference for estimating the age of the MTD-CG1. Interval velocities obtained from velocity analysis (Fig. 13a) were used to depth-convert the seismic profile (Fig. 13b), allowing the calculation of deposition ages. By applying a proportional depth-age method, we determined that the base of the MTD-CG1 is located at 53 m, while its top is at 13 m below the sea bed (Fig. 13b). These depths correspond to estimated ages of approximately 250 ky (yellow arrow in Fig. 13c) and 60 ky (blue arrow in Fig. 13c). The MTD-CG1 consists of Middle Pleistocene sediments, which have been deformed by gravity sliding from the Holocene to the Present. Fig. 4. a) Seismic profile crossing the MTD-CG1.

Evidence of fluids Presence of diffuse fluids and fluid escape structures have been evi- denced within the Upper Quaternary succession in the Gulf of Cagliari (Fig. 3). The phenomenon is particularly concentrated in the intra- and lower slope regions, between the Sarrabus shelf and Banghittu High. Fluid upwelling affects the MTD-CG1, highlighted by widespread pockmarks (Figs. 5, 6, 7, 10). These fluid escapes occur through vertical or oblique conduits into the mass transport deposit and, in some cases, they form mushroom diapiric-like structures (Figs. 5c,7c,10c) likely due to forced expulsion of overpressured material linked to a liquefaction process, analog to what

the Gulf of Cagliari (Fig. 3). The phenomenon is particularly concentrated in the intra- and lower slope regions, between the Sarrabus shelf and Banghittu High. Fluid upwelling affects the MTD-CG1, highlighted by widespread pockmarks (Figs. 5, 6, 7, 10). These fluid escapes occur through vertical or oblique conduits into the mass transport deposit and, in some cases, they form mushroom diapiric-like structures (Figs. 5c,7c,10c) likely due to forced expulsion of overpressured material linked to a liquefaction process, analog to what analyzed by Ogata et al. (2012) in south-central Pyrenees region. According to Allen (1982), liquefaction occurs when fine sediments produced by the erosion process are fluidized by excess pore pressure triggered by mass transport events. We hypothesize that in the Gulf of Cagliari the erosion by mass transport processes removed sedimentary material mixed with fluids, contributing to the liquefaction, facilitating fluid migration and leading to the formation of fluid escape structures (Figs. 5, 7, 9, 10). In areas affected by mass transport erosion, the bsz acts as an irregular surface capping the underlying undeformed substrate. These observations are supported by studies in other regions, such as in the Gulf of Mexico, where Hampton et al. (1996) linked pockmarks to gas hydrate destabilization and sediment liquefaction during mass transport events. Similarly, in the North Sea, Hovland et al. (1993) found that fluid migration, driven by overpressure in sedimentary columns, resulted in pockmark formation. In other regions, similar associations have been documented as Lastras et al. (2004) highlighted in the western Medi- terranean, where fluid expulsion occurred due to sediment destabilization. 5.2. (2004) highlighted in the western Medi- terranean, where fluid expulsion occurred due to sediment destabilization. 5.2. The buried isolated carbonate platform The seismic profiles have allowed us to identify an unaffected “un- disturbed sector” which is located above a positive structure (Figs. 5, 6, 14). We ascribe this structure to a carbonate buildup following the criteria discussed by Bubb and Hatlelid (1977), Veeken (2013), and Słonka and Krzywiec (2019), based on seismic evidence: (i) mound- shaped reflection configurations; (ii) lateral changes in seismic facies between the accumulations and the surrounding layers; and (iii) draping effects of overlying layers on these accumulations (Figs. 5, 6). This interpretation is further supported by regional studies on carbonate production (Lecca et al., 1998; Lecca et al., 2005) demonstrating car- bonate activity on the Cagliari Shelf since the Middle to Upper Pleisto- cene. Additionally, modern carbonate productivity remains significant in the Capo Carbonara Canyon area, where coral colonies have been identified at depths of 80 to 115 m (Orrù and Panizza, 2009; Cau et al., 2015) (Fig. 14d). Furthermore, Hovland (1990) correlated the presence of carbonate reefs to the migration of methane fluids, as also recognized by Del Ben et al. (2011) in the central Mediterranean Sea. We hypothesize that this small structural high would have offered greater strength to the movement of the upper layer, slowing its advance. Conversely, it produced an accentuated lateral gravitational flow and compression along the flanks of the rigid structure, resulting in some little thrusts (Figs. 5, 6). In this setting, the undisturbed sector above the structural high likely represents in situ preserved sediments rather than a true component of the MTD. The MTD-CG1 appears

Conversely, it produced an accentuated lateral gravitational flow and compression along the flanks of the rigid structure, resulting in some little thrusts (Figs. 5, 6). In this setting, the undisturbed sector above the structural high likely represents in situ preserved sediments rather than a true component of the MTD. The MTD-CG1 appears to have flowed around this obstacle, from the slope towards the basin, moving on the path of least resistance and leaving this sector largely undeformed. 5.3. Instabilities of the area The Gulf of Cagliari hosts multiple mass transport deposits (MTDs), landslides, debris flows and retrogressive erosion processes along the continental slope. In addition, multibeam data and seismic surveys from the MaGIC project have identified large-scale gravitational instability at the summit of Sant’Elia-Foxy Canyon (Deiana et al., 2017). The Banghittu Landslide (BLs), on the eastern flank of the Banghittu High, causes significant seafloor deformation, disrupting the buried sedimentary layers and producing an irregular seabed morphology (Figs. 3, 11), indicating ongoing landslide activity. Basal interaction of the MTD-CG1 Based on the interaction of the bsz with the underlying substrate, and using the terminology proposed by Sobiesiak et al. (2018), we have interpreted “free-slip flows” in the translational domain, where the mass movement can travel over the underlying substrate with minimal interaction, leaving no significant trace of its passage on the shear sur- face, absence of large-scale erosion or deformation and only minor mobilization between the flow and the underlying deposits (Sobiesiak et al., 2016; Sobiesiak et al., 2018; Kumar and Sain, 2020). The key drivers behind free-slip flows include: (i) basal erosion in the headwall domain, where substrate ma- terial is detached and incorporated into the flow (Alves, 2015; Dunlap et al., 2010; Festa et al., 2016; Macdonald et al., 1993); (ii) internal deformation of the substrate, leading to plastic deformation, as observed by Sobiesiak et al. (2018); (iii) a combination of these causes. Seismic data reveal the presence of fluids throughout the sedimentary sequence, which can form a lubricating layer at the base of the MTD, reducing shear strength and facilitating movement (Mohrig et al., 1999). In contrast, in the toe domain, basal interactions are characterized by “no-slip flows”, leading to considerable erosion of the underlying sub- strate (Fig. 3) which extends deep beneath the displaced mass (Figs. 8, 9, 10). The no-slip condition suggests that the bsz is not sufficiently lubricated, resulting in deep erosion of material beneath the MTD. The interaction in the toe domain of the MTD-CG1 results in signif- icant substrate erosion and incorporation of displaced material into the flow. The erosion and embedding of the underlying layers are indicative of a no-slip flow regime, where the MTD movement exerts considerable forces on the substrate, preventing the mass flow from sliding freely over the surface. These interactions result

no-slip condition suggests that the bsz is not sufficiently lubricated, resulting in deep erosion of material beneath the MTD. The interaction in the toe domain of the MTD-CG1 results in signif- icant substrate erosion and incorporation of displaced material into the flow. The erosion and embedding of the underlying layers are indicative of a no-slip flow regime, where the MTD movement exerts considerable forces on the substrate, preventing the mass flow from sliding freely over the surface. These interactions result in deepening of the bsz over time, as the flow becomes more turbulent and erosive (Cattaneo et al., 2020) (Fig. 4). Probably, the presence of fluids and overpressure in the sub- strate, reducing the shear strength at the bsz, facilitated this process. This spatial variability results from a combination of several factors during mass flow, including water incorporation, increases in material velocity, changes in pore fluid pressure, frictional stresses, viscosity, interactions with underlying lithologies or degrees of lithification, bathymetric obstacles, and flow channelization, among others (Mohrig et al., 1998; Elverhøi et al., 2000; Posamentier and Kolla, 2003; Moscardelli et al., 2006; Alves, 2010). In the toe domain the thrust faults originate from the bsz (Figs. 8, 9, 10) (Lewis, 1971) indicating a compressional regime further evidenced by the local dip attribute (Fig. 8c). Compression is predominantly active near the Banghittu High and at the foot of the Sarrabus shelf (Fig. 10). Potential factors controlling instability Based on the available data, the instability that led to the formation of MTD-CG1 is driven by a combination of predisposing factors such as slope gradient and presence of fluids, and plausible triggers, such as sea level changes and earthquakes. i) Slope Gradient. The bathymetric slope map (Fig. 3a) highlights significant variations along the margin. In the upper slope, where several submarine landslide are present (mapped by the MAGIC project, Fig. 2), the gradients range between 5◦ and 10◦, which appears to favor gravitational instability (Fig. 3a). In the middle slope, characterized by translational domain, the gradients range from 1.5◦ to 3◦, while in the lower slope, dominated by compressional deformation, the values range from 0.5◦ to 2◦ (Fig. 3a). Although steep slopes are typically linked to more possible failure (Canals et al., 2004), large landslides can also occur on more moderate gradients, even up to 2–5◦. This is favoured under conditions of increased pore pressure or seismic activity (Sultan et al., 2004; Kvalstad et al., 2005; Hornbach et al., 2007; Masson et al., 2006; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013). We suppose that the potential feeding area of the MTD-CG1 was at the boundary of the continental shelf and upper slope, close to the current shelf break (Fig. 3b). ii) Presence of Fluids. Fluid migration in the Upper Quaternary sedimentary layers is evidenced by pockmarks and vertical conduits, significantly contributing to the destabilization of the MTD-CG1. Furthermore, the chaotic

activity (Sultan et al., 2004; Kvalstad et al., 2005; Hornbach et al., 2007; Masson et al., 2006; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013). We suppose that the potential feeding area of the MTD-CG1 was at the boundary of the continental shelf and upper slope, close to the current shelf break (Fig. 3b). ii) Presence of Fluids. Fluid migration in the Upper Quaternary sedimentary layers is evidenced by pockmarks and vertical conduits, significantly contributing to the destabilization of the MTD-CG1. Furthermore, the chaotic architecture within the produced MTD likely enhances fluid migration. The migration of overpressured fluids through these layers is a direct trigger for slope instability. In particular, the presence of fluid escape structures such as pockmarks is indicative of fluid migration. These fluids interact with sedimentary materials by increasing pore pressure which, in turn, induces frictional resistance between sediment particles and a reduction in shear strength, thus promoting the initiation of landslides (Hovland et al., 1993; Hampton et al., 1996; Lastras et al., 2004). This phenomenon has been already recognized in the Mediterranean submarine landslides, where fluid presence often correlates with landslides (Hovland et al., 2002; D’Angelo et al., 2020). The fine-grained sediments present in the slope environment of the Gulf of Cagliari (Lecca et al., 1998) are more sus- ceptible to fluidization and failure, due to the weakening effect of fluids, particularly under overpressure conditions (Ogata et al., 2012). This process contribute to the significant weakening of the sediment sequence (Grozic, 2003; Liu et al., 2022). iii) Eustatic Sea-Level Changes. During the Quaternary ice age and the subsequent Holocene sea-level rise after the Last Glacial Maximum (Lambeck and Chappell, 2001), sea level fluctuations likely played a significant role in triggering the destabilization of the MTD-CG1 in the Gulf of Cagliari, particularly due to their impact on sediment compac- tion and

fluids, particularly under overpressure conditions (Ogata et al., 2012). This process contribute to the significant weakening of the sediment sequence (Grozic, 2003; Liu et al., 2022). iii) Eustatic Sea-Level Changes. During the Quaternary ice age and the subsequent Holocene sea-level rise after the Last Glacial Maximum (Lambeck and Chappell, 2001), sea level fluctuations likely played a significant role in triggering the destabilization of the MTD-CG1 in the Gulf of Cagliari, particularly due to their impact on sediment compac- tion and pore pressure in the Upper Quaternary layers. In particular, the rising sea levels, by increasing the hydrostatic pressure in the sediments, can reduce effective stress and promote destabilization, particularly in areas with pre-existing weak structures (Gargani, 2024). Also the fall in sea level causes changes in sediment compaction and triggers the reac- tivation of pre-existing weaknesses, leading to failure (Sultan et al., 2004; Masson et al., 2006; Hermanrud et al., 2013; Vanneste et al., 2014). In the potential feeding area of the MTD-CG1 (Fig. 3b), these fluctuations, coupled with enhanced fluid migration, further mobilized the sediment and triggered mass transport processes. iv) Seismic Activity. Seismic events represent a significant external trigger for mass transport deposits because earthquakes can induce liquefaction, increasing pore pressure and reducing the cohesion of fine- grained sediments, making them more prone to movement (Lee and Edwards, 1986; Sultan et al., 2004). Recent seismic events in southern Sardinia, such as those documented by Meletti et al. (2020), highlight the seismic activity of the region. For instance, the 13 November 1948 earthquake (Mw 5.1) and the seismic activity recorded in 2000 and 2006, in southern Sardinia, could have contributed to sediment desta- bilization. No specific earthquake has been directly linked to the MTD- CG1, anyway the seismic events occurred in the gulf testify potential triggers for mass transport

Conclusions The seismic dataset with unprecedented resolution in the study area provided essential insights into the geomorphological evolution of the seabed during the Upper Quaternary. This dataset allowed for the identification of buried gravity glidings (MTD-CG1÷4), with MTD-CG1 representing the youngest event in the region. The MTD-CG1, which is primarily composed of a sedimentary unit deposited during the Middle-Upper Pleistocene, was significantly deformed by gravity gliding during the Holocene time and subsequently affected by the last incision of the canyon system. This youngest MTD extends approximately 26 km in a NW-SE di- rection along the continental slope, with a clear movement towards the basin. The deposit exhibits a complex internal structure, with flow variations: from a “free-slip” regime in the translational domain, weakly deformed and crossed by fluid migration and pockmarks, to the “no-slip” flow in the toe domain where a compressional regime prevails. The basal shear zone provides insights into flow transformation of mass flows from “non-erosional”

the canyon system. This youngest MTD extends approximately 26 km in a NW-SE di- rection along the continental slope, with a clear movement towards the basin. The deposit exhibits a complex internal structure, with flow variations: from a “free-slip” regime in the translational domain, weakly deformed and crossed by fluid migration and pockmarks, to the “no-slip” flow in the toe domain where a compressional regime prevails. The basal shear zone provides insights into flow transformation of mass flows from “non-erosional” regime in the translational domain to “erosional” regime in the compressional domain. A buried carbonate buildup pro- vided greater strength to the sliding, determining an undisturbed sector composed by in situ preserved sediments bordered by little thrusts of the translational domain. Beyond MTD-CG1, on the northeast flank of the Banghittu High, seismic profiles evidence that the Banghittu Landslide (BLs) interacts with older deposits of the MTD-CG2 and both appear to be active. These findings point to a long-term process of gravitational sliding, with different MTDs and BLs shaping the geological landscape over time. Geomorphological features observed in the study area, such as ver- tical conduits and pockmarks, provide clear evidence of fluid migration within the sediments. This fluid presence, in combination with the steep slope gradient, is highly conducive to gravitational sliding. Role of eustatic sea level changes and seismic activity in triggering mass transport events remain uncertain. Although this, the overall geological context of the area, characterized by Plio-Quaternary extensional tec- tonics and rare earthquakes, suggest their potential contribution to slope destabilization. Further research is needed to refine our understanding of the timing, triggers, and mechanisms behind these mass transport events. activity in triggering mass transport events remain uncertain. Although this, the overall geological context of the area, characterized by Plio-Quaternary extensional tec- tonics and rare earthquakes, suggest their potential contribution to slope destabilization. Further research is needed to refine our understanding of the timing, triggers, and mechanisms behind these mass transport events. Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Marine and Petroleum Geology journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/marpetgeo Research paper Mass transport deposit (MTD) relief as a control on post-MTD sedimentation: Insights from the Taranaki Basin, oﬀshore New Zealand Jeﬀerson Nwoko∗, Ian Kane, Mads Huuse Basin Studies, School of Natural Sciences, University of Manchester, UK A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Mass transport deposits (MTDs) Turbidite Stratigraphic trap Seismic facies A B S T R A C T Mass transport deposits (MTDs) can aﬀect the routing of turbidite systems and their resultant sedimentary ar- chitecture. With the aid of a high-quality 3D-seismic reﬂection dataset a buried Pleistocene MTD has been identiﬁed in the deep-water Taranaki Basin. Three MTD seismic facies have been identiﬁed and fall within three morphological domains – extensional, translational and contractional areas. Thickness relationships between the MTD and the overburden deposits were investigated quantitatively and matched using TWT-thickness maps. The results reveal that in proximal and distal areas with signiﬁcant MTD relief, occupied by megaclasts and folded facies respectively, post-MTD accommodation is dominated by small ‘micro-basins’ with tortuous sediment pathways. In contrast the translational domain, containing homogeneous MTD facies, hosts larger low-relief basins. These characteristics can be used to deﬁne two main types of depositional topography: Type I, High-relief depositional topography, is observed prominently in the headwall and toe regions of MTDs, resulting in loosely- connected and isolated ponds of sediment; Type II, Low-relief depositional topography, is observed in the trans- lational domain where the eﬀects of sediment loading are clearly observed. Turbidite sandstone distribution aﬀected by Type I topography, is characterised by irregular pinch-outs which present a signiﬁcant risk for stratigraphic trapping whereas Type II topography may form overlying sedimentary bodies which are more extensive. Turbidite deposits in both settings are prone to syn-depositional remobilisation on

Turbidite sandstone distribution aﬀected by Type I topography, is characterised by irregular pinch-outs which present a signiﬁcant risk for stratigraphic trapping whereas Type II topography may form overlying sedimentary bodies which are more extensive. Turbidite deposits in both settings are prone to syn-depositional remobilisation on top of the MTD, which may introduce considerable heterogeneity, particularly at reservoir margins. 1. Introduction Mass Transport Deposits (MTDs) are sedimentary bodies that are translated downslope from topographic highs and slopes to areas of lower gradient as a result of gravity–induced mass failure (Hampton et al., 1996; Moscardelli and Wood, 2008; Kneller et al., 2016; Nwoko et al., 2020). They commonly contain sediments remobilised from re- latively shallower water depth which have been redeposited on the slopes and ﬂoor of deep water basins (Posamentier and Walker, 2006; Kneller et al., 2016). MTDs may cover large areas (kilometres across) and are therefore very important to exploration and production activ- ities in many hydrocarbon provinces; acting as seals or preconditioning reservoir geometries (Armitage et al., 2009; Omeru, 2014). The oﬀ- shore Taranaki Basin (New Zealand) includes world-class examples of MTDs both in seismic sections from the oﬀshore subsurface and exposed along the coast (Strachan, 2002; King et al., 2011). From their initiation point through to their termination point, MTDs create diﬀerent accommodation proﬁles through a combination of erosion and deposition (Olaﬁranye et al., 2013). By deﬁnition, accommodation is the space created above the sediment surface – the background slope surface, but below the equilibrium proﬁle, i.e.an ideal slope proﬁle where no erosion and/or deposition takes place (Pirmez et al., 2000; Kneller, 2003).

seismic sections from the oﬀshore subsurface and exposed along the coast (Strachan, 2002; King et al., 2011). From their initiation point through to their termination point, MTDs create diﬀerent accommodation proﬁles through a combination of erosion and deposition (Olaﬁranye et al., 2013). By deﬁnition, accommodation is the space created above the sediment surface – the background slope surface, but below the equilibrium proﬁle, i.e.an ideal slope proﬁle where no erosion and/or deposition takes place (Pirmez et al., 2000; Kneller, 2003). MTDs aﬀect seaﬂoor morphology, post-MTD strata deposition and deformation, and can control submarine channel and lobe location (Von Huene et al., 2004; Hühnerbach and Masson, 2004; Hühnerbach et al., 2005). The internal characteristics of MTDs provide insight into how intrinsic structural features such as folding, stratal disaggregation and diﬀerential compaction can create topographic highs and lows which will aﬀect later turbidite deposition (e.g. Kneller et al., 2016). Com- plicated onlap or downlap relationships may develop against these depositional highs depending on their orientation. The inﬂuence that MTD-related bathymetric lows and highs have depends on the scale of the accommodation space with respect to incoming turbidity currents; end-members may be (i) isolated ponded reservoirs, or (ii) tortuously connected reservoirs (Kneller et al., 2016). Exposed outcrop examples of sedimentary units deposited around and on top of MTDs have been recognised and illustrate their interac- tion from bed scale to reservoir scale. However, due to a lack of high resolution 3D seismic-reﬂection data explicitly resolving the eﬀect of MTD surface topography on post-depositional strata, post-MTD sedi- ment connectivity in the subsurface is poorly understood (e.g. Pickering and Corregidor, 2000; Armitage et al., 2009; Jackson and Johnson, 2009). This paper examines how internal architecture and external morphology of MTDs are related and how the latter controls subsequent turbidite deposition. We do this by studying an exceptionally well-im- aged MTD in a 3D seismic volume from oﬀshore Taranaki, New Zealand. We investigate this by: a) Characterising and interpreting the seismic facies associated with the MTD and how diﬀerent MTD domains aﬀected depositional re- lief; b) Studying the internal architecture of the MTD, kinematic indicators and basal structures associated with the deposit in order to establish their impact on topography creation throughout the developmental stage, from the initiation point to their termination; c) Analysing how diﬀerent accommodation styles aﬀect the amount of sediment accumulation and the type of facies developed above. 2. Tectonic and stratigraphic setting The study area covers an area of ca. 1925 km2 of the deep-water Taranaki Basin, which has a total area of ca. 330,000 km2 (Panpichityota et al., 2018) (Fig. 1a and b). Taranaki Basin initially evolved as a marine basin, as part of the new Caledonian Basin (now located north-west of the present day Taranaki Basin), and was created by the interaction of the Australian and Paciﬁc convergent plates (Uruski, 2010). Structurally, Taranaki Basin evolved from a passive to active margin basin through time. Starting in the mid Cretaceous, spreading of the Tasman Sea and the break-up of Eastern Gondwana resulted in localised fault-controlled extensional subsidence leading to the development of rifted sub-basins now considered to be a failed continental rift (Laird, 1981, 1994). The Taranaki rift formed the associated northern west coast rift connected to the southern part of the Tasman Sea ridge and interpreted to be part of the rift transform system (Thrasher, 1990; King and Thrasher, 1992). Basin-wide subsidence and post-rift thermal contraction characterised the basin history from the late Eocene to early Oligocene. Throughout the Neogene, the oﬀshore deep-water part of the Taranaki Basin developed on a gently subsiding passive margin, and experienced a complicated tectonic history with periods of active margin extension, rapid deepening, and compression, occurring si- multaneously in diﬀerent areas of the basin (King and Thrasher, 1992; Giba et al., 2010). The depositional history of the Taranaki Basin is recorded by up to 8 km of sediment deposited during a transgressive – regressive cycle from the Late Cretaceous to Early Miocene

the Neogene, the oﬀshore deep-water part of the Taranaki Basin developed on a gently subsiding passive margin, and experienced a complicated tectonic history with periods of active margin extension, rapid deepening, and compression, occurring si- multaneously in diﬀerent areas of the basin (King and Thrasher, 1992; Giba et al., 2010). The depositional history of the Taranaki Basin is recorded by up to 8 km of sediment deposited during a transgressive – regressive cycle from the Late Cretaceous to Early Miocene (Fig. 1c). The early Oligo- cene marked the cessation of sediment supply, where transgression progressively shifted sedimentation southwards (King and Thrasher, 1996). This depositional hiatus in the southern Taranaki region – known as the “base Oligocene paraconformity”, separates the passive margin phase from the active margin phase (King and Thrasher, 1992); while Miocene sedimentation was inﬂuenced by the evolving Australian - Paciﬁc plate transform boundary (the Alpine fault). The middle Mio- cene saw the end of compression within the northern part of the basin and at the same time, the Mohakatino submarine volcanic arc formation began (King and Thrasher, 1992; 1996). These volcanic mounds con- trolled the distribution of sediments with low amplitude, chaotic re- ﬂections identiﬁed and interpreted as MTDs interbedded with channels and turbidites up until the late Pliocene (King and Thrasher, 1996; Uruski, 2010). These sedimentary units occur below base of prograda- tional clinoforms, identiﬁed as the Giant Foresets Formation (Salazar et al., 2016). During the late Pleistocene, several feeder canyons cut into the shelf, intercepting along-shore and ﬂuvial sediment supply and diverting it to deeper parts of the basin. These canyons feed the nu- merous basin ﬂoor submarine channel systems ubiquitous on the pre- sent day seaﬂoor – Fig. 1b (King and Thrasher, 1992). A chronostratigraphic framework for the basin has been developed using biostratigraphic data

occur below base of prograda- tional clinoforms, identiﬁed as the Giant Foresets Formation (Salazar et al., 2016). During the late Pleistocene, several feeder canyons cut into the shelf, intercepting along-shore and ﬂuvial sediment supply and diverting it to deeper parts of the basin. These canyons feed the nu- merous basin ﬂoor submarine channel systems ubiquitous on the pre- sent day seaﬂoor – Fig. 1b (King and Thrasher, 1992). A chronostratigraphic framework for the basin has been developed using biostratigraphic data from the nearby oﬀshore well (Romney-1, Fig. 1d) which constrains the age of the youngest MTD (the MTD of study) to the Pleistocene (Rad, 2015; Nwoko et al., 2020). This MTD covers an estimated area of 22,397 km2, with a remobilised volume of ~3700 km3 (Omeru et al., 2016) (Fig. 1d). 3. Data and methods The dataset consists of a 3D seismic reﬂection survey covering an area of approximately 1925 km2 in the north western part of the deep- water Taranaki Basin, oﬀshore New Zealand (Fig. 1a and b). The Romney 3D seismic reﬂection data only images parts of the study MTD in the deep-water Taranaki Basin (Fig. 1b). This 3D seismic volume was acquired by a single sleeve Airgun source array and a 6 × 3500 m streamer array, sampled at an interval of 4 ms, and a dominant fre- quency of 41 Hz, resulting in a vertical resolution of about 9 m, with a sediment velocity of 1850 ms−1 (Rusconi, 2017; Nwoko et al., 2020). Bin spacing of the seismic volume is 12.5 m × 25 m. The bin spacing is such that the extent and stratigraphy of MTD is easily mappable. Data processing includes post-stack time migration (PSTM), resampling, automatic gain control (AGC) and zero phase conversion. Well data from the Romney-1 well comprise a full well

fre- quency of 41 Hz, resulting in a vertical resolution of about 9 m, with a sediment velocity of 1850 ms−1 (Rusconi, 2017; Nwoko et al., 2020). Bin spacing of the seismic volume is 12.5 m × 25 m. The bin spacing is such that the extent and stratigraphy of MTD is easily mappable. Data processing includes post-stack time migration (PSTM), resampling, automatic gain control (AGC) and zero phase conversion. Well data from the Romney-1 well comprise a full well log suite for a deeper exploration drilling objective and is available together with checkshot data that constrain the age of the MTD to be well above the top Miocene reﬂector where there are no log data available (Fig. 1c, d and 1e) (Nwoko et al., 2020). Methods for the morphometric characterisation of MTDs described by Clare et al. (2019) are used in this study. Mapping of the Pleistocene MTD required a combination of guided and autotracked seismic inter- pretation, interpreting the data at a constant grid spacing (16 × 16 m), given the chaotic nature of many of the internal reﬂections and the spatially variable nature of the bounding horizons. Negative amplitude reﬂections (denoted by blue troughs) indicate a decrease in acoustic impedance, while positive amplitudes (denoted by red peaks) on seismic reﬂection proﬁles represents an increase in acoustic impedance following the SEG-Normal polarity conventions. Internal seismic facies were deﬁned on selected seismic lines and have been imaged using several seismic attributes and variance slices from the 3D volume to generate amplitude, dip, thickness and RMS maps. These attributes il- luminated the abrupt truncations of the seismic reﬂections and de- formation styles within the MTD. Quantitative analysis using TWT- thickness maps was used to assess the relationship between the MTDs and their associated facies, and the relief around and on

Internal seismic facies were deﬁned on selected seismic lines and have been imaged using several seismic attributes and variance slices from the 3D volume to generate amplitude, dip, thickness and RMS maps. These attributes il- luminated the abrupt truncations of the seismic reﬂections and de- formation styles within the MTD. Quantitative analysis using TWT- thickness maps was used to assess the relationship between the MTDs and their associated facies, and the relief around and on top the MTD (Fig. 2). TWT-thickness of overburden deposits (using the seaﬂoor as a reference horizon) has been generated to trace the development of post- MTD sedimentation inﬂuenced by MTD relief (Fig. 2). 4. Results 4.1. Basal shear surface Description: In the study area, the basal surface of the MTD is generally sub-parallel to the underlying undeformed stratigraphy (Fig. 3). In planform view, there is an extensive east-west trending feature that creates a step on the basal surface (Fig. 3a, b and 3c). This step-like geometry corresponds to a change in dip with a general NW dip – between 9° and 15° (Fig. 3a, b and 3c). The basal surface of this MTD coalesces with infrajacent stratigraphy making it diﬃcult to trace. J. Nwoko, et al. M arine and Petroleum Geology 120 (2020) 104489 2 The MTD appears to have eroded and/or remobilised part of this un- derlying stratigraphy, or buttresses against conﬁning topography (Fig. 3d). At the eastern part of the extensive E-W feature, are two prominent U-shaped depressions (Fig. 3b and e). On both sides of the depressions are N-S localised faults cutting through the underlying stratigraphy, detached at the same stratigraphic level as the U-shaped depressions (Fig. 3e). Three sets of elongate negative-relief linear fea- tures, 2–8 km long and 0.2–0.3 km wide, are identiﬁed on the

eroded and/or remobilised part of this un- derlying stratigraphy, or buttresses against conﬁning topography (Fig. 3d). At the eastern part of the extensive E-W feature, are two prominent U-shaped depressions (Fig. 3b and e). On both sides of the depressions are N-S localised faults cutting through the underlying stratigraphy, detached at the same stratigraphic level as the U-shaped depressions (Fig. 3e). Three sets of elongate negative-relief linear fea- tures, 2–8 km long and 0.2–0.3 km wide, are identiﬁed on the basal surface of the MTD; these sets are labelled as L1, L2 and L3 (Fig. 3b). L1 linear features have a general NE-SW orientation sub-parallel to the E- W extensive feature, L2 and L3 have a SE – NW orientation but L3 are less well-imaged. Interpretation: Ramps step through stratigraphy whilst the ﬂats are parallel to stratigraphy (Bull et al., 2009). Ramps are useful palaeo- transport kinematic indicators and result from a slide stepping up through stratigraphic level(s) resulting in a step-like geometry (Frey- Martínez et al., 2006; Bull et al., 2009). Thus, the extensive E-W trending feature on the MTD basal surface is interpreted to be a basal ramp. The N-S faults that terminate beneath the basal surface de- marcate the blocks which appear to have undergone some degree of shearing towards the west (Omeru, 2014). Moreover, the identiﬁed zones where the basal surfaces are not distinctively apparent are in- terpreted as basal shear zones with evidence of substrate erosion (Alves and Lourenço, 2010), and lineation orientations in this case, are Fig. 1. Several authors suggest that lineations can also be palaeo-transport trend indicators and have interpreted these features to be grooves, formed by the erosional action of coherent blocks grinding into the substrate (Posamentier and Kolla, 2003; Bull et al., 2009; Nwoko et al., 2020). Hence a more eﬀective prediction of the overall MTD translation direction will combine lineation orienta- tions with the orientation of the ramp and the transport direction given by block organisation (Bull et al., 2009; Omeru and Cartwright, 2019). Ramps have also been reported to occur parallel to the direction of transport (Bull et al., 2009). The orientation of the identiﬁed basal ramp is laterally sub-parallel to the lineation orientations, thus we suggest a gross SW translation direction for this MTD. Additionally, since the N-S faults are localised to the eastern parts of the basal ramp only in the vicinity of the depressions, we interpret this basal ramp as a generally non-fault controlled lateral-frontal basal ramp.

The orientation of the identiﬁed basal ramp is laterally sub-parallel to the lineation orientations, thus we suggest a gross SW translation direction for this MTD. Additionally, since the N-S faults are localised to the eastern parts of the basal ramp only in the vicinity of the depressions, we interpret this basal ramp as a generally non-fault controlled lateral-frontal basal ramp. The variability in orientation of L1 and L2 lineations could be attributed to rotation of blocks during translation (Alsop and Marco, 2014; Omeru and Cartwright, 2019); while the poorly imaged L3 lineations may be due to diﬀerences in substrate lithiﬁcation (Posamentier and Kolla, 2003; Nwoko et al., 2020). 4.2. MTD internal characteristics The general internal seismic expression of the MTD is chaotic in nature (Fig. 4). Three main seismic facies labelled 1) Megaclast facies (MF), 2) Homogeneous facies (HF), and 3) Folded facies (FF), can be observed from proximal northeast to distal southwest limits of the survey (Figs. 3a and 4a). This MTD has a tripartite morphology with an extensional domain – denoted by planar features, protruding mega- clasts on the seaﬂoor (Nwoko et al., 2020; their Fig. 3e) (Fig. 4b, c and 4d), a translational domain (Fig. 4a, e and 4f), and a contractional domain (arcuate scarps, thrusts features, frontal conﬁnement – Fig. 3b, d, 4a and 4f). The seismic facies associated with these MTD domains are described based on their seismic reﬂection attributes, amplitude re- ﬂection strength, internal conﬁguration, and continuity of the reﬂec- tions. MF description: This facies is well imaged and covers an area of approximately 413.82 km2. From northeast to southwest, a dominantly moderate to high amplitude reﬂection pattern that typically delineates angular protruding megaclasts

and 4f), and a contractional domain (arcuate scarps, thrusts features, frontal conﬁnement – Fig. 3b, d, 4a and 4f). The seismic facies associated with these MTD domains are described based on their seismic reﬂection attributes, amplitude re- ﬂection strength, internal conﬁguration, and continuity of the reﬂec- tions. MF description: This facies is well imaged and covers an area of approximately 413.82 km2. From northeast to southwest, a dominantly moderate to high amplitude reﬂection pattern that typically delineates angular protruding megaclasts is observed (Fig. 4b and c). The mega- clasts, measuring about 0.4–1.5 km high and 0.7 km–1 km in width, are internally stratiﬁed and characterised by coherent reﬂections that are separated by extensional planar contacts, encased within a chaotic mix (Fig. 4b and c). Within the same facies, a characteristic set of moder- ately deformed seismic reﬂections with some localised faults abruptly terminates against a series of relatively undeformed parallel reﬂectors (Fig. 4d). These undeformed parallel reﬂectors are mainly composed of symmetrical fore- and back-thrust sediment blocks (Fig. 4d). MF interpretation: This facies is interpreted to represent discrete megaclasts carried within a deformed (debrites) laminar ﬂow (Fig. 4b and c) (Frey-Martinez et al., 2005; Bull et al., 2009; Ortiz-Karpf et al., 2015; Omeru and Cartwright, 2019; Nwoko et al., 2020). Megaclasts within debris ﬂows have often been associated with disintegration of retrogressive failures into more chaotic packages in the downslope di- rection (Normandeau et al., 2019). Based on the conﬁguration, or- ientation, and the way they map out, we imply the megaclasts facies mainly occupy upstream, in the extensional domain, and that the megaclasts stopped while the material behind them was still in motion, building up and buckling behind the megaclasts. Thus, we interpret these fore-and back thrust structures to have formed during downslope translation of the MTD, and megaclast

of retrogressive failures into more chaotic packages in the downslope di- rection (Normandeau et al., 2019). Based on the conﬁguration, or- ientation, and the way they map out, we imply the megaclasts facies mainly occupy upstream, in the extensional domain, and that the megaclasts stopped while the material behind them was still in motion, building up and buckling behind the megaclasts. Thus, we interpret these fore-and back thrust structures to have formed during downslope translation of the MTD, and megaclast of this unit are separated by localised faults (Fig. 4d) signalling late-stage disintegration of the initial deposit (Normandeau et al., 2019). HF description: This region of the MTD covers an area of ca. 197 km2 and is mostly restricted to the MTD translational region (Fig. 4a). Planform and cross-section imaging of this facies shows the conﬁguration to be dominated by homogenously disrupted and amor- phous low-amplitude reﬂection patterns (Fig. 4a, e and 4f). Very low- amplitude seismic reﬂections can be observed towards the southwest (Fig. 4e). HF interpretation: The degree of deformation undergone during emplacement is recorded by the weak amplitude reﬂection strength with no apparent internal organisation (e.g. Doughty-Jones et al., 2019; Omeru and Cartwright, 2019).
