However, the absence of salt at the surface and the 600 m thick Albian to Santonian sediments deposited in the area (Fig. 4) suggests that this structure probably remained buried, never piercing the surface. The depocenter located between the Bakio salt wall and the Armintza fault, where up to 1800 m of Black Flysch Group is deposited may thus be considered as a minibasin, namely the Jata minibasin (Fig. 15). Following the location of the Bakio salt wall and the Armintza fault, the width of the Jata minibasin is about 6000 m, which is consistent with minibasin geometries globally (e.g. Madof et al., 2009; Hearon et al., 2014a, b; Kergaravat et al., 2016; Saura et al., 2016; Martín-Martín et al., 2017; Verg´es et al., 2020). The Cormor´an-1 well located within the Jata minibasin suggests the absence of salt at depth in the minibasin (e.g. G´omez et al., 2002). This may result from the creation of a primary weld during the Cretaceous and/or of welding during the Cenozoic shortening (Roca et al., 2020) (Fig. 15a). East of Bakio, Roca et al. (2020) defined two distinct minibasins, the Burgoa and the Sollube mini-basins separated by an E-W trending sub-salt extensional fault, called the Bermeo fault (Fig. 15a). The pres- ence of such fault is not obvious on the field, since the only evidence is the small Triassic outcrop located in the Bermeo town (Fig. 3). 7.2. The transport directions inferred for all the MTDs in this study support the palaeogeography described above (Fig. 15) and the recent interpretations of Roca et al. (2020). MTDs 2 and 4 are clearly derived from the Bakio salt wall (Fig. 15b), demonstrating that it was growing during the deposition of the Bakio breccias Fm. and the Black Flysch Group, as postulated in Rowan et al. (2012), Ferrer et al. (2014) and Poprawski et al. (2014, 2016). The source area for the MTDs 6 and 7 clearly coincides with the footwall of the Armintza sub-salt extensional fault (Fig. 15b). MTD5 may be derived from the Bakio salt wall or from the footwall of the Armintza sub-salt extensional fault (Fig. 15b). However, the presence of several rounded quartz gravel-to pebble sized clasts within the matrix of the MTD5 (Fig. 10c and d) is more consistent with a source located on the footwall of the Armintza sub-salt extensional fault, that directly received reworked sediment from the Landes Massif. (2020). The NNE- or ENE-directed slope north of Bermeo may be explained either by rotation on the northern flanks of the Bermeo diapir or by a slight northward tilting of the footwall of the Bermeo sub-salt extensional fault. The palaeogeographic reconstruction of the study area and the measured palaeocurrent directions demonstrate that regionally-derived turbidity currents were flowing axially through the minibasins and were laterally confined between salt structures, similarly to other minibasins (e.g. Gee and Gawthorpe, 2006; Mayall et al., 2010; Back et al., 2011; Oluboyo et al., 2014), while MTDs were transported from the salt structures, in an oblique or orthogonal direction compared with the turbidity flows (Fig. 15b). This interpretation is validated by the local palaeoflow south of the Matxitxako Cape, which shows SSW-directed turbidity flows (Fig. 15b). These SSW-directed flows may thus be considered as driven along the axis of the Burgoa minibasin (Cumber- patch et al., 2021). By contrast, local turbidity flows near Gaztelugatxe together with the MTDs 2 and 4 rather represent a lateral source roughly orthogonal with the axial palaeoflow (Fig. 15b). This differs from the run-out distance that corresponds to the transport dis- tance for each particle within a MTD (Moscardelli and

the Armintza fault and the Jata minibasin, similar to that described by Prather et al. (2012) in the Gulf of Mexico. 7.3. MTDs length estimations The MTD length is defined as the horizontal distance in the down- slope direction following the main axis of a MTD body to its terminal end, excluding the evacuation area (Moscardelli and Wood, 2016). This differs from the run-out distance that corresponds to the transport dis- tance for each particle within a MTD (Moscardelli and Wood, 2016). Run-out distances within the same MTD may have variable values, as clasts may undergo different amounts of displacements for example because of flow transformation, or differential settling. The run-out distances can be qualitatively approached in the field by comparing the reworked facies within MTDs with the in situ facies from the hosting succession. We used the palaeogeographic map (Fig. 15b) to infer rough length estimations, taking into account the distance between the potential source area and the current location of each outcrop where MTDs are exposed. MTDs usually have their headwall dominated by extensive structures and their toe by compressive structures (e.g. Frey-Martínez et al., 2006; Alsop et al., 2016; Cardona et al., 2020). The outcrops in this study are dominated by compressive structures and thus they may represent any part of the compressive toe of larger bodies. In addition, the location of the source area and evacuation area of for each MTD is not accurate. Consequently, the estimated values represent a minimal length from the source to any part of the compressive toe, rather than the real MTD length. The current distance between the outcrop of MTD1 and the Bermeo diapir/subsalt extensional fault, that was possibly the source for this MTD suggests a minimum length of about 800 m (Fig. 15b). Since MTD2 pinches out onto the

Since MTD2 pinches out onto the Gaztelugatxe escarpment that bounds the carbon- ate platform located above the Bakio salt wall (Fig. 3) (Poprawski et al., 2014, 2016), a minimum length of 100 m is assumed (Fig. 15b). The current distance between the outcrop of MTD3 and the Bermeo dia- pir/subsalt extensional fault, that was possibly the source for this MTD, suggests a minimum length of about 1600 m (Fig. 15b). The current distance between the outcrop of MTD4 and the carbonate platform located offshore along the strike of the Bakio salt wall (Fig. 15b) gives an estimation of the minimum length of 2000 m. MTD5 is devoid of car- bonate clasts typically derived from the Bakio diapir and thus it prob- ably comes from the footwall of the Armintza fault (Fig. 15b). The minimum length assuming the Bakio salt wall is the source, is 1500 m and assuming the footwall of the Armintza fault is the source is up to 2000 m. The current distance between the outcrops of MTDs 6 and 7 and their possible source suggests a minimum length of 2000 m and of 1000 m, respectively (Fig. 15b). 7.4. Clasts nature and matrix proportion in MTDs In this section, the relationships between the MTD minimum length, the clast nature and the matrix proportion are discussed for the MTDs of the Burgoa and Jata minibasins. MTD2 contains both limestone debris in the basal debrite and in the matrix, and slump folds composed of turbidite sandstones in its upper part. Individual fossils among the carbonate debris suggests this MTD recorded the last mass-failure from the active platform, since it is covered by the first pure siliciclastic interval of unit 2 (Cumberpatch et al., 2021). The slumped turbidite sandstones have relatively similar facies compared to the overlying interval of unit 2, interpreted as deposited in a proximal lobe fringe environment (Cumberpatch et al., 2021). This suggests slope failure from the last remaining carbonate platform patches above the Bakio diapir followed by incorporation of turbidite sandstones during the down-slope transport across a proximal lobe fringe environment. Since MTD2 is probably deposited while the carbonate platform was still active, it is considered here as a carbonate-dominated MTD, although it could be also classified as a mixed MTD. The carbonate-dominated MTDs (MTDs 1, 2) from the Burgoa min- ibasin display carbonate clasts, reworked from a carbonate platform (Fig. 6a and b and 7a), and individual fossils that are similar to those hosting redeposited carbonates, suggesting a relatively short run-out distance. The carbonate-dominated MTDs (MTDs 1, 2) from the Burgoa min- ibasin display carbonate clasts, reworked from a carbonate platform (Fig. 6a and b and 7a), and individual fossils that are similar to those hosting redeposited carbonates, suggesting a relatively short run-out distance. MTD2 is inferred to have a minimum length of only 100 m (Fig. 15b) and exhibits a relatively low proportion of matrix (35%), consistent with the proximity to the carbonate platform growing above the Bakio salt wall. However, abundant individual carbonate clasts mixed in the matrix are below the resolution of the method use to es- timate the proportion of matrix and thus this proportion may be slightly overestimated. MTD1 has an estimated minimum length of about 800 m (Fig. 15b) and a high percentage of matrix (75%), coherent with a more distal location with respect to the source area. These results are consistent with the downslope clast distribution within MTDs, that may reflect the initial redeposited carbonate facies distributed along the slope, which are slightly disturbed by downslope migrating deforma- tion, similarly to recent work (Le Goff et al., 2019). This suggests an initial sedimentary transport by rock falls, gravel and grain flows (Poprawski et al., 2016) from the carbonate platforms toward the slope and subsequent sliding of redeposited carbonates during the MTDs emplacement (Poprawski et al., 2016). These MTDs are thus interpreted to initiate in clast-rich redeposited facies near the platform and, following the downslope migration and deformation of muddier facies, is predicted in the more distal areas. The siliciclastic-dominated MTDs (MTDs 3, 4, 5, 6, 7) from the Jata and Burgoa minibasins are devoid of shallow-water clasts and commonly comprised of reworked turbidites (Figs. 8–12). These MTDs are thus interpreted to initiate in clast-rich redeposited facies near the platform and, following the downslope migration and deformation of muddier facies, is predicted in the more distal areas. The siliciclastic-dominated MTDs (MTDs 3, 4, 5, 6, 7) from the Jata and Burgoa minibasins are devoid of shallow-water clasts and commonly comprised of reworked turbidites (Figs. 8–12). MTDs 4 and 7 display relatively homogeneous reworked facies and a relatively low proportion of matrix, (59% and 47%; Figs. 9 and 12), respectively. In MTD4, reworked facies are slightly finer and muddier than the under- lying deposits and the folded beds show a partial lateral continuity, despite the presence of sheath folds. MTD7 shows relatively similar facies compared to its underlying deposits. In deep-water siliciclastic dominated minibasins, coarse-grained turbidites are usually confined between the adjacent diapirs and thus restricted to the axial part of the minibasins, while fine-grained turbidites and mud drapes the diapir roofs (e.g. Madof et al., 2009; Mayall et al., 2010; Cumberpatch et al., 2021). This suggests that MTDs 4 and 7 initiated on the flanks of the adjacent salt structures in muddy facies and then interacted with local coarser facies in proximal fringe – lobe-off axis environments, since MTD are able to incorporate pieces of the underlying substrate (e.g. Ogata et al., 2014; Sobiesiak et al., 2016, 2018; Cardona et al., 2020). MTD7 may have incorporated more pieces of substrate or possibly underwent a more limited run-out transport compared to MTD4, as it displays rela- tively similar facies compared to its underlying deposits. In MTD7, the presence of a basal sand-rich debrite west of Bakio (Fig. 13d) suggests Y. Poprawski et al. Marine and Petroleum Geology 132 (2021) 105194 21 down-slope flow transformation may occur. This suggests that the toe of such MTDs may be dominated by mud-rich or clast-rich debrites. MTD3, 5 and 6 show more heterogeneous reworked facies including fine and medium grained turbidites, siderite layers and nodules and quartz pebbles (only in MTD5) (Figs. 10 and 11) and a relatively high proportion of matrix, 81%, 77% and 64% respectively. These values may be slightly overestimated as centimeter scale clasts are abundant (Fig. 10c and d and 11c, d), but under the image resolution. These MTDs are characterized by rootless folds, completely disconnected beds and by refolded structures suggesting an intense layer normal shear. The pres- ence of siderite usually found in basin plain environments (e.g. ´Abalos and Elorza, 2012) together with quartz pebbles usually found in channel-lobe transition zone deposits (e.g. Cumberpatch et al., 2021) suggest reworking of different environments during the downslope transport of MTD5. In addition, these MTDs display relatively different facies compared to their underlying deposits suggesting a relatively significant run-out distance, consistent with the reworked heteroge- neous facies, the rootless folds and their associated debritic facies. Compared with MTDs 4 and 7 (59% and 47% of matrix respectively), MTDs 3, 5 and 6 have also a relatively short length (≤2000 m) but a higher proportion of matrix (81%, 77% and 64% respectively). Assuming slope failure in muddy facies similar to those of MTDs 4 and 7 and relatively similar length, MTDs 3, 5 and 6 were possibly transported over muddier substrates compared with MTDs 4 and 7 and thus were not able to incorporate coarse facies in by the same proportion, due to the lack of coarser facies. Alternatively, flow evolution from turbulent to laminar, may have favored a down-slope transport of muddier facies (e. g. Cardona et al., 2020). 8. Discussion This section compares the MTDs of the Jata and Burgoa minibasins with other MTDs from salt dominated basins and margins, worldwide. It also addresses the triggering processes for the local MTDs in the study area. 8.1. Sources for MTDs and comparison with subsurface analogs MTDs can be classified according to their source areas and triggering mechanisms. Following the classification of Moscardelli and Wood (2008, 2016), regionally-derived MTDs or attached MTDs can be shelf-attached or slope-attached and usually reach thousands of square kilometers in area and hundreds of meters in thickness. They may be triggered by

salt dominated basins and margins, worldwide. It also addresses the triggering processes for the local MTDs in the study area. 8.1. Sources for MTDs and comparison with subsurface analogs MTDs can be classified according to their source areas and triggering mechanisms. Following the classification of Moscardelli and Wood (2008, 2016), regionally-derived MTDs or attached MTDs can be shelf-attached or slope-attached and usually reach thousands of square kilometers in area and hundreds of meters in thickness. They may be triggered by sea level variations, regional tectonics, or high sedimen- tation rates (Moscardelli and Wood, 2016). Locally-derived MTDs or detached MTDs correspond to localized units, only reaching tens of square kilometers in area and triggered by local processes. Regionally-derived, also called extra-basinal, MTDs found in salt controlled basins commonly consist of debrites associated with rafted blocks and are usually devoid of preserved slumps because of their long run-out distance (Gamboa et al., 2011; Jackson, 2012; Omosanya and Alves, 2013; Ward et al., 2018; Doughty-Jones et al., 2019). The length of locally-derived MTDs from salt-controlled basins ranges from 1000 to 10,000 m (Madof et al., 2009; Arfai et al., 2016; Gamboa and Alves, 2016; Doughty-Jones et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020). They commonly show more preserved structures including slide deposits and folded strata, normal faults in their headwall and imbricate thrusts in their toe (Madof et al., 2009; Gamboa and Alves, 2016; Doughty-Jones et al., 2019), consistent with their relative small length and limited run-out distance. Tripsanas et al. (2004) described slumps near the diapirs that evolve laterally to muddier debris flows near the center of minibasins. In the Jata and Burgoa minibasins, the studied MTDs are clearly sourced from the local, adjacent structures, including the Bakio salt wall and the footwalls of the Armintza and Bermeo sub-salt extensional faults (Fig. 15b).

their toe (Madof et al., 2009; Gamboa and Alves, 2016; Doughty-Jones et al., 2019), consistent with their relative small length and limited run-out distance. Tripsanas et al. (2004) described slumps near the diapirs that evolve laterally to muddier debris flows near the center of minibasins. In the Jata and Burgoa minibasins, the studied MTDs are clearly sourced from the local, adjacent structures, including the Bakio salt wall and the footwalls of the Armintza and Bermeo sub-salt extensional faults (Fig. 15b). The minimum length estimations for the MTDs from the Jata and Burgoa minibasins are in line with the locally sourced MTDs in the aforementioned literature from other salt-controlled basins, since they range from 100 to 2000 m long. The reworked facies within these MTDs are also consistent with local sources, as they are relatively similar to their host rocks. In addition, MTDs 1, 2, 3, 4 and 7 are dominated by slides and slumps and contain relatively well-preserved strata (Figs. 6–9 and 12). Only MTDs 5 and 6 are muddier with disconnected rootless folds (Figs. 10 and 11). All this evidence suggests that MTDs in the Jata and Burgoa minibasins are locally-derived MTDs, similar to those described in Madof et al. (2009), Arfai et al. (2016), Gamboa and Alves (2016), Doughty-Jones et al. (2019) and Wu et al. (2020). MTDs 5 and 6 may correspond to the distal part of similar MTDs, similarly to the study of Tripsanas et al. (2004) and Cardona et al. (2020). However, MTD5 may also have a more regional signature because of the higher facies heterogeneity among its clasts and reworked beds, which include quartz gravels and pebbles (Fig. 10b, c, d). The presence of these pebbles and gravels suggests the initial source area as the Landes Massif. This sug- gests an initial phase of

5 and 6 may correspond to the distal part of similar MTDs, similarly to the study of Tripsanas et al. (2004) and Cardona et al. (2020). However, MTD5 may also have a more regional signature because of the higher facies heterogeneity among its clasts and reworked beds, which include quartz gravels and pebbles (Fig. 10b, c, d). The presence of these pebbles and gravels suggests the initial source area as the Landes Massif. This sug- gests an initial phase of sediment transfer from the Landes Massif to the footwall of the Armintza fault, by gravity-driven process and then local slope failure reworking the pebble-rich facies, following a ‘fill-and-spill’ model (e.g. Prather et al., 2012; Soutter et al., 2019). Alternatively, MTD5 may be sourced directly from the Landes Massif and thus it may be considered as a regionally-derived or slope-attached MTD, following the classification of Moscardelli and Wood (2016). similar with those described in Jackson (2012), Ward et al. (2018) and Doughty-Jones et al. (2019). This is supported by the debritic character of MTD5. However, the small size of MTD5 suggests that it more likely to represent a locally derived MTD. 8.2. Seal and reservoir potential in MTDs Few subsurface studies focus on the facies of locally-derived MTDs in salt-controlled basins, since such facies are often below the scale of seismic data and thus facies studies concentrate on cored data, which is usually of commercial interest and therefore not publicly available for academic studies. Since fine-grained turbidites and mud usually drape the diapir roofs, locally-derived MTDs are assumed to be mud-rich (Madof et al., 2009). However, in the North Sea Davison et al. (2000) reported locally-derived Palaeocene MTDs including clasts of Creta- ceous chalk mixed inside a muddy matrix, derived from the adjacent diapirs. In addition, Wu et al. (2019) described

data and thus facies studies concentrate on cored data, which is usually of commercial interest and therefore not publicly available for academic studies. Since fine-grained turbidites and mud usually drape the diapir roofs, locally-derived MTDs are assumed to be mud-rich (Madof et al., 2009). However, in the North Sea Davison et al. (2000) reported locally-derived Palaeocene MTDs including clasts of Creta- ceous chalk mixed inside a muddy matrix, derived from the adjacent diapirs. In addition, Wu et al. (2019) described locally-derived MTDs containing from 40 to 60% sandstone. This shows that the nature of facies within MTDs in salt controlled basins can be widely variable be- tween different minibasins. This field-based study brings some new ideas regarding the possible facies reworked within locally-derived MTDs. When the apex of the di- apirs remain in deep-water environments, they are assumed to receive mainly mud and find-grained turbidites (Madof et al., 2009; Mayall et al., 2010; Cumberpatch et al., 2021). Consequently, slope failure creating MTDs are assumed to occur by diapir flank steepening in muddy facies (Fig. 16 a, b). However, during their downslope trajectories MTDs may interact with other allocyclically-derived facies deposited at the sea floor. If these MTDs are transported above a muddy substrate, they may remain relatively muddy and thus they may have relatively good sealing properties in a hydrocarbon system, near and away from the diapirs as postulated by Madof et al. (2009) (Fig. 16a). Such MTDs are exemplified by MTD 5 and 6 from the Jata minibasin (with up to 81% and 77% of matrix, respectively at 2000 m from the source). If the trajectory of a locally-derived muddy MTD includes substrates with coarser facies, it may incorporate portions of the substrate (e.g. Ogata et al., 2014; Sobiesiak et al., 2016, 2018; Cardona et al., 2020) Y. MTDs 1 and 2 show specific clasts (here shallow-water carbonates) may be exported to deep-water environments, when the facies devel- oped on the diapir roofs strongly differs from the facies in the adjacent minibasins (Fig. 15c and d). This may be the case when carbonate platforms are growing on the crest of diapirs in the photic zone, while the adjacent depocenters mainly received deep-water facies, like in Bakio (Poprawski et al., 2016) (Fig. 16c). The comparison between MTD1 (100 m from the source and 35% of matrix) and 2 (800 m from the source and 75% of matrix) suggests that the proportion of matrix may increase away from the diapirs and the associated carbonate platforms for these MTDs, reflecting the lack of ability of mass failures to transport large clasts long distances. Consequently, such MTDs may have good reservoir properties near diapir, while their distal toe may be muddier with good sealing properties (Fig. 16c). All these elements suggest that reservoir and sealing properties could be roughly predicted for MTDs in subsurface studies by characterizing the nature of the diapir roof and the nature of the facies at the seafloor found along the MTDs trajectories. For siliciclastic-dominated MTDs, the seal risk may be reduced by drilling near the diapir, as postulated by Madof et al. (2009), since coarse-grained porous facies are restricted to the axial part of the minibasins.

For siliciclastic-dominated MTDs, the seal risk may be reduced by drilling near the diapir, as postulated by Madof et al. (2009), since coarse-grained porous facies are restricted to the axial part of the minibasins. By contrast, the seal risk increases near the diapir for carbonate-dominated MTDs, since redeposited carbonates with porous facies are redeposited near the diapir, if the carbonate platform grows above the topographic high. Regardless of the type of MTD, the geometries of the internal structures must be studied since internal thrusts for example may form paths for fluids (e.g. Wu et al., 2019; Cardona et al., 2020). 8.3. Triggering processes for MTDs in the Jata and Burgoa minibasins In salt-controlled basins, the primary process triggering locally- derived MTDs is halokinesis (Davison et al., 2000; Madof et al., 2009; Back et al., 2011; Gamboa et al., 2011; Omosanya and Alves, 2013; Arfai et al., 2016; Gamboa and Alves, 2016; Doughty-Jones et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020). Following the halokinetic sequence model (Giles and Lawton, 2002; Giles and Rowan, 2012), MTDs are inferred to occur during relatively low sediment accumulation rates, allowing the diapir to reach the surface, inducing rotation of its flanks and gravitational destabilization of its roof (Fig. 16a). In the Jata and Burgoa minibasins, the studied MTDs are locally- derived and thus halokinesis may is the primary trigger for these MTDs, since they were sourced from the Bakio salt wall and the foot- walls of the Armintza and Bermeo sub-salt faults (Fig. 15b). However, the studied MTDs are concentrated

inferred to occur during relatively low sediment accumulation rates, allowing the diapir to reach the surface, inducing rotation of its flanks and gravitational destabilization of its roof (Fig. 16a). In the Jata and Burgoa minibasins, the studied MTDs are locally- derived and thus halokinesis may is the primary trigger for these MTDs, since they were sourced from the Bakio salt wall and the foot- walls of the Armintza and Bermeo sub-salt faults (Fig. 15b). However, the studied MTDs are concentrated near the Middle to Late Albian boundary. This boundary is characterized by a regional unconformity or hiatus present at the margins of the Basque-Cantabrian basin (e.g. García-Mond´ejar et al., 1996; Agirrezabala and L´opez-Horgue, 2017), recently interpreted as the result of the transition from crustal thinning to mantle exhumation (Rowan, 2014). This suggests that the studied MTDs were probably triggered by a combination of salt growth responding to more regional tectonics and to low sedimentation rates. Roca et al. (2020) assumed that the Armintza and Bermeo sub-salt faults and the basal salt detachment became inactive and that the Bakio diapir growth was mainly governed by differential loading during Late Albian. Less regional tectonic activity and limited salt growth may thus explain the decrease of the amount of MTDs in the upper part of the Black Flysch Group (Fig. 4). MTDs 1 and 2 (Figs. 6 and 7) show abundant carbonate clasts including individual fossils that indicate an active carbonate production top of the Bakio salt wall and possibly top of the Bermeo diapir/sub-salt fault. Aggradation of carbonate platform in relative sea level rise settings (Poprawski et al., 2016) may also favored the building of steep clino- forms and thus slope destabilization. Consequently, MTDs 1 and 2 were possibly triggered by a combination of salt growth and aggradation of the platform that

and 2 (Figs. 6 and 7) show abundant carbonate clasts including individual fossils that indicate an active carbonate production top of the Bakio salt wall and possibly top of the Bermeo diapir/sub-salt fault. Aggradation of carbonate platform in relative sea level rise settings (Poprawski et al., 2016) may also favored the building of steep clino- forms and thus slope destabilization. Consequently, MTDs 1 and 2 were possibly triggered by a combination of salt growth and aggradation of the platform that also favored slope destabilization. The footwall of the Armintza fault considered as the source for MTDs 5, 6 and 7 probably remained buried since it was covered by an up to 600 m thick package of Albian to Santonian sediments (Fig. 15a), sug- gesting elevated sedimentation rates in the area. This suggests that slope failure associated with the emplacement of MTDs 5, 6 and 7 may also be triggered by high sedimentation rates generating an increase of fluid pore pressures and thus slope instability. Table 1 9. Conclusions The Burgoa and Jata minibasins adjacent to the Bakio salt wall provide a new outcrop-based example of MTDs related to both growing diapirs and regional tectonics. This study highlights key elements con- cerning: (i) the understanding of the local palaeogeography controlling the transport direction of MTDs in the study area (ii), the distinction of three different types of locally-derived MTDs in minibasins and (iii) the reservoir and sealing potential of MTDs in deep-water minibasins. These new elements are detailed below: (1) The comparison between different structural methodologies assuming layer parallel (MAM, MAPS, MFOM) and normal shear (DAM, AIM, GFPM) used in this study is a useful approach for the reconstruction of transport direction in MTDs at the outcrop. The inferred transport directions for MTDs can be compared to turbidity current directions that

distinction of three different types of locally-derived MTDs in minibasins and (iii) the reservoir and sealing potential of MTDs in deep-water minibasins. These new elements are detailed below: (1) The comparison between different structural methodologies assuming layer parallel (MAM, MAPS, MFOM) and normal shear (DAM, AIM, GFPM) used in this study is a useful approach for the reconstruction of transport direction in MTDs at the outcrop. The inferred transport directions for MTDs can be compared to turbidity current directions that provide additional information about slope direction. Facies comparison within MTDs and in their hosting succession allows approaching qualitatively the run- out distance for local outcrops. Finally, assumptions about the source areas and MTD length can be made when all these infor- mation are summarized together with palaeogeographic infor- mation in a comprehensive way, (2) Using slumping sense reconstruction methods, this study dem- onstrates that the MTDs 5, 6 and 7 are derived from the footwall of the Armintza sub-salt extensional fault, MTDs 2 and 4 are derived from the Bakio salt wall and MTD1 is derived from the Bermeo diapir/sub-salt extensional fault. This supports the palaeogeography recently defined by Roca et al. (2020), with two minibasins adjacent of the Bakio salt wall: the Burgoa and Jata minibasins. MTDs in this study are derived from local growing salt structures and thus, they are interpreted as locally-derived MTDs, triggered by salt growth associated with regional tec- tonics, although other triggers such as slope steepening associ- ated with carbonate platform aggradation and salt withdrawal cannot be excluded. (3) Three types of diapir-associated MTDs are defined using seven outcrop examples from the Jata and Burgoa minibasins and comparison to subsurface examples. They differ by their source and clast type. In deep-water environments, MTDs are assumed to initiate as mud dominated bodies since the diapir

they are interpreted as locally-derived MTDs, triggered by salt growth associated with regional tec- tonics, although other triggers such as slope steepening associ- ated with carbonate platform aggradation and salt withdrawal cannot be excluded. (3) Three types of diapir-associated MTDs are defined using seven outcrop examples from the Jata and Burgoa minibasins and comparison to subsurface examples. They differ by their source and clast type. In deep-water environments, MTDs are assumed to initiate as mud dominated bodies since the diapir apex mainly received fine-grained facies. During their downslope trajectories, MTDs may interact only with muddy facies and thus remain relatively muddy. These MTDs are assumed to be dominated by mud from their headwall to their toe. By contrast, after their initiation in muddy facies at the diapir crest, MTDs may interact with coarser facies during their downslope trajectories and thus they may incorporate sands. In this case, their headwalls are Y. Poprawski et al. Marine and Petroleum Geology 132 (2021) 105194 24 mud-dominated, their translational domains may become rela- tively sand-rich, while their toes may be dominated by mud-rich or clast-rich debrites related to dissagregation during flow evo- lution. When the apex of diapir is in the photic zone, carbonate platforms may develop and thus diapir growth may induce the genesis of carbonate-dominated MTDs. These MTDs highly differ from the siliciclastic counterparts since their headwall are dominated by carbonate platform-derived clasts and their toe by mud. Bermeo Late Albian Robles et al. (1988) 9 Calcareous Flysch - Rotalipora appenninica - Rotalipora cushmani Not mentioned Middle Cenomanian to Santonian Feuill´ee (1967) Y. Poprawski et al. Marine and Petroleum Geology 132 (2021) 105194 25 (4) This study suggests that reservoir and sealing properties could be roughly predicted for MTDs in subsurface studies by character- izing the nature of the diapir roof and the nature of the facies at the seafloor found along the MTDs trajectories. MTDs initiated from diapirs in deep-water environments tend to have relatively good sealing properties from the headwall to their toe, if there are no interactions with coarser facies at the sea floor. If coarse facies are present on the seafloor, sands incorporated by MTDs may form paths for fluids (“thief sands”) and thus may decrease the sealing potential. MTDs initiated from diapirs with their apex in the photic zone associated with syn-kinematic carbonate plat- form growth tend to have high risk seals near diapirs, due to the high proportion of clasts and reworked beds with a good porosity. A review of approaches for submarine landslide-tsunami hazard identification and assessment Jean H.M. Roger a,*, Suzanne Bull a, Sally J. Watson b,c, Christof Mueller a, Jess I.T. Hillman a, Andrea Wolter a, Geoffroy Lamarche d, William Power a, Emily Lane b, Susi Woelz b, Sam Davidson b a GNS Science, 1 Fairway Drive, Lower Hutt, New Zealand b National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research (NIWA), Wellington, New Zealand c Institute of Marine Science, The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand d School of Environment, The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Tsunami Submarine landslides Geoscience New Zealand Aotearoa Numerical simulations Hazard assessment PTHA Scenario databases Susceptibility mapping Data science A B S T R A C T Submarine landslides can generate destructive tsunamis. Yet their recurrence intervals and tsunamigenic mechanisms are poorly understood, hampering quantification of global exposure and risk. With growing coastal populations and climatic changes, the impacts of tsunami hazards will increase, and will disproportionately affect peoples in underdeveloped countries (e.g., Small Island Developing States). Specific hazard characteristics of submarine landslide-tsunami include the potential for: short tsunami travel times, highly directional waves, locally extreme wave amplitudes and lack of forewarning. Probabilistic tsunami hazard assessments (PTHA) have emerged as the gold standard for tsunami hazard quantification, enabling science understanding to be translated into policy and decision making. However, our knowledge gaps and research challenges largely prohibit PTHA for submarine landslide sources. In this review, we reference Aotearoa New Zealand, as a western Pacific Island nation vulnerable to tsunami hazards, to illustrate the fragmentary nature of the evidence for, and perceived threat of submarine landslide-tsunami typical of many

and lack of forewarning. Probabilistic tsunami hazard assessments (PTHA) have emerged as the gold standard for tsunami hazard quantification, enabling science understanding to be translated into policy and decision making. However, our knowledge gaps and research challenges largely prohibit PTHA for submarine landslide sources. In this review, we reference Aotearoa New Zealand, as a western Pacific Island nation vulnerable to tsunami hazards, to illustrate the fragmentary nature of the evidence for, and perceived threat of submarine landslide-tsunami typical of many regions globally. We present an overview of geoscience approaches for the identification and assessment of submarine landslide-tsunami hazards, which include con- struction of submarine landslide databases, source-specific numerical tsunami simulations, successful approaches to submarine landslide-specific PTHA, submarine landslide susceptibility mapping and emerging data science techniques. We recommend a sequence of logical and accessible steps for enhanced submarine landslide iden- tification and hazard assessment aimed at geoscience disciplines. Our recommendations promote strategic ap- proaches to future data collection and enhanced multi-disciplinary collaboration between marine geoscientists, and tsunami, hydrodynamic, hazard and risk specialists. 1. Introduction Society is often underprepared for destructive tsunami events, despite decades of disaster risk reduction activities. Tsunami risk will increase along with growing population concentrations in coastal re- gions, particularly within the low-elevation coastal zone (i.e., altitude <10 m). It was estimated that 625 million people (about 10.9 % of the World’s population) lived below 10 m elevation in 2000 (Neumann et al., 2015), a number that is forecast to reach as many as 949 million people by 2030, with most of those people living in underdeveloped countries. Historically, the focus of tsunami research has been on earthquake sources as they cause ~80 % of known tsunamis globally (Harbitz et al., 2014) (Fig. 1). Recent events including the 1883 eruption of Anak Krakatau volcano in Indonesia (e.g., Nomanbhoy

% of the World’s population) lived below 10 m elevation in 2000 (Neumann et al., 2015), a number that is forecast to reach as many as 949 million people by 2030, with most of those people living in underdeveloped countries. Historically, the focus of tsunami research has been on earthquake sources as they cause ~80 % of known tsunamis globally (Harbitz et al., 2014) (Fig. 1). Recent events including the 1883 eruption of Anak Krakatau volcano in Indonesia (e.g., Nomanbhoy and Satake, 1995; Maeno and Imamura, 2011), the complex event at Palu, Indonesia in 2018 (e.g., Carvajal et al., 2019; Gusman et al., 2019), and recently, the 2022 Hunga Tonga-Hunga Ha’apai eruption in Tonga (e.g., Gusman et al., 2022) highlight the need to better understand diverse sources of tsunami (Grezio et al., 2017; Behrens et al., 2021). Landslides that result in tsunami have been known to damage coastal and marine infrastructure including submarine communication cables (e.g., Piper et al., 1999; Assier-Rzadkieaicz et al., 2000; Carter et al., 2014; Harbitz et al., 2014; Tappin et al., 2014). In the following, all the en masse movement of material down a slope under gravity is considered to be a landslide. This includes all types of material fall as presented by Hungr et al. (2014) like rockfall, debris flow, debris avalanche. Tsunamigenic landslides can occur in differing settings, including entirely underwater, partially submerged, or initiating terrestrially and impacting a water body such as the sea, a fjord, or a lake (e.g., Harbitz et al., 2006; Løvholt et al., 2015; Takabatake et al., 2020). The highest tsunami run-up ever recorded is attributed to a landslide that initiated on land and impacted an embayment (534 m in Lituya Bay, Alaska 1958; Miller, 1960). These are of particular concern due to the potential for large volume events (i.e., >100’s–1000’s km3) to generate regional tsunami (e.g., Masson et al., 2006; Bondevik et al., 2005) and the po- tential for occurring without warning. Submarine landslides occurring on continental slopes can represent “local” tsunami sources (defined in Aotearoa New Zealand as having less than 1 h propagation time between the source region and the New Zealand coast; Power, 2013) which allow less time to predict hazard impacts and make emergency management decisions compared to more distant sources. warning. Submarine landslides occurring on continental slopes can represent “local” tsunami sources (defined in Aotearoa New Zealand as having less than 1 h propagation time between the source region and the New Zealand coast; Power, 2013) which allow less time to predict hazard impacts and make emergency management decisions compared to more distant sources. Hazard characteristics of submarine landslide tsunami may include: (1) Rapid arrival time or short “tsunami travel time” (TTT: time between wave generation and arrival at coastline) due to high potential for submarine landslides to originate on continental slopes close to coast- lines; (2) Highly directional tsunami waves, which can lead to focussed “corridors” of waves impacting sections of coastline (Ward, 2001; Walters et al., 2006); (3) Large wave amplitudes that can locally exceed those from co-seismic tsunamis as submarine landslides may displace the water column vertically many times more in comparison to fault ruptures (Okal and Synolakis, 2003). This effect can lead to locally extreme runup heights at nearby coasts (e.g., Tappin et al., 2008); (4) Lack of forewarning. While key modern examples of submarine landslide-tsunami have involved submarine landslides triggered by earthquakes (e.g., Tappin et al., 2008; Sepúlveda et al., 2020), there are few ways to realise that a tsunamigenic submarine landslide has been generated in real-time, leading to unexpected wave arrival at coastlines. 1.1. Marine and Petroleum Geology 162 (2024) 106729 3 Probabilistic tsunami hazard and risk assessments (PTHA and PTRA, respectively) offer structured frameworks and procedures that allow key elements and uncertainties to be weighted and traced (e.g., Power, 2013). Whereas deterministic methods consider the impact of single scenarios or specific parameter values, probabilistic (or stochastic) models consider a range of possible scenarios, their likelihood and associated impacts. Because of this, PTHA are becoming the standard basis for tsunami hazard assessment globally (Grezio et al., 2017; Beh- rens et al., 2021). The infrequency of tsunami events remains intrinsi- cally challenging, however, and even more so for rare submarine landslide sources. Significant knowledge gaps and research challenges around submarine landslide sources mean that they are rarely included in regional or national tsunami hazard and risk assessments. Given that scientific information is the basis of informed decision making and public awareness, this article aims to: (1) Bring

standard basis for tsunami hazard assessment globally (Grezio et al., 2017; Beh- rens et al., 2021). The infrequency of tsunami events remains intrinsi- cally challenging, however, and even more so for rare submarine landslide sources. Significant knowledge gaps and research challenges around submarine landslide sources mean that they are rarely included in regional or national tsunami hazard and risk assessments. Given that scientific information is the basis of informed decision making and public awareness, this article aims to: (1) Bring greater awareness to the danger posed by submarine landslide–tsunami using Aotearoa New Zealand in the southwestern Pacific as a case study; (2) Encourage further research aimed at better understanding exposure to submarine landslide-tsunami globally; (3) Provide accessible strategies as next steps for improving submarine landslides identification and risk assessment; and (4) Promote collaborations between science disciplines (i.e., marine geoscientists, sedimentologists, physicists, tsunami scien- tists, data scientists, social scientists, hazard and risk scientists) neces- sary to achieve risk reduction. Tsunami generation by submarine landslides Tsunami may be generated by submarine landslides through energy transfer, as the displacement of material at the seafloor results in displacement of the overlying water column. There is a complex coupling between landslide volume, motion, material properties and water surface deformation (Fig. 3; e.g., Fritz, 2002; Fritz et al., 2003; Harbitz et al., 2006; Løvholt et al., 2015). In comparison to seismic ruptures, submarine landslides typically produce relatively short wavelength, highly dispersive waves primarily because of radial spreading (i.e., a small submarine landslide can be viewed as a point source in comparison to a linear fault rupture; Watts et al., 2003; Grilli and Watts, 2005; Enet and Grilli, 2007; Tappin et al., 2008). Submarine landslide tsunami can be locally higher amplitude than earthquake tsunami as the energy available for the tsunami is proportional to the square of the displacement of the seabed (Okal and Synolakis, 2003). A submarine landslide moves less material globally than a fault rupture; however, it can locally displace the seafloor many times more vertically than a fault displacement, resulting in a comparable amount of tsunami energy (Okal and Synolakis, 2003). The motion of the frontal mass of the submarine landslide generates a positive amplitude wave propagating in the direction of submarine landslide

energy available for the tsunami is proportional to the square of the displacement of the seabed (Okal and Synolakis, 2003). A submarine landslide moves less material globally than a fault rupture; however, it can locally displace the seafloor many times more vertically than a fault displacement, resulting in a comparable amount of tsunami energy (Okal and Synolakis, 2003). The motion of the frontal mass of the submarine landslide generates a positive amplitude wave propagating in the direction of submarine landslide motion (Fig. 3; Sue et al., 2011). The main component of im- pulse wave generation occurs as the water surface is drawn down above the site of submarine landslide initiation (negative amplitude wave), which “rebounds” and creates a large positive amplitude wave, followed by smaller trailing waves, propagating radially towards, and away from the source area (Fig. 3A; Enet and Grilli, 2007; Sue et al., 2011; Tadepalli and Synolakis, 1994; Yavari-Ramshe and Ataie-Ashtiani, 2016). Submarine landslide initial volume and initial acceleration exert first order control over wave magnitude (Ward, 2001; Lee, 2009; Kirby et al., 2016; Løvholt et al., 2015). Both the initial acceleration and maximum velocity achieved by a submarine landslide are governed by material density, submergence depth, and slope angle (Haugen et al., 2005; Grilli and Watts, 2005). Rotational “slump”-type landslides are consequently thought to be more effective wave generators (e.g., Matsumoto et al., 2003; Tappin et al., 2008) versus more shallowly-detaching “slab slide” or “grain flow” type failures. Submergence depth influences the land- slide Froude number (ratio of landslide velocity to impulse wave celerity; see Harbitz et al., 2006). Higher Froude numbers indicate more efficient wave generation (Harbitz et al., 2014), although tsunamis can still be generated for lower Froude numbers (Whittaker et al., 2015). Abrupt deceleration of the landslide is also thought to contribute to larger

wave generators (e.g., Matsumoto et al., 2003; Tappin et al., 2008) versus more shallowly-detaching “slab slide” or “grain flow” type failures. Submergence depth influences the land- slide Froude number (ratio of landslide velocity to impulse wave celerity; see Harbitz et al., 2006). Higher Froude numbers indicate more efficient wave generation (Harbitz et al., 2014), although tsunamis can still be generated for lower Froude numbers (Whittaker et al., 2015). Abrupt deceleration of the landslide is also thought to contribute to larger wave heights (Sue et al., 2011; Løvholt et al., 2015; Power et al., 2016). Previous studies have suggested that submarine landslide ge- ometry may influence wave generation, as for a given volume, thicker landslides lead to higher wave amplitudes (e.g., Ward, 2001; Grilli and Watts, 2005; Haugen et al., 2005; Løvholt et al., 2005; Harbitz et al., 2006; Ataie-Ashtiani and Naja-Jilani, 2008). Flow transformation (i.e., the process by which submarine landslides transform into other flow types such as turbidity or density flows; Nemec, 1990; Martinsen, 1994; Eyles and Eyles, 2000; Strachan, 2008; Joanne et al., 2013) may also be a factor in controlling wave amplitude. Submarine landslides showing a longer runout distance have shown larger maximum wave amplitudes than examples with shorter runout distances (Haugen et al., 2005), but transformation is also linked to energy loss and predominantly occurs as the landslide enters deeper water, thus inducing only minor influence. Modern events have demonstrated the link between submarine landslide and tsunami, including at the northern coastline of Papua New Guinea in 1998. A Mw 7.1 earthquake was followed 10–25 min later by a series of waves reaching a maximum height of 10–15 m along a 40 km coastal stretch, resulting in 2200 deaths (Davies et al., 2003). While the tsunami was initially attributed to the earthquake, subsequent analysis showed that

the landslide enters deeper water, thus inducing only minor influence. Modern events have demonstrated the link between submarine landslide and tsunami, including at the northern coastline of Papua New Guinea in 1998. A Mw 7.1 earthquake was followed 10–25 min later by a series of waves reaching a maximum height of 10–15 m along a 40 km coastal stretch, resulting in 2200 deaths (Davies et al., 2003). While the tsunami was initially attributed to the earthquake, subsequent analysis showed that the observed wave characteristics were more consistent with a submarine landslide source (e.g., Tappin et al., 1999; Heinrich et al., 2001; Synolakis et al., 2002; Lynett et al., 2003; Tappin et al., 2008). Geophysical surveys revealed evidence of multiple submarine landslides nearby, including a recent “slump” type failure with an esti- mated volume of 6.4 km3 in ~1600 m water depth (e.g., Synolakis et al., 2002; Tappin et al., 2008). The 1929 Mw 7.2 earthquake on the southwestern Grand Banks of Newfoundland was followed by waves with a maximum run up height of 13 m at the Newfoundland coast, resulting in 28 deaths and severe damage to property and infrastructure (Murty, 1977; Piper et al., 1988; Evans, 2001). Waves were also recorded as far away as mainland Portugal (Fine et al., 2005; Løvholt et al., 2019). Turbidity flows generated by the event broke a series of sub-sea telecommunication cables and travelled a total distance of 1000 km at speeds of 60–100 km/h over 4–11 h (Heezen and Ewing, 1952; Kuenen, 1952; Evans, 2001). This tsunami has been attributed to submarine landslides (Murty, 1977; Clague, 2001; Fine et al., 2005), with evidence of widespread surficial slope failures, estimated at a total of 150–175 km3 (Piper et al., 1988, 1999) later discovered. A more recent study also identified a 560 km3 “complex

the event broke a series of sub-sea telecommunication cables and travelled a total distance of 1000 km at speeds of 60–100 km/h over 4–11 h (Heezen and Ewing, 1952; Kuenen, 1952; Evans, 2001). This tsunami has been attributed to submarine landslides (Murty, 1977; Clague, 2001; Fine et al., 2005), with evidence of widespread surficial slope failures, estimated at a total of 150–175 km3 (Piper et al., 1988, 1999) later discovered. A more recent study also identified a 560 km3 “complex slump” in the area (Schulten et al., 2019). The most intensely studied prehistoric tsunamigenic submarine landslide is the ~8150 BP Storegga Slide of western Norway (e.g., Haflidason et al., 2004, 2005; Bryn et al., 2005; Kim et al., 2019). The slide mobilized 3200 km3 of material, which has been linked to contemporaneous inundations of up to 20 m run-up heights in the sur- rounding region (Bondevik et al., 2005). Interestingly, this region ex- periences low levels of seismicity due to isostatic glacial adjustment of the Scandinavian Region after the last glacial maximum ice-sheet loss (Shiddiqi et al., 2022). A dynamic trigger (e.g., an earthquake) is still postulated however, with slope failure strongly preconditioned by rapid deposition of sediments associated with glacial and interglacial climatic cycles (Solheim et al., 2005). 1.3. Major challenges for submarine landslide-tsunami hazard assessment Previous works have summarized major knowledge gaps for sub- marine landslides (e.g., Huhn et al., 2019) and for tsunami hazard and risk assessment (e.g., Behrens et al., 2021; Sassa et al., 2023). Behrens et al. (2021) present the results of a panel of 50 experts who identified the following gaps for all submarine landslide sources: (1) Lack of un- derstanding and likelihoods for submarine landslide volumes; (2) Dif- ferences between onshore and submarine landslides; (3) Limited constraints on landslide dynamics and material behaviour;

summarized major knowledge gaps for sub- marine landslides (e.g., Huhn et al., 2019) and for tsunami hazard and risk assessment (e.g., Behrens et al., 2021; Sassa et al., 2023). Behrens et al. (2021) present the results of a panel of 50 experts who identified the following gaps for all submarine landslide sources: (1) Lack of un- derstanding and likelihoods for submarine landslide volumes; (2) Dif- ferences between onshore and submarine landslides; (3) Limited constraints on landslide dynamics and material behaviour; (4) Limited availability of benchmarks; and (5) limited past events to inform hazard models. They concluded that the most important, yet challenging research gap, is the lack of understanding and likelihoods for tsunami- genic landslide volumes and occurrence rates. In the following section, we provide an overview of specific elements as a basis for examining ways to address them in later sections. 1.3.1. Lack of tsunamigenic landslide volumes and occurrence rate Comprehensive PTHA relies on establishment of magnitude fre- quency curves which is exceptionally challenging for submarine land- slides. Documenting past submarine landslides from the modern seafloor relies on the acquisition and availability of high-quality, extensive bathymetric data coverage that, ideally, is supported and/or calibrated by subsurface geophysical data (e.g., sub-bottom profiles, seismic reflection data) and sediment samples. Several regional and national J.H.M. Roger et al. Marine and Petroleum Geology 162 (2024) 106729 5 submarine landslide databases have been compiled in recent decades (see Section 3.1). Such endeavors essentially provide past submarine landslide magnitudes and can reveal spatial relationships to geomorphic domains and potential triggering mechanisms (e.g., Watson et al., 2020; Gamboa et al., 2021). Clare et al. (2019) identified the need for a consistent approach to the documentation of submarine landslide pa- rameters and presented a framework that will help to underpin data integration and enhanced knowledge of submarine

Such endeavors essentially provide past submarine landslide magnitudes and can reveal spatial relationships to geomorphic domains and potential triggering mechanisms (e.g., Watson et al., 2020; Gamboa et al., 2021). Clare et al. (2019) identified the need for a consistent approach to the documentation of submarine landslide pa- rameters and presented a framework that will help to underpin data integration and enhanced knowledge of submarine landslide occurrence globally. A further challenge is related to the natural preservation of submarine landslide headscarps and deposits. Watson et al. (2020) showed that fewer than 1 % of documented submarine landslide head- scarps offshore eastern New Zealand could be associated with a deposit, which inhibits accurate assessment of submarine landslide prevalence. Return rates for large volume submarine landslides (hundreds to thousands of km3) are very low (e.g., every 100 ka on the mid- Norwegian Continental Margin; Solheim et al., 2005). Various pre- conditioning factors may prime slopes for failure, including over- pressure due to rapid sediment deposition (Stigall and Dugan, 2010), sea level change (Piper et al., 2003; Maslin et al., 2004; Lee, 2009; Lee et al., 2010; Leynaud et al., 2009), and weak layers (e.g., Wu et al., 2023). When submarine slopes are prone to failure; an earthquake is a likely trigger mechanism (e.g., Micallef et al., 2012; Mountjoy et al., 2014), however, heavy rainfalls (e.g., Pope et al., 2017; Clare et al., 2018), or anthropogenic origin (human-induced landslides [HIL]; e.g., Jaboyedoff et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020), are also implicated. Often, such factors are weakly tested, and impacted by a lack of known submarine landslide ages and age uncertainties, many of which have uncertainty intervals too large to be directly assigned to a particular sea level

is a likely trigger mechanism (e.g., Micallef et al., 2012; Mountjoy et al., 2014), however, heavy rainfalls (e.g., Pope et al., 2017; Clare et al., 2018), or anthropogenic origin (human-induced landslides [HIL]; e.g., Jaboyedoff et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020), are also implicated. Often, such factors are weakly tested, and impacted by a lack of known submarine landslide ages and age uncertainties, many of which have uncertainty intervals too large to be directly assigned to a particular sea level condition (Urlaub et al., 2015). Uncertainty is mainly introduced due to the location of dating samples above and below the submarine landslide deposit (see Urlaub et al., 2015, for a summary of sampling strategies and dating techniques), and to a lesser extent, the “marine reservoir effect” which causes radiocarbon ages of marine samples to appear several hundred years older than coeval terrestrial samples (Stuiver and Braziunas, 1993). Deeply buried (i.e., older) submarine landslides are generally under-sampled, while some margins or regions are preferen- tially studied relative to others (Urlaub et al., 2015). Also, the level of preservation of the landslide scars and deposits is dependent on the erosion vs. sedimentation processes and could introduce a non-negligible size bias in the individual landslide identification (e.g., Chaytor et al., 2009). Because of these collective biases and un- certainties, along with the very small number of well-dated events globally and particularly for small events, sampled submarine landslide ages appear temporally random (Hunt et al., 2013; Urlaub et al., 2015; Clare et al., 2014; Pope et al., 2015) which prohibits confident corre- lation to specific triggers or precursor events. Earthquake ground shaking is the predominant triggering mecha- nism cited or inferred for submarine landslides and demonstrated by modern examples such as Grand Banks in 1929, Papua New Guinea, 1998 (Section 1.2), and more recently,

small number of well-dated events globally and particularly for small events, sampled submarine landslide ages appear temporally random (Hunt et al., 2013; Urlaub et al., 2015; Clare et al., 2014; Pope et al., 2015) which prohibits confident corre- lation to specific triggers or precursor events. Earthquake ground shaking is the predominant triggering mecha- nism cited or inferred for submarine landslides and demonstrated by modern examples such as Grand Banks in 1929, Papua New Guinea, 1998 (Section 1.2), and more recently, Palu, Indonesia in 2018 (Carvajal et al., 2019). Modelling and statistical studies suggest that some, but not all, Mw 7.0 and above earthquakes can trigger numerous submarine landslides, many of them mobilizing small volume materials (Watts and Borrero, 2001; V¨olker et al., 2011). We also note direct evidence of submarine landslides triggered by storms (Zhang et al., 2016), for example 14 submarine landslides were triggered during Typhoon Mor- akot in Taiwan, 2009 (Lin, 2015). Finally, gas hydrate dissociation can trigger submarine landslides via the reduction of the cohesion between the sediment particles of the storage reservoir (e.g., Zhang et al., 2021). However, there is also growing evidence of submarine landslides initi- ating in the absence of known triggers (Fan et al., 2020; Clare et al., in press). There is, however, evidence of fewer submarine landslides since establishment of the current sea-level highstand at 6 ka, than during the period of sea-level rise since the Last Glacial Maximum (LGM) from ~21 ka (Urlaub et al., 2015). Continental slopes are expected to be relatively stable during high, stable sea levels, as sediment delivery is reduced, sediment stress conditions equilibrate and post-glacial seismicity de- creases. Urlaub et al. (2015) therefore suggests that recent and modern submarine landslides occurrences are more likely to be caused by sea level-independent factors such as weak layers and

sea-level highstand at 6 ka, than during the period of sea-level rise since the Last Glacial Maximum (LGM) from ~21 ka (Urlaub et al., 2015). Continental slopes are expected to be relatively stable during high, stable sea levels, as sediment delivery is reduced, sediment stress conditions equilibrate and post-glacial seismicity de- creases. Urlaub et al. (2015) therefore suggests that recent and modern submarine landslides occurrences are more likely to be caused by sea level-independent factors such as weak layers and tectonic seismicity. 1.3.2. Limited knowledge on landslide dynamics and material behaviour The dynamic processes of submarine landslides are poorly under- stood because we lack direct observations in situ at full scale. For example, we have very little empirical information on initial accelera- tion of submarine landslides, which is a first order control on tsunami- genesis (see Section 1.2). To provide a ‘fix’ for this challenge, Heller et al. (2009) and Heller and Hager (2014) developed an empirical method in Swiss lakes. It is the most broadly applied method to estimate generic displacement wave parameters based on laboratory tests. It provides estimates of displacement wave height, amplitude, period, length, run-up height, average discharge, etc. Maximum runout and mobility have been shown to be influential (De Blasio et al., 2006a; Lee, 2009), yet the mechanisms that govern flow transformation are not well understood. The initial density of the un- stable mass may be important, (Lee et al., 1993) along with hydro- planing, which occurs when a thin, underlying water layer reduces friction between the failed mass and the seafloor as it travels downslope (De Blasio et al., 2006a). The internal shear strain of the unstable mass has control over emplacement as well (Hungr et al., 2005; Di Maio et al., 2013; Stead and Eberhardt, 2013; Yerro et al., 2016; Steventon et al.,

understood. The initial density of the un- stable mass may be important, (Lee et al., 1993) along with hydro- planing, which occurs when a thin, underlying water layer reduces friction between the failed mass and the seafloor as it travels downslope (De Blasio et al., 2006a). The internal shear strain of the unstable mass has control over emplacement as well (Hungr et al., 2005; Di Maio et al., 2013; Stead and Eberhardt, 2013; Yerro et al., 2016; Steventon et al., 2019), and may be distributed throughout the mass profile or concen- trated on discrete shear planes within the mass. Conversely, seafloor erosion and entrainment of material may occur where the landslide exerts a shear stress higher than the shear resistance of the seabed (De Blasio et al., 2006b). Grilli and Watts (2005), Løvholt et al. (2015), and Kirby et al. (2016) all recommend a more detailed investigation into the role of submarine landslide thickness, runout, and density on tsunami- genic potential. Behrens et al. (2021) also point out that fluid resistance forces (i.e., pressure drag, skin friction, and added mass) could be important (e.g., Harbitz, 1992), but have barely been explored. Poor understanding of landslide dynamics is compounded by the simplistic representation of submarine landslides in numerical models (e.g., Fine et al., 2005; Grilli and Watts, 2005; Hill et al., 2014; Løvholt et al., 2005). The simplest way to represent a submarine landslide is to use a block model, which has been successful in reconciling certain as- pects of observed submarine landslides attributed to “slump type” fail- ures, such as the 1998 Papua New Guinea event (Synolakis et al., 2002; Tappin et al., 2008; see Section 1.2). Such simplified approaches cannot accurately replicate or predict real-world submarine landslide tsunami, where complex dynamics and material behaviour occur (e.g., Elverhøi et al.,

2014; Løvholt et al., 2005). The simplest way to represent a submarine landslide is to use a block model, which has been successful in reconciling certain as- pects of observed submarine landslides attributed to “slump type” fail- ures, such as the 1998 Papua New Guinea event (Synolakis et al., 2002; Tappin et al., 2008; see Section 1.2). Such simplified approaches cannot accurately replicate or predict real-world submarine landslide tsunami, where complex dynamics and material behaviour occur (e.g., Elverhøi et al., 2005; Talling et al., 2014). Granular flow (e.g., Ma et al., 2015; Yavari-Ramshe and Ataie-Ashtiani, 2017) and multiphase (granular) flow (e.g., Yu and Lee, 2019; Dai et al., 2021) simulations are now more frequently used to reproduce the change of cohesion within a sliding mass and the interactions between the sliding mass and its environment (e.g., the surrounding water). However, current evidence from physical models and numerical studies relating to the influence of landslide type, behaviour and geometry (typically “rigid blocks” versus thinner, more deformable “granular flows”) on wave parameters is divergent (as for the terrestrial cases), and warrants further investigation (e.g., Zweifel, 2004; Panizzo et al., 2005; Ataie-Ashtiani and Nik-Khah, 2008). 2. Submarine landslides in a young, dynamic, mostly marine continent Aotearoa New Zealand is situated on a dynamic convergent plate boundary between the Australian and Pacific plates in the southwestern Pacific Ocean (Fig. 4). The emergent islands are part of the larger 4.9 million km2 continent of Zealandia, which is 94 % submerged (Mortimer et al., 2017). A few studies have highlighted the presence of submarine J.H.M. However, the majority of them is counted in New Zealand’s terri- torial waters: over 3000 submarine landslide features (headscarps and deposits) have been identified on New Zealand’s continental margins, including some of the largest in the world (e.g., Collot et al., 2001; Lewis et al., 2004; Joanne et al., 2013; Omeru, 2014; Bull et al., 2019; Watson et al., 2020; Bull et al., in prep) as detailed further in Section 2.1. Despite this, Aotearoa New Zealand’s exposure to tsunami hazard associated to submarine landslides is unquantified, except for the Cook Strait, the narrow seaway between North and South Island (Fig. 4; Lane et al., 2016). This is because unknown return rates and an incomplete under- standing of past events prohibits derivation of submarine landslide occurrence probabilities (Power et al., 2013). Hikurangi Margin is dominated by high seismicity and high rates of tectonic deformation up to 47 mm/yr (e.g., Nicol and Wallace, 2007). Continuing south, the subduction margin transitions to continental collision and strike-slip along the Alpine Fault onshore the South Island. The boundary continues offshore to the southwest of the South Island, with a reversed subduction polarity, whereby oceanic crust of the Australian Plate is being subducted obliquely beneath Pacific Plate continental crust at the Puysegur Trench (Gurnis et al., 2019). A domain of low to no tectonic strain, considered to be a passive margin (Litchfield et al., 2014) is located offshore along eastern South Island, from the Chatham Rise southward to the Otago Canyons). In northwestern New Zealand, the margin that borders the Tasman Sea is also considered to be passive, despite modern seismicity originating from extensional offshore faults (Seebeck et al., 2023). To the south, adjacent to the Challenger Plateau, the margin associated with the western South Island is narrow, steep and interrupted by sig- nificant submarine canyons that drain to the deep Tasman Basin (Ste- vens et al., 2021). 2.1. Documented submarine landslides in Aotearoa New Zealand As previously indicated, many footprints of submarine landslides have been identified on the continental margins of Aotearoa New Zea- land. The majority of these landslides are as yet undated and located off the New Zealand east coast (see Fig. 5). Fig. 5. Eastern New Zealand Several examples of submarine landslides from eastern New Zealand have been extensively studied on a site-specific basis, including the Matakaoa Submarine Instability Complex (Fig. 5a; Lamarche et al., 2008) which totals 2000 km3 in volume. The Complex includes the Matakaoa Debris Flow, a 200-km-long mass-transport deposit which resulted from the most recent failure, dated to ca. 38–100 ka ago (Joanne et al., 2013). Joanne (2008) used a numerical model to simulate the potential of smaller failures on the flanks of the Complex to produce tsunami and demonstrated the potential for significant inundation along the coastline of East Cape. The Ruatoria Debris Avalanche (Fig. 5a), offshore of the East Cape, consists of a proximal, up to 200-m-thick, lobe characterized by numerous kilometre-scale megablocks, and a more distal debris flow deposit extending a total distance of 100 km (Collot et al., 2001). Formation of the Ruatoria re-entrant and associated failure deposits occurred as a result of multi-phase collapse of the outer trench-wedge slope in response to seamount subduction over the last

for significant inundation along the coastline of East Cape. The Ruatoria Debris Avalanche (Fig. 5a), offshore of the East Cape, consists of a proximal, up to 200-m-thick, lobe characterized by numerous kilometre-scale megablocks, and a more distal debris flow deposit extending a total distance of 100 km (Collot et al., 2001). Formation of the Ruatoria re-entrant and associated failure deposits occurred as a result of multi-phase collapse of the outer trench-wedge slope in response to seamount subduction over the last 2 Myrs (Collot et al., 2001; Davidson, 2022). The Tuaheni Landslide Complex (Fig. 5a) covers 145 km2 (Mountjoy et al., 2009), and is interpreted as a reactivated, slow-moving failure (Mountjoy et al., 2009). It includes units dated from the Holocene to Late Pliocene (i.e., last 11.7 ka–3.6 Ma; Luo et al., 2020). Geotechnical testing on samples from the base of the complex suggested that a hy- pothetical local Mw 8.0 megathrust earthquake would be required to mobilise the sediments (Carey et al., 2022). Movement is likely to be episodic, ceasing when ground shaking reduces or stops. This supports the “slow-moving” morphological interpretation and the hypothesis that submarine landslides of similar morphology may not evolve into cata- strophic, tsunamigenic events. Watson et al. (2020) provides the first regional compilation of sub- marine landslides based on an extensive, compiled bathymetric dataset that spans both active and passive stretches of continental margin (Section 2; Fig. 5a). More than 2200 submarine landslides were identi- fied, with the prevailing morphologies (99 %) being scars. No correla- tion between estimated landslide volume and a range of preconditioning and triggering factors (including seafloor slope, proximity to faults, gas hydrate stability, and ground shaking) was found. However, the study showed that most submarine landslides occur within canyon systems, with 68 % being within 5 km of canyon thalwegs.

No correla- tion between estimated landslide volume and a range of preconditioning and triggering factors (including seafloor slope, proximity to faults, gas hydrate stability, and ground shaking) was found. However, the study showed that most submarine landslides occur within canyon systems, with 68 % being within 5 km of canyon thalwegs. Higher proportions of submarine landslides were recorded on the active margin (1/52 km2) compared to the passive margin (1/121 km2), which is contrary to global studies outcomes (Camerlenghi et al., 2010; Sawyer and DeVore, 2015). The authors attribute this to the prevalence of submarine land- slides occurring on trench-parallel thrust ridges, which may be more prone to failure due to a range of localised factors including uplift, oversteepening, faulting and fluid seepage. In addition, submarine landslides identified in the passive-margin setting were observed to be more eroded and modified by post-failure processes (Watson et al., 2020). Cook Strait (Fig. 4) is underlain by the multi-branching Cook Strait Canyon system which crosscuts the complex transition zone between subduction and continental collision along the plate boundary (e.g., Barnes et al., 1998). 130 submarine landslides have been identified here (Fig. 4; Micallef et al., 2012; Watson et al., 2020), ranging from 0.1 to 10 km3 (Mountjoy et al., 2009; Micallef et al., 2012). The larger submarine landslides preferentially occur on bedding plane surfaces, with earth- quake ground shaking likely to be the main trigger (Micallef et al., 2012; Mountjoy et al., 2014). Power et al. (2016) modelled submarine land- slides in the upper portions of the canyon (i.e., 50–200 m water depth), and predicted coastal wave amplitudes of up to 5 m along

(2016) modelled submarine land- slides in the upper portions of the canyon (i.e., 50–200 m water depth), and predicted coastal wave amplitudes of up to 5 m along the southern coast of the North Island, close to Wellington, the capital city of New Zealand. It was also shown that sudden deceleration of the submarine landslide due to the confined canyon geometry generated large waves propagating in the opposite direction to submarine landslide motion. 2.1.2. Western New Zealand There is comparatively little bathymetric data available for the western New Zealand margins (Fig. 5a). Knowledge of prehistoric sub- marine landslides is taken from rock outcrop studies (e.g., Strachan, 2002; King et al., 2011; Sharman et al., 2015; Bull et al., 2020) and a large offshore legacy seismic reflection dataset (The New Zealand Exploration Data Pack, 2018; https://www.nzpam.govt.nz/mapsg eoscience/petroleum-datapack/). Regionally, a total of 180 buried submarine landslide deposits have recently been mapped as part of the New Zealand submarine landslides database (Bull et al., in prep.). Most notably, 5 extreme volume submarine landslides (estimates ranging from 377 to 4064 km3), including the largest volume in New Zealand, have been identified in the Deepwater Taranaki Basin (Figs. 4 and 5a, d; Omeru, 2014; Omeru et al., 2016; Bull et al., 2020). The submarine landslides have occurred throughout the Plio-Pleistocene (i.e., last 5 Ma) and are linked to high sediment supply in response to an increased plate boundary convergence rate (Kamp et al., 2004; Hansen and Kamp, 2006). Sediments shed from the ancestral

submarine landslides (estimates ranging from 377 to 4064 km3), including the largest volume in New Zealand, have been identified in the Deepwater Taranaki Basin (Figs. 4 and 5a, d; Omeru, 2014; Omeru et al., 2016; Bull et al., 2020). The submarine landslides have occurred throughout the Plio-Pleistocene (i.e., last 5 Ma) and are linked to high sediment supply in response to an increased plate boundary convergence rate (Kamp et al., 2004; Hansen and Kamp, 2006). Sediments shed from the ancestral Southern Alps were trans- ported north by oceanic current action and built steep, high relief (up to 1500 m) prograding clinoforms at the margin front (Hansen and Kamp, 2002; Anell and Mitkandal, 2017; Salazar et al., 2015), but specific preconditioning factors and triggering mechanisms are poorly under- stood. Mollison et al. (2021) carried out a preliminary modelling study of the tsunamigenic potential of New Zealand’s largest known subma- rine landslide, estimated to have occurred 1.8 Ma (Omeru, 2014) and mapped using geophysical data with a total volume of 3776 km2 (Fig. 6; Mollison et al., 2021). The study used three different volume cases (low, mid and high case) and highlights the potential of large wave amplitudes (up to 54 m) at the northwest New Zealand coastline along with a short TTT of 18 min (Fig. 6; Mollison et al., 2021). Also, it shows that waves propagate across the Tasman Sea in all the tested scenarios, with esti- mated wave heights of a few meters on the Australian Coast, associated to TTT of ~3 h (Fig. 6; Mollison et al., 2021). This highlights the fact that submarine landslide-tsunamis can exhibit a regional, if not ocean-wide impact and are not limited to the location where they are generated. 2.2. This highlights the fact that submarine landslide-tsunamis can exhibit a regional, if not ocean-wide impact and are not limited to the location where they are generated. 2.2. Submarine landslide-tsunamis in Aotearoa New Zealand Despite its complex geodynamic setting and rich evidence of historic submarine landslides, Aotearoa New Zealand does not have a confirmed tsunami resulting from a submarine landslide. In New Zealand, the National Tsunami Hazard Model (NTHM, ; Power et al., 2022) is the key science product which estimates tsunami heights at the shoreline for return periods of up to 2500 years, and underpins evacuation zone design, land-use planning and risk assessments (Power et al., 2013; Power et al., 2022). The NTHM does not yet include submarine landslide sources of tsunami due to: (1) Perceived infrequency of tsunami events; (2) A lack of specificity in tsunami hazard return rates; (3) Knowledge gaps in scenario-specific studies for large potentially hazardous events; and (4) The hazard posed from these tsunami sources on time frames of up to 2500 years is considered secondary to the hazard from earthquake-generated tsunami (Power, 2013). While no distant (i.e., more than 3 h TTT from New Zealand) sub- marine landslide sources are presently identified as potentially threat- ening New Zealand, regional (i.e., 1–3 h TTT from New Zealand) submarine landslide sources including small collapses from Kermadec volcanoes (e.g., Wright et al., 2006) and the Central New Zealand-Wellington area are acknowledged in Power (2013). Coastal submarine landslide sources are also acknowledged, but further scenario-specific studies are required to better understand potential impacts. The Mw 7.8 Kaik¯oura earthquake
