Coastal submarine landslide sources are also acknowledged, but further scenario-specific studies are required to better understand potential impacts. The Mw 7.8 Kaik¯oura earthquake in November 2016 is the only New Zealand example of submarine slope failure confirmed to have occurred in association with an earthquake and tsunami (epicentre shown in Fig. 4). Multiple onshore and offshore faults ruptured, producing coastal uplift of up to 6 m, and triggering ~30,000 terrestrial landslides (Massey J.H.M. Roger et al. Marine and Petroleum Geology 162 (2024) 106729 9 et al., 2020). A tsunami was generated, reaching a maximum run up height of 6.9 m to the south of the earthquake epicentre (Power et al., 2017). In the Kaikoura Canyon (Fig. 4), an estimated 0.14 km3 of ma- terial was removed from the canyon rim in water depths of around 500 m, generating a powerful and complex “canyon flushing” event, involving multiple small submarine landslides and sheet failures and which generated turbidity currents which travelled a total distance of >680 km (Mountjoy et al., 2018). Heidarzadeh and Satake (2017) and Heidarzadeh et al. (2019) investigated the possible contribution of a submarine landslide to the Kaik¯oura tsunami, arguing that the spectral analysis of observed water level time series included dual peaks and that previous modelling could not reproduce the maximum measured runup. Heidarzadeh et al. (2019) modelled a dual source scenario, using a hypothetical 5 km3 submarine landslide occurring 10–20 min after the earthquake, and successfully reproduced near-field tide

of >680 km (Mountjoy et al., 2018). Heidarzadeh and Satake (2017) and Heidarzadeh et al. (2019) investigated the possible contribution of a submarine landslide to the Kaik¯oura tsunami, arguing that the spectral analysis of observed water level time series included dual peaks and that previous modelling could not reproduce the maximum measured runup. Heidarzadeh et al. (2019) modelled a dual source scenario, using a hypothetical 5 km3 submarine landslide occurring 10–20 min after the earthquake, and successfully reproduced near-field tide gauge records. They also reproduce the maximum runup height, but to the north of Kaik¯oura Peninsula, rather than the south where it was observed. A review by Lane et al. (2020) notes that several other studies (Bai et al., 2017; Borrero and Greer, 2013; Gusman et al., 2018) demonstrate that the earthquake generates most of the observed tsunami effects. Although examples of contemporaneous tsunami inundation events exist from co-seismic submarine landslides along Aotearoa New Zea- land’s active margins, records of tsunami generated within lakes pose significant risk to inland communities (Benn, 2023; Wang et al., 2023). For example, on November 30, 2022, a Mw 5.7 earthquake underneath Lake Taup¯o, a volcanic lake located in the middle of the North Island, was followed by a tsunami which inundated multiple areas along the lake shoreline and was recorded by lake-level gauges. Further in- vestigations (including diving and bathymetric surveying) helped to identify a fresh underwater rotational slump off Wharewaka Point, a promontory located on the northeast shore of the lake, as a likely candidate for local tsunami generation. In fact, the location of the un- derwater landslide area was also proximal to the greatest shoreline inundation (~40 m runup distance) (Power et al., in prep). 3. Approaches to submarine landslide-tsunami hazard identification and assessment The conflicting and fragmentary evidence for submarine landslide- tsunami in Aotearoa New Zealand, including inconclusive results of the 2016 Kaik¯oura tsunami source modelling, rich evidence of historic submarine landslides, but no confirmed submarine landslide-tsunami is typical of very infrequent but potentially high impact natural hazard events. As pointed out by Grezio et al. (2017), society has often been caught unprepared by tsunami events, irrespective of their source, and step-changes in disaster risk reduction have often happened in the aftermath of major disasters. Significant submarine landslide-tsunami events such as Papua New Guinea 1998 (Davies et al., 2003) and Palu, Indonesia 2018 (Carvajal et al., 2019) demonstrate the occurrence and impact of submarine landslide-tsunamis so, what can we do to promote enhanced preparedness? Here, we provide an overview of established and emerging scientific techniques and approaches that can be employed to better understand submarine landslide-tsunami potential and hazards, and enhanced preparation for future events. 3.1. Building submarine landslide databases Submarine landslide databases can yield spatial distributions and magnitudes of historic submarine landslides and form the basis for sta- tistical analysis to identify preconditioning factors, triggering mecha- nisms, geomorphic relationships and controls on magnitude-frequency distributions (Clare et al., 2019). A minimum requirement is the compilation of all available bathymetric data for a region or area, fol- lowed by morphometric analysis. Morphometric parameters (e.g., scar dimensions, run out distance, volume, directionality etc.) are especially relevant to tsunami generation modelling. Examples of regional com- pilations include the US Atlantic margin (ten Brink et al., 2009; Chaytor et al., 2009; Twichell et al., 2009), the Mediterranean Sea (Camerlenghi et al., 2010; Migeon et al., 2011; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013; Casalbore et al., 2020; Urgeles et al., 2023), Gulf of Mexico (Pampell-- Manis et al., 2016), Australia (Clarke et al., 2019), New Zealand (Watson et al., 2020), the Spanish margins (Le´on et al., 2020), Iberia (Gamboa et al., 2021) and Cascadia (Lenz and Sawyer, 2021; Hill et al., 2020, 2022). Compilation of submarine landslide databases does not directly solve the problem of establishing return periods, especially considering the multiple bias introduced by the

et al., 2011; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013; Casalbore et al., 2020; Urgeles et al., 2023), Gulf of Mexico (Pampell-- Manis et al., 2016), Australia (Clarke et al., 2019), New Zealand (Watson et al., 2020), the Spanish margins (Le´on et al., 2020), Iberia (Gamboa et al., 2021) and Cascadia (Lenz and Sawyer, 2021; Hill et al., 2020, 2022). Compilation of submarine landslide databases does not directly solve the problem of establishing return periods, especially considering the multiple bias introduced by the individuals’ identification methodology (see Section 1.3.1). However, the information and relationships yielded can identify areas of historically high submarine landslide activity and biases that exist in data collection and analysis. This information in turn can be used to support the case for acquisition of additional data and analysis, including boreholes and subsurface geophysical information to develop age frameworks, tsunami source modelling and PTHA. One example is regarding the Cook Strait (see Section 3.3), whereby initial identification of submarine landslide (Mountjoy et al., 2009) was built upon by further efforts to comprehensively document the number, size and distribution of all events in the area and acquire targeted age in- formation (Micallef et al., 2012). The combined dataset enabled iden- tification of primary controls and likely triggers (Micallef et al., 2012; Mountjoy et al., 2014) and facilitated a numerical modelling study to determine the potential tsunami hazard associated with a large failure from the upper canyon (Power et al., 2016). The culmination of this research effort is the first and only PTHA for submarine landslide sources in Aotearoa New Zealand carried out to date (Lane et al., 2016; Mueller et al., 2016; see Section 3.3). The New Zealand submarine landslide database initiated by Watson et al. (2020) is currently being expanded to include buried landslide deposits identified from below the modern seafloor

The culmination of this research effort is the first and only PTHA for submarine landslide sources in Aotearoa New Zealand carried out to date (Lane et al., 2016; Mueller et al., 2016; see Section 3.3). The New Zealand submarine landslide database initiated by Watson et al. (2020) is currently being expanded to include buried landslide deposits identified from below the modern seafloor using seismic reflection data (Bull et al., in prep.). This component of the database will enable enhanced long-term understanding of submarine landslide occurrence. The data that supports identification of buried deposits is mostly derived from offshore energy resource exploration. With similar datasets available from many continental margins globally (e.g., see examples in Moscardelli and Wood, 2016), this approach could be applied in many other geographic locations to supplement submarine landslide databases based on seafloor bathymetry. 3.2. Submarine landslide-tsunami source simulations Computational numerical modelling of submarine landslide pro- cesses and the tsunami waves they generate is one of the key approaches to understand the hazard potential of submarine landslides, alongside physical laboratory experiments (e.g., Grilli et al., 2002; Enet et al., 2003; Ma et al., 2015). Numerical models can predict wave behavior, arrival times, coastal wave heights and current velocities. Of particular value to our general understanding are high fidelity back-analyses of real-world events, where both the submarine landslide source parame- ters and tsunami characteristics are well constrained. The Papua New Guinea, 1998 event is one of the best modern examples of this (Tappin et al., 2008). Identification and detailed correlation of paleotsunami deposits along with a wealth of marine geophysical data provided excellent constraints on numerical modelling of the prehistoric Storegga Slide and tsunami (Bondevik et al., 2005). An extremely

particular value to our general understanding are high fidelity back-analyses of real-world events, where both the submarine landslide source parame- ters and tsunami characteristics are well constrained. The Papua New Guinea, 1998 event is one of the best modern examples of this (Tappin et al., 2008). Identification and detailed correlation of paleotsunami deposits along with a wealth of marine geophysical data provided excellent constraints on numerical modelling of the prehistoric Storegga Slide and tsunami (Bondevik et al., 2005). An extremely diverse range of numerical and conceptual approaches exists (see Yavari-Ramshe and Ataie-Ashtiani (2016) for an in-depth review) and includes up to four stages including landslide initiation and run out, tsunami generation, tsunami propagation and coastal inundation. Examples of submarine landslide-tsunami modelling studies vary in complexity and have been based on: (1) Shallow Water Equa- tions, (e.g., Harbitz, 1992); (2) Fully nonlinear potential flow (e.g., Grilli and Watts, 2005); (3) Boussinesq equations (Lynett and Liu, 2002); and (4) Navier–Stokes equations (e.g., Heinrich, 1992). The majority of numerical models currently in use for submarine landslide-specific sources have been in continuous development and adapted following initial application to earthquake sources. Examples include: TUNAMI-N2 (Imamura, 1995), MOST (Titov and Gonzalez, 1997), COMCOT (Liu et al., 1998), FUNWAVE (Kirby et al., 1998), Basilisk (formerly Gerris; Popinet, 2003), RiCOM (Walters et al., 2006), NHWAVE (Ma et al., 2012), and HySEA (Macías et al., 2015) numerical models. For more information about the available models, see Kirby et al. (2022) and references therein. For submarine landslide hazard identification and assessment where insufficient data prohibits derivations of submarine landslide magnitude-frequency relationships, a deterministic data-driven approach based on credible real-world scenarios is often the best alter- native (Harbitz et al., 2014). Most submarine landslide source models include bathymetric data as inputs, ideally, with considerations for both a “before” (pre-failure) and

al., 2012), and HySEA (Macías et al., 2015) numerical models. For more information about the available models, see Kirby et al. (2022) and references therein. For submarine landslide hazard identification and assessment where insufficient data prohibits derivations of submarine landslide magnitude-frequency relationships, a deterministic data-driven approach based on credible real-world scenarios is often the best alter- native (Harbitz et al., 2014). Most submarine landslide source models include bathymetric data as inputs, ideally, with considerations for both a “before” (pre-failure) and “after” (post-failure) surface morphology. Additionally, submarine landslide input parameters considered for modelling include volume, thickness, density, frictional and rheological properties, and initial failure velocity. Masson et al. (2006) notes the reliability of modelled outputs are sensitive to the quality of available input geological data. Given limitations in currently available geological datasets across submarine landslides in the marine environment and the significant investment required to collect additional samples, one avenue to increase our understanding of submarine landslide-tsunami hazards and impacts is to increase the number of submarine landslide source simulations undertaken. While current models are typically developed on a site-specific basis for notable events, moving towards more regional-scale simulations of multiple events, ideally a subset of real-world examples from a submarine landslide database should be used. This approach can provide a range of magnitude distributions, a detailed picture of potential tsunami hazards and reveal insights into sensitivity of submarine landslide tsunami to various parameters including water depth and submarine landslide geometry. 3.3. Probabilistic tsunami hazard assessments PTHA has become a standard approach for assessing tsunami hazard (see Grezio et al., 2017 for a thorough review of PTHA) as it helps to account for both natural randomness (aleatoric variability) of hazardous processes, as well as our incomplete understanding (epistemic uncer- tainty; Grezio et al., 2017). PTHA typically estimates the probability of exceeding

hazards and reveal insights into sensitivity of submarine landslide tsunami to various parameters including water depth and submarine landslide geometry. 3.3. Probabilistic tsunami hazard assessments PTHA has become a standard approach for assessing tsunami hazard (see Grezio et al., 2017 for a thorough review of PTHA) as it helps to account for both natural randomness (aleatoric variability) of hazardous processes, as well as our incomplete understanding (epistemic uncer- tainty; Grezio et al., 2017). PTHA typically estimates the probability of exceeding specific levels of tsunami intensities, including coastal wave heights, run-up heights and water current velocities (e.g., Power et al., 2013, 2022) for given coastlines or specific sites within a certain period of time. The results are most often communicated as hazard curves, hazard maps or probability maps (Grezio et al., 2017). These output metrics in turn are used for tsunami risk assessment including J.H.M. Roger et al. Marine and Petroleum Geology 162 (2024) 106729 11 inundation modelling, evacuation zone mapping, and loss models (Grezio et al., 2017; Moratalla et al., in prep). Although viewed as a standard for assessing tsunami hazard, PTHA can take many forms, be expressed via many different outputs, and lacks standardization (Grezio et al., 2017). For submarine landslides, rela- tively few PTHA have been carried out and we highlight some pertinent examples here. Geist and Pasons (2010) estimated the empirical prob- ability of submarine landslide return rates using a Bayes Poisson-Gamma model. Rates were derived using the number of identified submarine landslides within a time interval constrained by dated seismic horizons (for the Santa Barbara Channel, California) and onshore paleoseismicity data (for Port Valdez, Alaska). Mean return times for submarine land- slides in the Santa Barbara Channel were 310 and 680 years (based on two differing estimates) and 730 years for Port Valdez, which was noted

(2010) estimated the empirical prob- ability of submarine landslide return rates using a Bayes Poisson-Gamma model. Rates were derived using the number of identified submarine landslides within a time interval constrained by dated seismic horizons (for the Santa Barbara Channel, California) and onshore paleoseismicity data (for Port Valdez, Alaska). Mean return times for submarine land- slides in the Santa Barbara Channel were 310 and 680 years (based on two differing estimates) and 730 years for Port Valdez, which was noted to relate closely to the 690 years mean return time of megathrust earthquakes. This approach assumed that submarine landslides occur with a known constant mean rate over the fixed time interval and do so independently of the time since the last event. While this approach is not suitable for sites where submarine landslides are clearly linked to climate cycles, it importantly quantifies confidence ranges for the estimates. A regional PTHA for submarine landslides has been completed for the U.S. Atlantic margin, where submarine landslides are the most probable tsunami source (ten Brink et al., 2014). Maretzki et al. (2007) performed a slope stability analysis within a Monte Carlo Simulation (MCS) model to estimate coastal run-up from seismically triggered submarine landslides. 45 representative slope transects along with dis- tributions of seismicity, sediment properties, location, volume and water depth parameters were used to perform a large number of stochastic slope stability analysis. When a slope failure occurred within the model, empirical relationships (e.g., from Watts et al., 2005) were used to calculate initial tsunami height and run-up. Numerous model runs were performed (10,000 runs per year for each transect for 10,000 years re- turn period). Model results were validated against observed submarine landslides. Grilli et al. The only PTHA for New Zealand submarine landslide-tsunami was carried out by Lane et al. (2016) for the Cook Strait (Fig. 7; see Section 2.1.1). An empirical magnitude/frequency relationship was derived from previous studies and included absolute ages for four submarine landslides (Micallef et al., 2012; Mountjoy et al., 2009, 2014). The remaining landslides were bracketed within a 15 ± 5 ka age model based on patterns of erosion and deposition in response to sea level rise. Submarine landslides were then numerically modelled, with a range of volumes (0.1, 0.3 and 1 km3) modelled on several idealized bathymetry transects. For each scenario, the maximum coastal wave elevations were combined with the magnitude-frequency relationship to produce annual probabilities of coastal wave heights. The 1 in 2500 year expected maximum water elevation was found to be similar to earthquake-generated tsunamis for some parts of the study area (i.e., 10 m; Power, 2013). However, it was noted that relatively shorter wave- length submarine landslide-tsunamis are likely to produce lesser inun- dation extents. The authors also emphasized several sources of uncertainty in their analysis including the true distribution of submarine landslide ages and noted the possibility that several submarine land- slides could occur simultaneously in the event of a large magnitude earthquake. 3.4. study area (i.e., 10 m; Power, 2013). However, it was noted that relatively shorter wave- length submarine landslide-tsunamis are likely to produce lesser inun- dation extents. The authors also emphasized several sources of uncertainty in their analysis including the true distribution of submarine landslide ages and noted the possibility that several submarine land- slides could occur simultaneously in the event of a large magnitude earthquake. 3.4. Predicting submarine landslide occurrence (susceptibility mapping) Submarine landslide occurrence can be investigated at the regional, local, or site-specific, scale. Regional scale analysis involves heuristic, deterministic, or most commonly statistical methods, investigating the susceptibility of material to mass movement given triggering and pre- conditioning variables. At the local or site-specific scale, more detailed analyses may be completed, including simulations of landslide initiation and runout, material testing, and physics-based analysis of failure initiation and behaviour. Below, we summarise the methods that have been used to investigate submarine landslide occurrence. We summarise approaches, typically used for onshore (earthquake-induced) landslides, in Fig. 8. At a regional scale, landslide susceptibility maps and models are used to predict landslide occurrence and are commonly used in onshore landslide hazard analysis (e.g., Dymond et al., 2006; Frattini et al., 2010; Guzzetti et al., 2006; Huang and Zhao, 2018; Reichenbach et al., 2018; Smith et al., 2021; Massey et al., 2022a,b). They typically employ GIS, multivariate statistical and/or machine-learning methods (see Section 3.6) over regional scales to determine the likelihood of landsliding, and do not consider physical process, behaviour, or material properties. Regional-scale statistical, heuristic and physics-based methods are a common approach onshore because material sampling, drilling data and remote monitoring methods are more expensive, site-specific ap- proaches that are not widely available and spatially constrained. In the offshore context, submarine landslide susceptibility is a rela- tively new endeavour using GIS tools and

employ GIS, multivariate statistical and/or machine-learning methods (see Section 3.6) over regional scales to determine the likelihood of landsliding, and do not consider physical process, behaviour, or material properties. Regional-scale statistical, heuristic and physics-based methods are a common approach onshore because material sampling, drilling data and remote monitoring methods are more expensive, site-specific ap- proaches that are not widely available and spatially constrained. In the offshore context, submarine landslide susceptibility is a rela- tively new endeavour using GIS tools and requires sufficient coverage of high-resolution bathymetry datasets (to derive slope angle, relief, cur- vature, and local slope relief). Only a few studies have dealt with this topic, for which various approaches to submarine landslide suscepti- bility mapping can be applied. Hitchcock et al. (2010), and Leon and Somoza (2011) used a heuristic (index-based) approach whereby slope variables were ranked and/or weighted and spatially layered to produce qualitative relative failure probability for the Mississippi Delta in the Gulf of Mexico, and the Gulf of Cadiz, Spain, respectively. Similar, but statistically based, contributions by Borrell et al. (2019) and Piedade et al. (2018) quantitively expressed landslide probability for larger re- gions (the Spanish Continental Shelf and the offshore Espírito Santo Basin, SE Brazil, respectively), but highlighted the challenges of data sparsity. At the local scale, deterministic quantitative assessments have investigated slope failure processes (e.g., Lee and Edwards, 1986; Lee et al., 1999; Urgeles et al., 2002; Strasser et al., 2011). Methods include simple static and pseudostatic analysis, permanent displacement anal- ysis (predicting how much permanent displacement is likely to occur in a given slope under, e.g., earthquake forcing), and stress-deformation analysis (a sophisticated simulation-based analysis investigating fail- ure mechanisms, behaviour and evolution) (see Massey et al., 2022 for a summary of earthquake-induced onshore landslide occurrence methods). These approaches require engineering judgement and

processes (e.g., Lee and Edwards, 1986; Lee et al., 1999; Urgeles et al., 2002; Strasser et al., 2011). Methods include simple static and pseudostatic analysis, permanent displacement anal- ysis (predicting how much permanent displacement is likely to occur in a given slope under, e.g., earthquake forcing), and stress-deformation analysis (a sophisticated simulation-based analysis investigating fail- ure mechanisms, behaviour and evolution) (see Massey et al., 2022 for a summary of earthquake-induced onshore landslide occurrence methods). These approaches require engineering judgement and as- sumptions and/or detailed data on material properties, which are commonly not available or difficult to acquire. Lee and Edwards (1986) were one of the first to suggest a method for evaluation of offshore earthquake-induced slope stability for Alaska and California, using a modified version of the pseudostatic infinite slope method. The infinite slope method calculates an averaged factor of safety, simply expressed as the ratio of the resisting shear strength of material on an assumed failure plane to the driving shear stress due to the self-weight of the slope (Morgenstern, 1967). Pseudostatic analyses are a simplified tool representing earthquakes as a static horizontal and/or vertical load on a slope. More recently, studies have focussed on probabilistic assessments of submarine landslide susceptibility which have the advantage of being more consistent with the needs of PTHA (see Section 3.2). Pioneering studies by Carlton et al. (2017) and Puzrin et al. (2017) mapped sub- marine landslides for relatively small areas (i.e., few hundred km2). Carlton et al. (2017) incorporated geotechnical data and produced static and pseudostatic factors of safety, probability of failure, and magnitude of seismically induced failures. Puzrin et al. (2017) further incorporated the concept of shear band propagation into the analysis. Collico et al. Despite the advances which have enabled landslide susceptibility to be applied offshore, there is currently a gap between these studies and PTHA. Regional susceptibility maps provide a probability that a land- slide will initiate in a general location (based on model resolution), not how far it will runout, or which landslide volume and velocity will occur, and other parameters required for tsunami simulations. Subma- rine landslides susceptibility does not directly translate into useful es- timates of landslide volumes for PTHA modelling which means that, while they might provide useful information on the most likely locations for submarine landslides, we currently still need to rely on mapped distributions of submarine landslides to provide magnitude-frequency relationships. Note that the link between landslide susceptibility and runout modelling is a current research topic onshore. 3.5. Deep learning, statistical meta models, or surrogate modelling The complexity of modeling cascading hazards, such as an earthquake-submarine landslide-tsunami system, for probabilistic as- sessments is an enormous technical challenge. Any component of this cascade can be simulated in different levels of detail, from highly complex computationally intensive to fast crude approximations. Therefore, in the past, large ensembles of individual hazards have mostly been studied with approximating algorithms that do not allow investigation of complex and important details of the hazard. For tsunami, as a prominent example, Geist (2002) investigates the effects of rupture complexity on the near shore tsunami wavefield but does not consider inundation as this would involve expensive non-linear simu- lations that were probably not feasible at the time. Others reduce the problem to a manageable size of ensembles that might not reach sta- tistical significance (e.g., Mueller et al., 2015)

studied with approximating algorithms that do not allow investigation of complex and important details of the hazard. For tsunami, as a prominent example, Geist (2002) investigates the effects of rupture complexity on the near shore tsunami wavefield but does not consider inundation as this would involve expensive non-linear simu- lations that were probably not feasible at the time. Others reduce the problem to a manageable size of ensembles that might not reach sta- tistical significance (e.g., Mueller et al., 2015) but then include the complex inundation step. Ensembles are a necessary consideration when uncertainties around the source mechanism are high. This is very much the case for potential submarine landslide sources (e.g., Zen- gaffinen-Morris et al., 2022). However, simulating the submarine landslide process in detail is another example of a computationally expensive process and often approximations are used to evaluate sta- tistically relevant ensemble sizes (e.g., Lane et al., 2016). In seismic hazard assessment probabilistic methods are widely established, but commonly based on ground motion prediction equations, which fit vast amounts of observations to empirical curves. This approach avoids the need for simulations altogether, delivers reliable estimates, but is only feasible due to the large number of observations available (Pagani et al., 2014). For the tsunami case, it results in approximations of tsunami waves behavior, because of the strong influences of bathymetry and sources’ orientation. However, for cascading hazard assessment details of the earthquake, submarine landslide and tsunami and their coupling become relevant. Often the number of required simulations is reduced by specifically designed sampling of the parameter-space to find a smaller but repre- sentative sample in the input space. These techniques are commonly referred to as ‘experimental design’ (e.g., Sacks et al., 1989). ‘Active experimental design’, also known as sequential design, uses results that have already been computed to

of bathymetry and sources’ orientation. However, for cascading hazard assessment details of the earthquake, submarine landslide and tsunami and their coupling become relevant. Often the number of required simulations is reduced by specifically designed sampling of the parameter-space to find a smaller but repre- sentative sample in the input space. These techniques are commonly referred to as ‘experimental design’ (e.g., Sacks et al., 1989). ‘Active experimental design’, also known as sequential design, uses results that have already been computed to select future input points from the parameter space (e.g., Beck and Guillas, 2016). This technique was used to study run-up amplification behind an island by Stefanakis et al. (2014). For further details please also refer to Behrens and Dias (2015). Recent advances in data science and deep machine learning, however, make the realization of complex cascading computational experiments more tangible. There are several different terms for this kind of approach. Apart from deep learning we find terms such as sur- rogate modelling, statistical modelling, statistical emulation (e.g., Beh- rens and Dias, 2015) or meta modelling (e.g., Friedman, 2012). Mostly these approaches try to estimate a multi parameter function that fits a large volume of simulated or measured examples. This is comparable to fitting a mathematical model to a large set of observations (e.g., ground motion prediction equations). The difference being that the data driven approach allows for finding the fit of a large parameter function to the available data which will normally not have any physical meaning or well-defined mathematical form. Statistical models can have hundreds of thousands of parameters. Such models recently have mostly been referred to as deep learning models. Machine learning methodologies have been used to address several aspects of the research surrounding earthquake, landslide or tsunami phenomena. Recent examples of submarine landslide hazard research range from

that the data driven approach allows for finding the fit of a large parameter function to the available data which will normally not have any physical meaning or well-defined mathematical form. Statistical models can have hundreds of thousands of parameters. Such models recently have mostly been referred to as deep learning models. Machine learning methodologies have been used to address several aspects of the research surrounding earthquake, landslide or tsunami phenomena. Recent examples of submarine landslide hazard research range from susceptibility modeling (Du et al., 2022) and mapping (Dyer et al., 2022), over detection and identification (Mark-Moser et al., 2022) to nowcasting of slope instability (Obelcz et al., 2019). In tsunami research we find that efforts are often focused on speeding up or replacing the inundation modelling process (Mulia, et al., 2022; Kamiya et al., 2022) or the forecast to the shore (Song and Cho, 2020) or to individual tide gauges (Liu et al., 2021) with machine learning models. Others have discussed how to replace numerical solvers of the mass and energy transport process (submarine landslide mechanism or the tsunami propagation or a combination thereof) with a faster statistical emulator (Behrens and Dias, 2015). The authors stress that using an emulator in place of a simulator adds additional uncertainty. This un- certainty needs to be estimated and included in the overall assessment. Recently Zhu et al. (2018, 2019, 2021) have published studies investi- gating slope stability and probability using machine learning and sur- rogate models. Deep machine learning and other statistical models can be trained and configured to consider the physics of the systems they are striving to emulate. One example of an application of physics informed models applied to the submarine landslide process was recently pub- lished by Yildiz et al. (2022). The authors investigate the estimation ability of

overall assessment. Recently Zhu et al. (2018, 2019, 2021) have published studies investi- gating slope stability and probability using machine learning and sur- rogate models. Deep machine learning and other statistical models can be trained and configured to consider the physics of the systems they are striving to emulate. One example of an application of physics informed models applied to the submarine landslide process was recently pub- lished by Yildiz et al. (2022). The authors investigate the estimation ability of a statistical emulator to estimate the run-out characteristics of two historical landslide events. Surrogate models have also been developed for speeding up complex seismic wavefield simulations (Moseley et al., 2020). Sarri et al. (2012) presented an application of Gaussian process statistical emulation for the accurate approximation of a submarine landslide-tsunami model. They demonstrated the potential of reducing significantly the computational cost using the emulator in place of the computer model. This had already been reported in Behrens and Dias (2015). 3.6. Seismo-acoustic characterisation and monitoring Targeted offshore monitoring is one of the few avenues available to acquire observational data on submarine landslides, but is challenged by deep water, remote locations, unpredictable landslide occurrence and nature, and potentially vast areas where submarine landslides may occur. Logistic and technological barriers also exist, including battery life and data storage issues (see Clare et al., in press, for an extensive review of offshore monitoring of submarine landslides). Recently, short-duration, multi-parameter targeted monitoring studies of turbi- dites have yielded measurements of event frequency, duration, velocity and sediment concentration (e.g., Talling et al., 2023). Onshore, acoustic monitoring of terrestrial landslides has been used for over a decade as an early warning tool after their seismo-acoustic signatures were first recorded unintentionally by passive systems (i.e., long-term, large-scale coverage, such as GeoNet in New Zealand; https://www.geonet.org,nz). Onshore, acoustic monitoring of terrestrial landslides has been used for over a decade as an early warning tool after their seismo-acoustic signatures were first recorded unintentionally by passive systems (i.e., long-term, large-scale coverage, such as GeoNet in New Zealand; https://www.geonet.org,nz). More recently, this has been extended offshore, showing for example that terrestrial broadband seismic net- works can detect offshore landslides (e.g., Lin et al., 2010). Also, an J.H.M. Roger et al. Marine and Petroleum Geology 162 (2024) 106729 14 opportunistic capture of 14 submarine landslides presented by Lin (2015), with data obtained during 2009 Typhoon Morakot in Taiwan using an onshore seismic monitoring network. Fan et al. (2020) identi- fied 85 submarine landslides from the Gulf of Mexico occurring between 2008 and 2015 via back interrogation of data from an onshore seismic monitoring network. The results of such studies demonstrate the ability to triangulate submarine landslide location, link occurrence with possible triggers, quantify duration of motion and even reveal kinematic information such velocity and direction (ten Brink et al., 2008; Fan et al., 2020; Yeh et al., 2021). Since the beginning of the 2000’s, researchers have also highlighted the potential to monitor submarine landslides using arrays of hydro- phones. For example, submarine landslides triggered by lava flows entering the ocean at Kilauea Volcano, Hawaii (i.e., so called “bench collapses”) (Caplan-Auerbach et al., 2001), the landslide triggered by a large eruption of the NW Rota-1 submarine volcano (Mariana Arc; Chadwick et al., 2012) or the debated ones triggered by the Bogoslof Volcano (Alaska; Tepp et al., 2020), were recorded by marine hydro- phones.

the 2000’s, researchers have also highlighted the potential to monitor submarine landslides using arrays of hydro- phones. For example, submarine landslides triggered by lava flows entering the ocean at Kilauea Volcano, Hawaii (i.e., so called “bench collapses”) (Caplan-Auerbach et al., 2001), the landslide triggered by a large eruption of the NW Rota-1 submarine volcano (Mariana Arc; Chadwick et al., 2012) or the debated ones triggered by the Bogoslof Volcano (Alaska; Tepp et al., 2020), were recorded by marine hydro- phones. Remarkably, the largest of these events (i.e., 0.001 km3) re- ported by Caplan-Auerbach et al. (2001), although a modest volume, was also detected by a separate hydrophone network over 5000 km away. These different studies highlight more or less the clear frequency spectral signature of the landslide compared to other signatures of earthquakes, volcanic eruptions or ambient noise. There is also further potential to monitor submarine landslides via instrumentation of submarine communication cables (e.g., Blum et al., 2010; Sgroi et al., 2014; Lior et al., 2021). In fact, for decades, numerous landslides, and especially turbidity currents, are known to have damaged or broken submarine cables (e.g., Hsu et al., 2008; Roger et al., 2011; Carter et al., 2014; Pope et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018). The SMART (Science Monitoring and Reliable Telecommunication) Cables initiative proposes new or decommissioned cables be instrumented by external sensor nodes (Howe et al., 2022). The CAM (Con- tinent-Azores-Madeira) Ring cable is anticipated to be the first opera- tional SMART Cable system (coming online in 2024), with ambitions for more accurate detection of offshore earthquakes, submarine landslides and tsunamis (Matias et al., 2021). In the southwest Pacific Ocean re- gion, a project started recently to install SMART Cables between New Caledonia and Vanuatu to monitor the Vanuatu Subduction Zone (Grossman et al., 2022), and discussions

be instrumented by external sensor nodes (Howe et al., 2022). The CAM (Con- tinent-Azores-Madeira) Ring cable is anticipated to be the first opera- tional SMART Cable system (coming online in 2024), with ambitions for more accurate detection of offshore earthquakes, submarine landslides and tsunamis (Matias et al., 2021). In the southwest Pacific Ocean re- gion, a project started recently to install SMART Cables between New Caledonia and Vanuatu to monitor the Vanuatu Subduction Zone (Grossman et al., 2022), and discussions for other developments within this region, for example between Ross Island, Antarctica, and New Zealand (Howe et al., 2022) are ongoing process. Clare et al. (in press) suggest that future efforts should focus on calibration of passive hydrophone and geophone records, as well as cabled networks, to increase confidence in characterization of subma- rine landslides, via targeted active monitoring studies. Priority sites should evidence previous submarine landslide activity, have pre-event bathymetry data available, and be suitable for both passive in- struments and active sensor deployment. Sites of high and predictable sediment supply and where active field campaigns are already planned could be targeted for efficiency. Back interrogation of existing broad- band seismic monitoring datasets could be further explored (e.g., Lin, 2015; Fan et al., 2020) and ideally complemented by new or repeat bathymetry surveys to confirm the occurrence of submarine landslides at triangulated locations. 4. Summary & recommendations Probabilistic Tsunami Hazard Assessment (PTHA) has become the gold standard for quantifying tsunami hazard (and following that, assessment of risk), driven mostly by the need to mitigate risk from destructive earthquake-tsunami. However, the lack of known return rates around submarine landslide sources makes PTHA challenging, as the critical input of magnitude-frequency relationships cannot be established. Consequently, it is difficult to give submarine landslide sources the same treatment as earthquake sources. The problem

Summary & recommendations Probabilistic Tsunami Hazard Assessment (PTHA) has become the gold standard for quantifying tsunami hazard (and following that, assessment of risk), driven mostly by the need to mitigate risk from destructive earthquake-tsunami. However, the lack of known return rates around submarine landslide sources makes PTHA challenging, as the critical input of magnitude-frequency relationships cannot be established. Consequently, it is difficult to give submarine landslide sources the same treatment as earthquake sources. The problem of quantifying submarine landslide return rates is not likely to be solved until: (1) Many more surveys at local or regional scale are carried out to acquire targeted age and/or geotechnical information for past landslide events (particularly smaller landslides) and regular surveys are needed in specific areas to estimate the disappearance time for small events; (2) Submarine landslide dating techniques improve so that margins for error in failure ages are significantly reduced; and (3) Monitoring of in-situ slope conditions and/or direct evidence of submarine landslide pro- cess initiation and dynamic evolution yield new insights into relation- ships between submarine landslides and their preconditioning factors, precursor events and dynamic triggers. Despite the challenges, scientists are increasingly borrowing from more mature practices applied to other natural hazards, including PTHA frameworks developed for earthquake sources and landslide suscepti- bility and process modelling developed for onshore landslides. The task before researchers is how to support increased and enhanced submarine landslide hazard and risk assessment that utilizes the most commonly available datasets and meaningfully quantifies submarine landslide- tsunami hazard against a complex backdrop of cascading hazards, perceived risk or likelihood, and justification for the resources required. We suggest a high level “road map”, framed within a realistic time period of the next 10–20 years, based on series of steps and initiatives that can be taken

for onshore landslides. The task before researchers is how to support increased and enhanced submarine landslide hazard and risk assessment that utilizes the most commonly available datasets and meaningfully quantifies submarine landslide- tsunami hazard against a complex backdrop of cascading hazards, perceived risk or likelihood, and justification for the resources required. We suggest a high level “road map”, framed within a realistic time period of the next 10–20 years, based on series of steps and initiatives that can be taken globally, to identify and assess submarine landslide- related hazards for any region or nation. We focus on logical and accessible actions to support disaster risk reduction measures (summa- rized in Fig. 9). Short term (i.e., next 1–5 years): Identification of past submarine landslide occurrence. This can be achieved via construction of subma- rine landslide databases (Section 3.1) using the framework of Clare et al. (2018) to produce consistent and comparable dataset of submarine landslide parameters. This begins with collation of existing conventional marine geophysical datasets, principally bathymetry – global collation of which is underway via the Seabed 2030 initiative (www. https://se abed2030.org). There is potential value in collating further data types however, including sub-bottom profiles, seismic reflection data and sediment samples (cores). Mid-term (i.e., next 5–10 years): (1) Improvement of simulation techniques, for both tsunami and submarine landslides, including vali- dation through real cases; (2) Transformation into tsunami hazard im- pacts; (3) probabilistic quantification of risk; and (4) integration of AI technologies. Marine and Petroleum Geology 162 (2024) 106729 15 inundation modelling of multiple credible scenarios (Section 3.2) offers the most powerful tool to demonstrate the hazard potential of identified submarine landslides, especially if coupled with detailed modelling of the landslide mechanisms and behavior. The tsunami numerical simu- lation codes for earthquake sources are improving with time, showing more and more robustness and their results are getting closer to reality with the help of significant modern events. However, there is still a need for further real-world examples to provide data of both the source (i.e., landslide parameters) and the tsunami (i.e., observation data and sea- level records) in order to validate the simulation models. Geospatial hazard metrics produced from source-specific numerical models can form the basis of evacuation zone mapping, probabilistic hazard and risk assessment and land use planning. Landslide susceptibility mapping (Section 3.4) offers a validated tool for forecasting submarine landslide occurrence with most relevance to cascading hazard scenarios involving earthquakes and other processes which trigger submarine landslides. Results can also be used for further tsunamis source modelling. Data science approaches (Section 3.5) can be applied to increase speed and efficiency of such analysis. We suggest that these mid-term steps should focus on higher risk areas (i.e., most prolific past submarine landslide occurrence balanced with socio-economic factors such as proximity to population centers, seasonal touristic areas, and/or critical coastal and marine infrastructure). other processes which trigger submarine landslides. Results can also be used for further tsunamis source modelling. Data science approaches (Section 3.5) can be applied to increase speed and efficiency of such analysis. We suggest that these mid-term steps should focus on higher risk areas (i.e., most prolific past submarine landslide occurrence balanced with socio-economic factors such as proximity to population centers, seasonal touristic areas, and/or critical coastal and marine infrastructure). Finally, probabilistic tsunami hazard assessment for submarine landslide sources (Section 3.3) can be carried out. We note the latter can take several forms and has been done comprehen- sively for some regions (e.g., U.S. Atlantic Margin), even in the absence of frequency information. For New Zealand, achievement of these mid- term goals could see submarine landslide sources included in the Na- tional Tsunami Hazard Model. The recent advent of AI technology will lead to significant im- provements in computation capability in all areas of science. AI tech- niques are currently very effective only in mainstream applications, such as generative AI in image, video and text generation, or image classifi- cation to mention a few. Geoscience comparatively speaking is still a niche where big data is not predominantly available. However, we will inevitably see more and more AI models appearing in geoscience (and other science areas). We assume that there will be a trust hurdle that will have to be overcome. Once technologies are better understood and available, we anticipate its seamlessly integration with science appli- cations with potential for huge improvements in our ability to simulate, interpret and generate complex and large data sets quickly and reliably. This shift will likely transform hazard and risk assessment studies from a high-performance computing application, run by experts over weeks and months, into applications available to laymen organizations (in- surance companies, councils etc.)

be a trust hurdle that will have to be overcome. Once technologies are better understood and available, we anticipate its seamlessly integration with science appli- cations with potential for huge improvements in our ability to simulate, interpret and generate complex and large data sets quickly and reliably. This shift will likely transform hazard and risk assessment studies from a high-performance computing application, run by experts over weeks and months, into applications available to laymen organizations (in- surance companies, councils etc.) and even individuals. Key to success in these areas is the use of modern data science, storage and delivery so- lutions which require technical expertise and domain knowledge at the same time. Close collaboration with domain trained data science teams is therefore also highly encouraged and science organizations need to invest in data science capabilities to be able to thrive in today’s data- driven landscape. Long-term (i.e., 10–20 years): (1) A strategic approach to new marine data acquisition; (2) enhanced sensor networks dedicated to monitoring and forewarning; and (3) entrain lessons learned from modern landslide- tsunami events. Fellow researchers are encouraged to prioritize and champion future data acquisition in areas with significant gaps in ba- thymetry data on continental margins and shelves. Conversely, areas where historic submarine landslides are identified, but not investigated in detail, can benefit from targeted acquisition of sub-surface geophys- ical information and sediment samples to provide age frameworks. Site specific studies on selected submarine landslides will continue to add value, especially if events are dated and tsunamigenic potential is investigated using source simulations. Ideally, such work will be com- plemented by further sub-regional to regional scale investigation of areas where multiple submarine landslides have occurred, providing detailed geophysical data, including sub-surface information, together with targeted sediment sampling to potentially date the event(s). Detection of submarine landslide

of sub-surface geophys- ical information and sediment samples to provide age frameworks. Site specific studies on selected submarine landslides will continue to add value, especially if events are dated and tsunamigenic potential is investigated using source simulations. Ideally, such work will be com- plemented by further sub-regional to regional scale investigation of areas where multiple submarine landslides have occurred, providing detailed geophysical data, including sub-surface information, together with targeted sediment sampling to potentially date the event(s). Detection of submarine landslide processes is still in its infancy (Section 3.6) but ideally, will be widely expanded. This would serve dual pur- poses of firstly, providing increased direct evidence of real-time pro- cesses and their relationship to precursor and triggering events, and secondly will form the basis for future early warning systems. Ideally future role-out of such capability will be guided by the more immediate short and mid-term activities of identifying areas prone to submarine landslides and the area’s most at risk from resulting tsunami hazards. In tandem with these suggestions which focus on hazard and risk quantification from a preparatory, “pre-event” perspective, we must also continue to learn from real events and consider how to formulate optimal response to future events. We have seen that confirmed exam- ples of submarine landslide-tsunamis are rare, with few modern exam- ples presenting both landslide source information and tsunami observations/records to learn from (e.g., 1979 Nice, France: Assier-R- zadkieaicz et al., 2000, 1994 Skagway Harbor, Alaska: Kulikov et al., 1996, 2002 Stromboli, Italy: Tinti et al., 2005, 2018 Palu, Indonesia: Nakata et al., 2020). There is however an opportunity to learn from smaller scale more frequent events, for example, those that occur in freshwater bodies such as fjords and lakes. information and tsunami observations/records to learn from (e.g., 1979 Nice, France: Assier-R- zadkieaicz et al., 2000, 1994 Skagway Harbor, Alaska: Kulikov et al., 1996, 2002 Stromboli, Italy: Tinti et al., 2005, 2018 Palu, Indonesia: Nakata et al., 2020). There is however an opportunity to learn from smaller scale more frequent events, for example, those that occur in freshwater bodies such as fjords and lakes. A recent New Zealand example is the November 2022 Lake Taup¯o earthquake, subaqueous landslide and tsunami which brings an unva- luable opportunity to study the processes from the landslide initiation to the tsunami generation, propagation, and inundation. On November 30, 2022, at night and close to a population center, a magnitude Mw 5.7 earthquake occurred in Lake Taup¯o, New Zealand’s largest lake, with a hypocenter located in the region of the Horomatangi Reef, in the eastern part of the lake (Fig. 10). Numerous terrestrial landslides were associ- ated with the shaking, and it was also followed by a small tsunami washing lines of pumice along the east and north shores and knocking over a few small boats around Wharewaka Point, a popular public beach, where the run-up distance was estimated around 42 m (Power et al., in prep). In fact, part of the beach and car park disappeared un- derwater in what appeared to be a rotational slump (identified via dives and a bathymetric survey). The very localized pattern of the tsunami with a pronounced run-up distance coincident with the location of the underwater landslide supports the hypothesis of tsunami generation locally by this landslide component, in addition to the earthquake component. Work is in progress to reproduce the tsunami records and validate the numerical model for tsunami generation with landslide source. Many data were obtained in real-time for scientists to quickly assess the event because

rotational slump (identified via dives and a bathymetric survey). The very localized pattern of the tsunami with a pronounced run-up distance coincident with the location of the underwater landslide supports the hypothesis of tsunami generation locally by this landslide component, in addition to the earthquake component. Work is in progress to reproduce the tsunami records and validate the numerical model for tsunami generation with landslide source. Many data were obtained in real-time for scientists to quickly assess the event because the Taup¯o Lake Region is highly monitored owing to a history of cataclysmic past eruptions including the largest on Earth within the last 5 ka (e.g., Wilson, 2001). A dense network of sensors takes continuous readings via seismometers, GNSS stations, and hy- drothermal fluid and gas vents analysis. In addition, there are also three lake level monitoring gauges which recorded the November 2022 tsunami with the largest amplitude recorded on the gauge facing Wharewaka Point (Fig. 10). The event attracted significant media attention and public interest. The fact that this event occurred during the night with no casualties enabled the response effort to focus on science understanding, instead of disaster relief procedures. The science response involved numerous people with different backgrounds and skills and provides an excellent example of a cohesive and well managed science response involving several agencies across science, indigenous leaders, emergency management and local government agencies. Lake Taup¯o demonstrates that we can formulate a rapid, well- managed response for a relatively small area (lake surface is ~616 km2) in a highly monitored setting. Transposing the lessons learned and methodologies applied to a larger submarine scenario in an open marine setting however is not straightforward, for several reasons: (1) the entire New Zealand coast is only sparsely instrumented by 18 operational coastal gauges transmitting real-time data, (in addition

science, indigenous leaders, emergency management and local government agencies. Lake Taup¯o demonstrates that we can formulate a rapid, well- managed response for a relatively small area (lake surface is ~616 km2) in a highly monitored setting. Transposing the lessons learned and methodologies applied to a larger submarine scenario in an open marine setting however is not straightforward, for several reasons: (1) the entire New Zealand coast is only sparsely instrumented by 18 operational coastal gauges transmitting real-time data, (in addition to 12 DART stations monitoring subduction zones more than 100 km away from the J.H.M. Roger et al. Marine and Petroleum Geology 162 (2024) 106729 16 coasts); (2) the area mobilized by the landslide could be much larger, occur in a wide variety of water depths and be difficult and very resource expensive to locate after the fact; and (3), the area impacted by the resulting tsunami could be much larger, potentially impacting coastal communities and infrastructure and necessitating disaster response measures that would take precedence over immediate science understanding. For optimal preparedness, a systematic and high-resolution bathy- metric coverage of shallow coastal zones (i.e., <50 m) and continental margins should be pursued (high resolution bathymetric data for New Zealand shallow coastal zones stands at only 30 % coverage). Additional offshore and coastal sea-level gauges should be added to the current networks to densify the instrumental coverage. Offshore gauges could be additional DART-like sensors in very specific locations where a high- degree of landslide susceptibility exists. We close by calling for enhanced collaboration of marine geo- scientists with tsunami or hydrodynamic modelers, and hazard and risk specialists. We look to the seamless integration of research by seismol- ogists and tsunami scientists towards disaster risk reduction for earth- quake tsunami, and argue that marine geologists and sedimentologists, who know most about

current networks to densify the instrumental coverage. Offshore gauges could be additional DART-like sensors in very specific locations where a high- degree of landslide susceptibility exists. We close by calling for enhanced collaboration of marine geo- scientists with tsunami or hydrodynamic modelers, and hazard and risk specialists. We look to the seamless integration of research by seismol- ogists and tsunami scientists towards disaster risk reduction for earth- quake tsunami, and argue that marine geologists and sedimentologists, who know most about enduringly enigmatic submarine landslide pro- cesses, should similarly become an integral part of tsunami disaster risk reduction. Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Journal of Asian Earth Sciences journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jseaes Petrophysics of ﬁne-grained mass-transport deposits: A critical review Qiliang Suna,b,c,d,⁎, Tiago Alvese a College of Marine Science and Technology, China University of Geosciences (CUG), Wuhan, Hubei 430074, PR China b Laboratory for Marine Mineral Resources, Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology, Qingdao 266061, China c Key Laboratory of Tectonics and Petroleum Resources, China University of Geosciences, Ministry of Education, Wuhan 430074, China d Department of Earth Sciences, University of Oxford, Oxford OX1 3AN, United Kingdom e 3D Seismic Lab, School of Earth and Ocean Sciences, Cardiﬀ University, Main Building, Park Place, Cardiﬀ CF10 3AT, United Kingdom A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Mass-transport deposits Fluid migration Petrophysics Seal competence IODP/ODP Gulf of Mexico Ulleung Basin Amazon Fan A B S T R A C T Submarine slope failures and their products occur at variable scales on continental margins and island ﬂanks. Here, we review the petrophysics of ﬁne-grained mass-transport deposits (MTDs) from three representative regions: the Ulleung Basin from oﬀshore Korea, the Ursa Region in the Gulf of Mexico, and the Amazon Fan in the Equatorial Brazil. This study shows that ﬁne-grained MTDs comprise a ‘main body’ and a ‘basal shear zone’. Compared to undeformed ‘background’ hemipelagic sediments, the main bodies of all studied MTDs are char- acterised by their: (1) higher resistivity, density, velocity and shear strength, and (2) lower water content, porosity and permeability. These properties indicate that MTDs are more consolidated than ‘background’ un- deformed strata due marked dewatering and shear compaction during their emplacement, thus enhancing the sealing competence of such strata. However, the basal shear zones show contrasting petrophysical trends, re- cording an increase in porosity when compared to the main MTD bodies. This

main bodies of all studied MTDs are char- acterised by their: (1) higher resistivity, density, velocity and shear strength, and (2) lower water content, porosity and permeability. These properties indicate that MTDs are more consolidated than ‘background’ un- deformed strata due marked dewatering and shear compaction during their emplacement, thus enhancing the sealing competence of such strata. However, the basal shear zones show contrasting petrophysical trends, re- cording an increase in porosity when compared to the main MTD bodies. This suggests that the fractured basal shear zones of MTDs serve as main ﬂuid paths, and ﬂuids can accumulate within or laterally migrate along them. This study ends by postulating that dipping strata on continental slopes can likely fail under its own gravity, with fractured, gas-charged basal shear zones at the base of MTDs comprising weak layers for further slope instability. 1. Introduction MTDs are a common component of continental margins and island ﬂanks around the world. They have been extensively studied over the past few decades, because of their signiﬁcant roles in: (1) reshaping the morphology of continental margins, (2) controlling the sedimentary architectures of continental slope basins (e.g. Posamentier and Kolla, 2003; Gee et al., 2006; Moscardelli et al., 2006; Moscardelli and Wood, 2008; Gong et al., 2014), (3) transporting large volumes of sediment into deep-water areas (e.g. Weimer, 1989; Frey-Martínez et al., 2005; Gee et al., 2006; Masson et al., 2006; Lee and Stow, 2007; Moscardelli and Wood, 2008; Li et al., 2015), and (4) generating catastrophic near- seaﬂoor geohazards (e.g. Locat and Lee, 2002; Krastel et al., 2006; Masson et al., 2006; Alves, 2015). Most studies of MTDs have thus far been based on geophysical data (e.g. multi-channel seismic, multibeam bathymetry and sidescan sonar data) and, as a result, the external morphologies and internal characters of such

1989; Frey-Martínez et al., 2005; Gee et al., 2006; Masson et al., 2006; Lee and Stow, 2007; Moscardelli and Wood, 2008; Li et al., 2015), and (4) generating catastrophic near- seaﬂoor geohazards (e.g. Locat and Lee, 2002; Krastel et al., 2006; Masson et al., 2006; Alves, 2015). Most studies of MTDs have thus far been based on geophysical data (e.g. multi-channel seismic, multibeam bathymetry and sidescan sonar data) and, as a result, the external morphologies and internal characters of such deposits are well known at a decametric (10 m) scale (e.g. McAdoo et al., 2000; Posamentier, 2004; Sawyer et al., 2007; Moscardelli and Wood, 2008; Bull et al., 2009). Mass-transport deposits have also been sampled by the DSDP/ODP/IODP drilling consortia and by exploration wells. Their general petrophysical characters (e.g. a tendency for a relative increase in density and a reduction in porosity) have conﬁrmed that MTDs chieﬂy comprise seal intervals in sedimen- tary basins (e.g. Piper et al., 1997; Shipp et al., 2004; Sawyer et al., 2007; Dugan, 2012; Reece et al., 2012; Riedel et al., 2012; Hornbach et al., 2015; Cardona et al., 2016; Bahk et al., 2017). Nevertheless, the scientiﬁc community recognises that the petrophysical characters of MTDs are still insuﬃciently addressed due to a relative lack of core samples and well-log data (e.g. Shipp et al., 2004; Dugan, 2012; Alves et al., 2014). Previous works have also been focused on discrete case studies, and broad syntheses using data from distinct continental mar- gins has seldom been completed. In order to improve the current knowledge on the petrophysical characters of MTDs, and to better as- sess their seal competence, one needs to: (1) quantitatively analyze the petrophysical character of distinct MTDs, (2) integrate distinct datasets and lithological information from diﬀerent continental margins with MTDs, and (3) explore the

T MTDs is particularly important on continental margins where petroli- ferous basins are located and ﬂuid (e.g. hydrocarbon) migration is ac- tive. MTDs triggered by ﬂuid migration occurs at present (e.g. Sultan et al., 2004; Flemings et al., 2008), but the opposite scenario in which ﬂuid migration is controlled by MTDs is still poorly understood (Sun et al., 2017). Though they are primarily interpreted as competent seal intervals, thus preventing vertical ﬂuid migration (e.g. Shipp et al., 2004; Alves et al., 2014), strata disaggregation in MTDs, reﬂected as variations in their structural and petrophysical properties, may also result in the migration and escape of ﬂuid to the surface (e.g. Paull et al., 2003; Bünz et al., 2005; Fleming et al., 2005; Masson et al., 2006). Though they are primarily interpreted as competent seal intervals, thus preventing vertical ﬂuid migration (e.g. Shipp et al., 2004; Alves et al., 2014), strata disaggregation in MTDs, reﬂected as variations in their structural and petrophysical properties, may also result in the migration and escape of ﬂuid to the surface (e.g. Paull et al., 2003; Bünz et al., 2005; Fleming et al., 2005; Masson et al., 2006). There is also limited knowledge about how homogeneous and predictable is seal competence in the largest MTDs at diﬀerent depths (from top to base) and in the three dimensions (3D). This study uses well-log data from IODP/ODP Expeditions 308 and 155, and an exploration well from the Ulleung Basin in South Korea (Riedel et al., 2012) (Fig. 1). Several MTDs were drilled by these wells and their general petrophysical characters are reported in previous studies (e.g. Piper et al., 1997; Sawyer et al., 2007, 2009; Dugan, 2012; Reece et al., 2012; Riedel et al., 2012; Bahk et al., 2017). In this study, we aim to: (1) quantitatively analyse and summarise the petrophysical characters of MTDs in three representative regions, based on our detailed observations and previous work (e.g. Piper et al., 1997; Sawyer et al., 2007; Dugan, 2012; Riedel et al., 2012); (2) understand horizontal and vertical seal heterogeneity in MTDs; (3) propose a formation model for slope failures, discussing the types of geohazards related to them. Compared to previous work, our study is quantitative and described in more detail, focusing on understanding the diﬀerences of petrophy- sical characters between MTDs and undeformed strata, and also be- tween diﬀerent parts of an MTD (“main body” and “basal shear zone”). This work also provides new data concerning the petrophysical char- acters of MTDs (see following section). 2. Data and methods Logging-while-drilling (LWD) data

This work also provides new data concerning the petrophysical char- acters of MTDs (see following section). 2. Data and methods Logging-while-drilling (LWD) data (resistivity, gamma ray, bulk density, porosity, compression velocity, shear strength and water con- tent) from IODP Sites U1322, U1323 and U1324 of Expedition 308 (Ursa Region in the Gulf of Mexico), and ODP Sites 933, 936 and 941 of Expedition 155 (Amazon Fan), plus wireline data collected from ex- ploration well UBGH 1–4 (Ulleung Basin, oﬀshore Korea), are used to address the petrophysical properties of representative MTDs in these regions. Most of the IODP and ODP data are available on http://brg. ldeo.columbia.edu/logdb/ﬁles.php, http://www-odp.tamu.edu/ publications/pubs_pr.htm and http://publications.iodp.org/. Data from exploration well UBGH 1–4 is based on Riedel et al. (2012). These data are publicly available and were used in previous studies to address the presence of gas hydrates and ﬂuid ﬂow (e.g. Long et al., 2011; Riedel et al., 2012), local sedimentation rates (e.g. Pirmez et al., 1997; Patricia et al., 1997), and the development of slope in- stability features oﬀshore South Korea (e.g. Piper et al., 1997; Sawyer et al., 2009; Urgeles et al., 2010; Dugan, 2012; Riedel et al., 2012). In this study, we mainly focus our attention on the petrophysical char- acters of strata within and involving discrete MTDs. The interpreted wireline data was loaded into Resform software. 1997), and the development of slope in- stability features oﬀshore South Korea (e.g. Piper et al., 1997; Sawyer et al., 2009; Urgeles et al., 2010; Dugan, 2012; Riedel et al., 2012). In this study, we mainly focus our attention on the petrophysical char- acters of strata within and involving discrete MTDs. The interpreted wireline data was loaded into Resform software. Sediment porosity (ø) values from IODP Expedition 308 were calculated from the bulk density (ρb) data using the method of Dugan (2012). Porosity is deﬁned as ∅ = (ρb − ρg)/(ρw − ρg), where ρg and ρw are solid-grain density and pore-ﬂuid density, respectively. Based on the observed porewater chemistry and measured grain density, the stan- dard seawater density is 1.024 g/cm3 and the constant grain density is 2.7 g/cm3 in the Gulf of Mexico (Flemings et al., 2005; Dugan, 2012). 3. Here, we focus on three sites (IODP Sites U1322, U1323 and U1324) in which ﬁne-grained MTDs accumulated. 3.1.1. IODP Site U1322 IODP Site U1322 drilled clay and mud to a depth of ~234.5 m below the sea ﬂoor (mbsf). Four MTDs, MTD1 to 4, are identiﬁed on wireline data (Fig. 2). At IODP Site U1322, resistivity increases from ~0.52 to ~0.95 Ohm·m above MTD 1 (Fig. 2). It jumps slightly to ~0.98 Ohm·m at the top of MTD 1, quickly reaching a maximum of ~1.22 Ohm·m at ~53.6 mbsf. It quickly decreases to ~1.05 Ohm·m at the base of this same MTD (Fig. 2). Peaks in resistivity are also observed at the tops of MTD 2, MTD 3 and MTD 4. For example, resistivity in- creases from ~1.20 to ~1.22 Ohm·m at the top of MTD 2. Conversely, lower resistivity values are observed at the bases of MTD 2, MTD3 and MTD 4 (Fig. 2). Gamma-ray values are highly variable, but no obvious diﬀerences are observed between MTDs and undeformed strata (Fig. 2). The bulk density of MTD 1 increases at its top until it reaches a maximum of ~1.76 g/cm3. It then decreases to ~1.70 g/cm3 at its base (Fig. 2). Similarly to MTD 1, bulk density sharply increases from ~1.80 g/cm3 to ~1.85 g/cm3 at the top of MTD 2. It gradually reaches ~1.98 g/cm3 at ~118.6 mbsf within MTD 2, and then decreases to ~1.85 g/cm3 at its base (Fig. 2). The bulk density of MTD 4 has a si- milar character to MTDs 1 and 2. However, the bulk density

it reaches a maximum of ~1.76 g/cm3. It then decreases to ~1.70 g/cm3 at its base (Fig. 2). Similarly to MTD 1, bulk density sharply increases from ~1.80 g/cm3 to ~1.85 g/cm3 at the top of MTD 2. It gradually reaches ~1.98 g/cm3 at ~118.6 mbsf within MTD 2, and then decreases to ~1.85 g/cm3 at its base (Fig. 2). The bulk density of MTD 4 has a si- milar character to MTDs 1 and 2. However, the bulk density in MTD 3 decreases from its top (~1.96 g/cm3) to its base (~1.89 g/cm3). In general, average bulk density is higher in MTDs than that in un- deformed strata (Fig. 2). For instance, MTD 2 is ~8.4% and ~3.2% denser than undeformed strata above and below. Porosity gradually decreases below the sea ﬂoor (Fig. 2). In addi- tion, both the tops and bases of MTDs 1, 2 and 4 are marked by sharp porosity changes (Fig. 2). The porosity of MTD 1 decreases from ~65% at its top to a minimum of ~55.5% at 57.6 mbsf. It then increases to ~59.6% at its base (Fig. 2). MTD 2 has similar trend to MTD 1. Its porosity decreases from ~53.1% at its top to ~43.0% at ~118.7 mbsf, increasing to ~50.5% at its base (Fig. 2). The average porosity of MTD 2 is ~11.4% and ~3.3% lower than the porosities of undeformed strata conﬁning it. The porosity of MTD 3 increases from ~43.3% at its top to ~46.9% at its base, showing an average porosity of ~44.6%. The porosity of MTD 4 is similar to that of MTD 1. It decreases to a minimum of ~44.4% at ~190.0 mbsf, to increase once again at its base (Fig. 2). Fig. 2. Petrophysical data for IODP Site U1322 (Expedition 308), Gulf of Mexico,

IODP Site U1323 IODP Site U1323 drilled through 241 m of hemipelagic mud (0–195 mbsf) and silty sand (195–241 mbsf) (Fig. 3). Two MTDs are identiﬁed on seismic and wireline data at this site. The shallower, mud-dominated MTD 5 is ~9 m thick (41–50 mbsf). The deeper MTD 6 is also mud dominated, but shows thin silt intervals (3–6 m). MTD 6 is ~98 m thick, occurring between 97 and 195 mbsf (Fig. 3). At IODP Site U1323, resistivity increases from ~0.5 to ~1.4 Ohm·m in the ﬁrst 195 m, within mud-dominated sediments (Fig. 3). Two sharp increases in resistivity, corresponding to the tops of MTDs, are ob- served. For example, resistivity increases from ~1.06 to ~1.30 Ohm·m at the top of MTD 6; the average resistivity of MTD 5 and MTD 6 is ~18% and ~23.6% higher than undeformed strata above, and ~8% and ~24.8% higher when compared with undeformed strata below (Fig. 3; Table 1). Compared to muddy undeformed sediment, the silty intervals drilled within and below MTD 6 have low resistivity. There are minor diﬀerences in

resistivity, corresponding to the tops of MTDs, are ob- served. For example, resistivity increases from ~1.06 to ~1.30 Ohm·m at the top of MTD 6; the average resistivity of MTD 5 and MTD 6 is ~18% and ~23.6% higher than undeformed strata above, and ~8% and ~24.8% higher when compared with undeformed strata below (Fig. 3; Table 1). Compared to muddy undeformed sediment, the silty intervals drilled within and below MTD 6 have low resistivity. There are minor diﬀerences in the gamma-ray curves when comparing MTDs with undeformed strata (Fig. 3). In general, gamma- ray values in the mud are much higher than in silt- or sand-dominated sediments (Fig. 3; Table 1). The bulk density of MTD 5 increases sharply at its top and keeps increasing to a maximum of ~1.68 g/cm3 at its base. The average bulk density of MTD 5 is slightly higher than undeformed strata (Table 1). There is a ~0.1 g/cm3 jump in bulk density at the top of MTD 6. Bulk density then remains constant at ~2.0 g/cm3 from 120 to 184 mbsf, with only two drops in density that correspond to silt/sand intervals (Fig. 3). At the base of MTD 6 (184–195 mbsf), the bulk density de- creases markedly and is ~12% lower than the average bulk density of MTD strata above. The bulk density of the silt/sand interval below MTD 6 gradually increases to ~1.86 g/cm3, remaining constant until the bottom of IODP Site U1323 (Fig. 3). In general, the average bulk density of MTD 6 is ~7% higher than that of undeformed strata (Table 1). IODP Site U1324 IODP Site U1324 terminates at 612 mbsf and crosses a 57 m-thick MTD 7 (110–167 mbsf) that is mud-dominated (Fig. 4). MTD 7 is characterised on seismic data by its chaotic internal reﬂections and marked top and basal surfaces (Fig. 4). Changes in resistivity, gamma ray, density and porosity are less marked at the top of MTD 7 than at its base (Fig. 4). In terms of resistivity, there is a minor increase (~0.06 Ohm·m) at the top of MTD 7, to then gradually increase with depth to an average of ~1.23 Ohm·m (Fig. 4). At the base of MTD 7, resistivity decreases sharply to ~1.1 Ohm·m (Fig. 4). Gamma-ray values at IODP Site U1324

top and basal surfaces (Fig. 4). Changes in resistivity, gamma ray, density and porosity are less marked at the top of MTD 7 than at its base (Fig. 4). In terms of resistivity, there is a minor increase (~0.06 Ohm·m) at the top of MTD 7, to then gradually increase with depth to an average of ~1.23 Ohm·m (Fig. 4). At the base of MTD 7, resistivity decreases sharply to ~1.1 Ohm·m (Fig. 4). Gamma-ray values at IODP Site U1324 decrease from ~78.0 to ~69.0 gapi from undeformed strata towards MTD 7 (Fig. 4). In MTD 7, gamma-ray values increase to ~81.5 gapi from its top to ~133 mbsf, and then decrease to ~62.0 gapi from 133 to 159 mbsf. At the base of MTD 7, the gamma ray increases again and reaches a maximum of ~82.0 gapi at 166 mbsf (Fig. 4). Bulk density increases to ~2.0 g/cm3 from the sea ﬂoor to 150 mbsf, and remains constant at ~2.0 g/cm3 from 150 to 162 mbsf (Fig. 4). The bulk density sharply decreases at the base of MTD 7 (162–166 mbsf) where a minimum of ~1.78 g/cm3 is reached. The porosity of MTD 7 shows a negative relationship with the bulk density curve. It increases sharply to reach to ~55% at the base of MTD 7 (Fig. 4). The porosity of undeformed sediments below MTD 7 is nearly constant, with an average bulk density of ~46.7%. 3.2. Petrophysical character of MTDs in the Ulleung Basin, South Korea Exploration wells were drilled in the Ulleung Basin in 2007 and 2010 to verify the presence of gas hydrates. MTDs were found in several wells (e.g. UBGH1-4, UBGH1-14, UBGH2-4, UBGH2-5A and UBGH2-8) (Riedel et al., 2012, 2013; Bahk et al., 2017). Wireline data for UBGH 1–4 is re-assessed in this work. UBGH 1–4 crossed a mud-dominated succession comprising three distinct MTDs (Fig. 5). The shallower MTD 8 is ~9 m thick (~38–47 mbsf), whereas the deeper MTD 10 is only ~4 m thick (~146–150 mbsf). MTD 9 at the middle of the drilled succession has a thickness of ~56 m from ~66 to 122 mbsf (Fig. 5). In UBGH 1–4, resistivity increases to ~1.4 Ohm·m at the top of MTD 8, dropping to ~0.8 Ohm·m at its base (Fig. 5). The resistivity sharply increases from ~0.8 to 1.4 Ohm·m at the top of MTD 9 (Fig. 5). It ﬂuctuates between ~1.2 and ~1.4 Ohm·m in MTD 9, with a sharp in- crease between 112 and 115 mbsf (maximum of ~1.8 Ohm·m). It de- creases to ~1.1 Ohm·m at the base of MTD 9 (Fig. 5). The resistivity of MTD 10 has a similar pattern to MTDs 8 and 9, increasing to ~1.5 Ohm·m at its top and dropping to ~0.8 Ohm·m at its base (Fig. 5). Gamma-ray curves do not reveal diﬀerences between MTDs and undeformed strata (Fig. 5). In MTD 9, gamma-ray values decrease slightly in its upper part, to then increase in its lower half (Fig. 5). There is a minor drop in gamma-ray values at the base of MTD 9 (Fig. 5). P-wave velocity (Vp) increases to ~1540 m/s
