ODP Site 933 ODP Site 933 has a total length of cored section approaching 254.2 m, in which a mass-transport deposit (MTD 11) with a thickness of ~70 m (~97.6–167.3 mbsf) was drilled (Fig. 6). The porosity of ODP Site 933 gradually decreases from the sea ﬂoor to the bottom of the well. Porosity greatly decreases at the top of MTD11, increasing at its base (Fig. 6a). For example, the porosity of MTD 11 decreases to ~50.8% from ~52% at its top and increases to ~58.1% from ~44.6% at its base. The porosity of MTD 11 ranges from ~41.1 to ~50.8%, with an average of ~45.8%. This is ~15.6% and ~14.2% lower than the average porosity of undeformed strata above (~54.3%) and below (~53.4%) MTD 11 (Fig. 6a). Resistivity is highly variable and generally increases with depth (Fig. 6b). It increases from ~0.39 Ohm·m in undeformed strata to ~0.45 Ohm·m at the top of MTD 11 (Fig. 6b). It also decreases from ~0.54 Ohm·m at the base of MTD 11 to ~0.36 Ohm·m in undeformed strata below MTD 11. In general, MTD 11 has an average resistivity of ~0.47 Ohm·m that is ~9.3% and ~17.5% higher than undeformed strata above (with an average of ~0.43 Ohm·m within a ~20 m in- terval) and below (average of ~0.40 Ohm·m within a ~30 m interval). MTD 11 is denser than undeformed strata above and below (Fig. 6c). The density increases from ~1.87 g/cm2 to ~1.92 g/cm2 at the top of MTD 11, and decreases to ~1.79 g/cm2 from ~2.04 g/cm2 at the base of MTD 11. In general, MTD 11 has an average

is ~9.3% and ~17.5% higher than undeformed strata above (with an average of ~0.43 Ohm·m within a ~20 m in- terval) and below (average of ~0.40 Ohm·m within a ~30 m interval). MTD 11 is denser than undeformed strata above and below (Fig. 6c). The density increases from ~1.87 g/cm2 to ~1.92 g/cm2 at the top of MTD 11, and decreases to ~1.79 g/cm2 from ~2.04 g/cm2 at the base of MTD 11. In general, MTD 11 has an average density of ~2.00 g/cm2, ranging from ~1.91 to 2.11 g/cm2. It is ~6.4% higher than undeformed strata above (~1.88 g/cm2 within a ~20 m interval) and below (~1.88 g/cm2 within a ~30 m interval). 3.3.2. ODP Site 936 ODP Site 936 drilled ~300 m of muddy deposits. A ~141 m thick MTD 12 was crossed from ~153 to 294 mbsf (Fig. 6). Similarly to ODP Site 933, porosity gradually decreases with depth (Fig. 6a). Porosity decreases from ~52.1% in undeformed strata above MTD 12 to ~45.9% at its top. It increases from ~40% of the base of MTD 12 to ~45.3% in undeformed strata below. The average porosity of MTD 12 is ~44%, varying from ~39.1% to 53.3% (Fig. 6a). These values are ~19.1% and ~3.6% lower than the average porosity of undeformed strata above (average of ~54.4% in a ~50 m-thick interval above MTD 12) and below (with an average porosity of ~45.7% in a ~10 m-thick interval below MTD 12). The resistivity of MTD 12 at ODP Site 936 ranges between ~0.41 Ohm·m and ~0.73 Ohm·m with an average of ~0.51 Ohm·m (Fig. 6b). It is ~24.4% and ~8.5% higher than the average resistivity of undeformed strata above (~0.41 Ohm·m within a ~40 m interval) and below (~0.47 Ohm·m within a ~10 m interval). Moreover, the re- sistivity

~50 m-thick interval above MTD 12) and below (with an average porosity of ~45.7% in a ~10 m-thick interval below MTD 12). It is ~24.4% and ~8.5% higher than the average resistivity of undeformed strata above (~0.41 Ohm·m within a ~40 m interval) and below (~0.47 Ohm·m within a ~10 m interval). Moreover, the re- sistivity varies sharply at the boundaries between MTD 12 and un- deformed strata (Fig. 6b). For example, it increases from ~0.42 Ohm·m to ~0.48 Ohm·m at the top of MTD 12, and decreases from ~0.61 Ohm·m to ~0.44 Ohm·m at the base of MTD 12. In general, the average density of MTD 12 is ~2.04 g/cm2, ranging between ~1.84 g/cm2 and 2.15 g/cm2. It is ~9.1% and ~1.0% larger than the density of undeformed strata above (average of ~1.87 g/cm2 in a ~40 m-thick interval above MTD 12) and below (with an average of ~2.02 g/cm2 in a ~10 m-thick interval below MTD 12). 3.3.3. The porosity of MTD 13 ranges from ~44.3% to ~64.3% and has an average of ~54.8%. It is ~8.1% higher than the average porosity of undeformed strata below (average of ~50.7% in a ~20 m-thick in- terval), but ~26.7% lower than the average porosity of undeformed strata above (~74.8%, ~5.3 m).

The porosity of MTD 13 ranges from ~44.3% to ~64.3% and has an average of ~54.8%. It is ~8.1% higher than the average porosity of undeformed strata below (average of ~50.7% in a ~20 m-thick in- terval), but ~26.7% lower than the average porosity of undeformed strata above (~74.8%, ~5.3 m). Porosity increases from ~49.4% at the base of MTD 13 to ~51.3% in undeformed strata below. It also in- creases from 64.2% at the top of MTD 13 to ~74.8% in undeformed strata above (Fig. 6a). Resistivity varies sharply across the upper and lower boundaries of MTD 13 (Fig. 6b). For example, it decreases from ~0.41 Ohm·m at the base of MTD 13 to ~0.39 Ohm·m in undeformed strata below. The average resistivity of MTD 13 is ~0.33 Ohm·m, ranging from ~0.21 Ohm·m to ~0.47 Ohm·m; i.e. ~65% higher than that of un- deformed strata above (~0.20 Ohm·m in the 5.3 m-thick interval to the sea ﬂoor). However, it is ~25% lower than the resistivity of un- deformed strata below MTD 13 (~0.44 Ohm·m within a ~20 m in- terval). In terms of density, MTD 13 shows similar trends to MTD 11 and MTD 12 (Fig. 6c). It shows a sharp increase in density from ~1.50 g/ cm2 in undeformed strata above, to ~1.65 g/cm2 at the top of MTD 12. At the base of MTD 13, the density decreases from ~1.99 g/cm2 to ~1.92 g/cm2. In general, the density of MTD 13 ranges from ~1.65 g/ cm2 to ~2.02 g/cm2, for an average of ~1.85 g/cm2. This average value is ~23.3% higher than for undeformed strata above MTD 13

Trendlines of MTDs and undeformed strata in the Amazon Fan Three ODP sites (ODP Sites 933, 936 and 941) in the Amazon Fan show some typical diﬀerences (trendlines) between ﬁne-grained MTDs and undeformed strata (Fig. 6). The densities of MTDs and undeformed strata from the three sites show well-ﬁtted logarithm relationships; R2 = 0.76 for undeformed strata and R2 = 0.80 for MTDs, respectively (Fig. 6c). However, the calculated best-ﬁt curves suggest that the density of MTDs is larger than undeformed strata, with this diﬀerence becoming more pronounced with depth (Fig. 6c). Regarding porosity, MTDs and undeformed strata also show a well- ﬁtted logarithm relationship; R2 = 0.73 for undeformed strata and R2 = 0.85 for MTDs (Fig. 6c). Similarly to the density trend, the dif- ference between the porosity of MTDs and undeformed strata becomes more pronounced with depth (Fig. 6c). The best-ﬁt curves suggest that the porosity is always lower in MTDs than in undeformed strata (Fig. 6c). Compared to density and porosity, the best-ﬁt resistivity curves diﬀer for MTDs and undeformed strata (Fig. 6b). In shallow strata (< 100 mbsf), the resistivity of MTDs is lower than undeformed strata. However, the opposite occurs in strata deeper than 100 mbsf. In gen- eral, the resistivities

between the porosity of MTDs and undeformed strata becomes more pronounced with depth (Fig. 6c). Shear strength of MTDs The shear strength of strata drilled at IODP Sites 1322 and 1324 in the Gulf of Mexico is highly variable, increasing gradually with depth (Fig. 7a). When considering MTD 1 and MTD 2 at IODP Site 1322, shear strength quickly increases from their top to their base, with maxima in shear strength recorded at their bases (Fig. 7a). Shear strength usually decreases sharply from the bases of MTDs into undeformed strata, e.g. shear strength drops from ~86.3 kPa to ~48.4 kPa at the base of MTD 2 (Fig. 7a). In MTD 7 at IODP Site 1324, shear strength increases from the top of MTD 7 to undeformed strata below. In general, the shear strengths of MTDs and undeformed strata at IODP Sites 1322 and 1324 show linear trends (Fig. 7a). The shear strength of undeformed strata is well-ﬁtted with a R2 = 0.71. However, the shear strength of MTDs only shows a tentative linear relationship (R2 = 0.30) (Fig. 7a). The trends in shear strength are usually higher in MTDs than in undeformed strata, but they tend to intersect at relatively shallow depths below the sea ﬂoor (Fig. 7a). The shear strength

shear strengths of MTDs and undeformed strata at IODP Sites 1322 and 1324 show linear trends (Fig. 7a). The shear strength of undeformed strata is well-ﬁtted with a R2 = 0.71. However, the shear strength of MTDs only shows a tentative linear relationship (R2 = 0.30) (Fig. 7a). The trends in shear strength are usually higher in MTDs than in undeformed strata, but they tend to intersect at relatively shallow depths below the sea ﬂoor (Fig. 7a). The shear strength of strata drilled at ODP Sites 933, 936 and 941 (Amazon Fan) is also highly variable, once again increasing gradually Q. Sun and T. Alves Journal of Asian Earth Sciences 192 (2020) 104291 8 with depth (Fig. 7b). The shear strength of MTDs is usually higher than undeformed strata. For example, the average shear strength of MTD 11 (~59.1 kPa) is ~53.8% and ~31.4% higher than undeformed strata above (~38.4 kPa within a ~20 m interval) and below (~45.0 kPa within ~20 m interval) (Fig. 7b). Moreover, the shear strength usually varies sharply at the boundaries of MTDs with undeformed strata, showing greater shear strength than undeformed strata above and below. For example, shear strength drops from ~69.3 kPa at the base of MTD 12 to ~53.6 kPa in undeformed strata below (Fig. 7b). In general, both the shear strengths of MTDs and undeformed strata at ODP Sites 933, 936 and 941 show well-ﬁtted linear relationships (Fig. 7b). Im- portantly, the ﬁtted curves of shear strength diverge with depth, with the MTDs’ best-ﬁt curve being always above that of undeformed strata (Fig. 7b). 3.5. It is ~27.0% and ~4.6% lower than those of undeformed strata above (average of ~31.1% within a ~10 m-thick interval) and below (average of ~23.8% in a ~10 m-thick interval). MTD 13 has an average water content of ~31.2%, ranging from ~22.9% to ~40.4%; a value that is ~40.2% lower than that recorded by undeformed strata above (average of ~52.2% within a 5.3 m in- terval) and ~13.5% lower than that of undeformed strata below (average of 27.5% within a 10 m interval). Water contents for MTDs and undeformed strata show well-ﬁtted logarithm relationships; R2 = ~0.92 for undeformed strata and R2 = ~0.87 for MTDs (Fig. 8). In addition, the water content curve for undeformed strata is always above that of MTDs, and this diﬀerence is kept constant at depth (Fig. 8). 3.6. Petrophysical properties of the main bodies and basal shear zones of MTDs According to the trends of wireline curves, MTDs can be subdivided into two parts: their main bodies (the upper parts of MTDs) and their basal shear zones (the lowermost parts of MTDs) (Figs. 2–5). These two distinctive parts show opposite trends on wireline data. For example, resistivity, bulk density and p-wave velocity (Vp) sharply decrease at the basal shear zone, when compared to main body of MTD (Figs. 2–5). MTDs According to the trends of wireline curves, MTDs can be subdivided into two parts: their main bodies (the upper parts of MTDs) and their basal shear zones (the lowermost parts of MTDs) (Figs. 2–5). These two distinctive parts show opposite trends on wireline data. For example, resistivity, bulk density and p-wave velocity (Vp) sharply decrease at the basal shear zone, when compared to main body of MTD (Figs. 2–5). Porosity greatly increases at the basal shear zone, contrasting to the relatively lower porosity documented in the main body of MTD (Figs. 2–5). The average resistivity of the main body and basal shear zone of MTD shows a linear correlation (R2 = ~0.54; Fig. 9a). Moreover, most of the average resistivity of basal shear zone (75%) is higher than that of the main body of MTD (Fig. 9a; Table 2). In contrast, there are no major diﬀerences between the average gamma-ray of the main MTD bodies and their basal shear zones (Fig. 9b; Table 2). The R2 value of ~0.92 estimated for gamma-ray indicates a near-perfect linear re- lationship (Fig. 9b). The average density of basal shear zones is lower than those in the main MTD bodies, with a R2 value of ~0.64 (Fig. 9c; Table 2). In contrast to the average density, the average porosity of basal shear zones is higher than those in the main MTD bodies (Table 2), and it has a relatively moderate R2 value of around 0.45 (Fig. 9d). Though the maximum thickness of basal shear zones can reach up to ~10.5 m (MTD 2), most (75%) are markedly thin, ranging in thickness between ~2.7 m and ~5.0 m (Fig. 9e; Table 2). The main bodies of MTDs are usually 2–19 times thicker than the basal shear zones (see also Alves and Lourenço, 2010) (Fig.

is higher than those in the main MTD bodies (Table 2), and it has a relatively moderate R2 value of around 0.45 (Fig. 9d). Though the maximum thickness of basal shear zones can reach up to ~10.5 m (MTD 2), most (75%) are markedly thin, ranging in thickness between ~2.7 m and ~5.0 m (Fig. 9e; Table 2). The main bodies of MTDs are usually 2–19 times thicker than the basal shear zones (see also Alves and Lourenço, 2010) (Fig. 9e). The total thickness of MTDs also shows a partial correlation with the thicknesses of basal shear zones (R2 = ~0.42; Fig. 9f). 4. Discussion 4.1. Factors inﬂuencing the petrophysical properties of MTDs Through the detailed analysis of petrophysical data from ﬁne- grained MTDs in the Gulf of Mexico, Ulleung Basin and Amazon Fan, and from MTDs samples by previous work (e.g. Piper et al., 1997; Sawyer et al., 2007, 2009; Dugan, 2012; Riedel et al., 2012; Reece et al., 2012), several key properties were recognised. They include: (1) Resistivity, bulk density and p-wave velocity (Vp) usually increase at the top of MTDs, but decreasing sharply at their bases; (2) Average resistivity is much higher in MTDs than that in undeformed strata conﬁning these former deposits (Figs. 2–5; Table 1); (3) Gamma-ray values show variable patterns in distinct MTDs, with no clear trends observed between MTDs and undeformed strata (Figs. 2–5); (4) Porosity usually drops at the top of MTDs to increase at their bases, showing a reverse trend to bulk density (Figs. 2–5 and 10); (5) The average porosity of MTDs is usually lower than the porosity of undeformed strata directly above and below (Figs. 2–5); Fig. 8. Alves Journal of Asian Earth Sciences 192 (2020) 104291 10 (6) The shear strength of MTDs increases from their tops to their bases, and is usually higher than the shear strength of undeformed strata above and below (Figs. 7 and 10); (7) Water content in MTDs is much lower than that in undeformed strata, with sharp variations observed at the top and base of MTDs (Fig. 8). Apart from these common characteristics, we also observe that the petrophysical character of basal shear zones of MTDs have opposite trends to their main bodies, i.e. increasing porosities and decreasing resistivity, bulk density and velocity (Figs. 2–5 and 10). Gamma-ray values are mainly controlled by lithology. As the stu- died MTDs are mainly composed of mud-dominated sediment of similar composition to ‘background’ slope strata, there are no sharp increases or decreases in gamma-ray values between MTDs and their conﬁning undeformed intervals (Figs. 2–5 and 10). Resistivity, bulk density, velocity and porosity indicate that the MTDs are more consolidated than background sediment, as also re- ported by many previous studies (e.g. Piper et al., 1997; Shipp et al., 2004; Strasser et al., 2011; Dugan, 2012; Sun et al., 2018) (Fig. 10). Apart from overburden stress, failed sediment is also subjected to shear stress during its emplacement

are no sharp increases or decreases in gamma-ray values between MTDs and their conﬁning undeformed intervals (Figs. 2–5 and 10). Resistivity, bulk density, velocity and porosity indicate that the MTDs are more consolidated than background sediment, as also re- ported by many previous studies (e.g. Piper et al., 1997; Shipp et al., 2004; Strasser et al., 2011; Dugan, 2012; Sun et al., 2018) (Fig. 10). Apart from overburden stress, failed sediment is also subjected to shear stress during its emplacement (Fig. 10), partly justifying the observed over-consolidation of the studied MTDs (Figs. 2–6). This additional source of shear stress results in a reduction in porosity, with consequent dewatering of failed strata. Dewatering would also result in relative increases in density and Vp values, as observed in this study; resistivity is more sensitive to the loss of water in mud-dominated sediments, and thus sharp variations can be observed at the tops and bases of MTDs (Fig. 10). Opposite trends to the latter are observed when comparing the basal shear zones with the main bodies of MTDs, suggesting that the con- solidation of strata in basal shear zones is relatively moderate. This characteristic is likely caused by extreme shearing at the base of MTDs to form shear fractures and pervasive fabric in their basal shear zones (Alves and Lourenço, 2010; Alves, 2015; Cardona et al., 2016) (Fig. 10). The shear stress always increases from the tops of the MTDs to their bases (e.g. Piper et al., 1997; De Blasio et al., 2004), because of the relative movement (friction) experienced by failed sediment, and also between the failed sediment and undeformed strata below. Such an observation is supported by the wireline curve shapes in the MTDs, particularly by the gradual increase in resistivity recorded in them (Figs. 2–6). When the shear stress exceeds

The shear stress always increases from the tops of the MTDs to their bases (e.g. Piper et al., 1997; De Blasio et al., 2004), because of the relative movement (friction) experienced by failed sediment, and also between the failed sediment and undeformed strata below. Such an observation is supported by the wireline curve shapes in the MTDs, particularly by the gradual increase in resistivity recorded in them (Figs. 2–6). When the shear stress exceeds the shear strength of inclined strata on a continental slope, at the critical point for shear failure, fractures would form within the soon-to-be basal shear zone, as cor- roborated by: (1) the larger shear stress limits of strata at the base of MTDs (Fig. 7), and (2) the widespread occurrence of fractures at the base of MTDs in cores and FMI (resistivity image logging) data from the Ulleung Basin (Riedel et al., 2012), Nankai Trough (Expedition 333 Scientists, 2011), Amazon Fan (Piper et al., 1997) and eastern North American margin (Tripsanas et al., 2008). MTDs and ﬂuid ﬂow Based on the low water contents of MTDs (Fig. 8) and the abundant ﬂuid ﬂow structures in them, we postulate a large volume loss of ﬂuid during MTD emplacement (Piper et al., 1997; Shipp et al., 2004; Strasser et al., 2011). The over-consolidation of MTDs during their emplacement (and burial) not only results in the loss of porosity, but also causes a relative reduction in permeability. For example, MTDs at IODP Sites U1324 (MTD 7) and U1322 (MTD 2) in the Gulf of Mexico show reductions in permeability in the order of ~33% and ~71% with respect to normally-consolidated mud above and below (Dugan, 2012). Porosity and permeability losses in MTDs greatly increase their seal competence, a character indicating that they generally comprise seal intervals and hinder vertical ﬂuid ﬂow after their emplacement (e.g. Shipp et al., 2004; Reece et al., 2012; Alves et al., 2014; Hornbach et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2017, 2018) (Fig. 10). Compared to the main body of the MTDs, the basal shear zone has a distinctive role in controlling ﬂuid ﬂow (Fig. 10). Due to the occurrence of highly porous zones (e.g. fractured strata), ﬂuid can accumulate within or migrate upslope along basal shear zones (e.g, Alves and Lourenço, 2010; Sun et al., 2017). This is the main reason why ﬂuid seepage and cold-water carbonates are usually observed at the headwall scarps of

Due to the occurrence of highly porous zones (e.g. fractured strata), ﬂuid can accumulate within or migrate upslope along basal shear zones (e.g, Alves and Lourenço, 2010; Sun et al., 2017). This is the main reason why ﬂuid seepage and cold-water carbonates are usually observed at the headwall scarps of MTDs, such as in the case of the Eivissa Channel of western Mediterranean Sea (Lastras et al., 2004), Amazon Fan (Dano et al., 2014), north Sicily continental margin (Pennino et al., 2014) and Great Bahama Bank (Principaud et al., 2015). The latest observations from the South China Sea show that free gas accumulates at the bases of MTDs, conﬁrming their basal shear zones are relatively porous (Sun et al., 2017). 4.3. Implications to the recurrent triggering of submarine landslides Submarine slope instability is a theme widely studied, and phe- nomena such as earthquakes (e.g. Moscardelli et al., 2006), high sedi- mentation rates (e.g. Dalla Valle et al., 2013; Noda et al., 2013), gas hydrate dissolution (e.g. Laberg and Vorren, 2000; Maslin et al., 2005), sea-level variations (e.g. Smith et al., 2013; Urlaub et al., 2013), and tectonic activity (e.g. Chadwick et al., 2012; Laberg et al., 2014), have been proposed in the literatures as capable of triggering large land- slides. However, one is not entirely sure of the factors triggering slope failures in most documented cases of submarine slope instability. In particular, there is signiﬁcant less knowledge about the critical condi- tions under which a slope failure will occur. Most published data con- cerns ancient slope failures, with their formation mechanisms and triggers being hard to estimate. In addition, it

et al., 2012; Laberg et al., 2014), have been proposed in the literatures as capable of triggering large land- slides. However, one is not entirely sure of the factors triggering slope failures in most documented cases of submarine slope instability. In particular, there is signiﬁcant less knowledge about the critical condi- tions under which a slope failure will occur. Most published data con- cerns ancient slope failures, with their formation mechanisms and triggers being hard to estimate. In addition, it is diﬃcult to predict when and where a slope failure will occur in the future, and there is an overall lack of in-situ measurements in the areas where recent sub- marine landslides have occurred. This study suggests that slope sediments can fail under their own gravity when gravitational forces along the slope overcome the shear strength of sediments. This failure mechanism does not require other triggers, such as earthquakes, gas hydrate dissolution and sea-level ﬂuctuations, and is likely responsible for some of the frequent sub- marine slope failures at the steep slopes, such as oﬀshore northern Norway where muddy contourites failed along the surfaces between them and coarse-grained turbidites on a steep continental slope (Laberg et al., 2016) (Fig. 11). Accompanying this increase in sediment thick- ness, fractures would occur in slope strata in response to increasing shear stress (Figs. 10 and 11b). Under the eﬀect of ﬂuids and the subsequent formation of the weak layer, overlying strata will fail and slope failure will occur in a process similar to the pressure- driven slope failure reported in Flemings et al. (2008), Shillington et al. (2012), Le Friant et al. (2015) and Hornbach et al. (2015) (Fig. 11c). Fluids can migrate laterally along the basal shear zone, to ﬁnally escape from the extensional headwall zone (Fig. 11c), as documented in the Eivissa Channel of the western Mediterranean Sea (Lastras et al., 2004). This study also suggests that the main bodies of MTDs can generate important overpressure below them, with unexpected high pore pres- sures being likely encountered during drilling. Such overpressures have been documented under MTDs in the Gulf of Mexico (Sawyer et al., 2009; Reece et al., 2012) and the Islands of Montserrat and Martinique in the Caribbean Sea (Hornbach et al., 2015). Furthermore, the lateral migration of ﬂuids, especially along the basal shear zone, may trigger new slope instability in shallow strata

also suggests that the main bodies of MTDs can generate important overpressure below them, with unexpected high pore pres- sures being likely encountered during drilling. Such overpressures have been documented under MTDs in the Gulf of Mexico (Sawyer et al., 2009; Reece et al., 2012) and the Islands of Montserrat and Martinique in the Caribbean Sea (Hornbach et al., 2015). Furthermore, the lateral migration of ﬂuids, especially along the basal shear zone, may trigger new slope instability in shallow strata to form regressive slope failures, as reported in the South China Sea (Sun et al., 2017). 5. Conclusions Through the detailedly qualitative and quantitative analysis of well- log data from IODP/ODP Sites and one exploration well, we ﬁnd that the main bodies of MTDs generally have similar geophysical properties to those indicated in previous studies. However, we identify some new characteristics and trends that are important for the analysis of MTDs as potential geohazards. The main results of this work are as follows: 1. When compared to undeformed strata above and below, the main bodies of MTDs are typically characterized by their higher re- sistivity, velocity, bulk density and shear strength, plus their lower porosity, water content and permeability; 2. The main bodies of MTDs are more consolidated than undeformed strata above and below, probably a character resulting from dewa- tering and shear compaction during MTD emplacement; 3. The petrophysical character of the lowermost parts of MTDs (basal shear zones) are the opposite (i.e. they show lower resistivity, ve- locity, bulk density and higher porosity) from the main bodies of MTDs. This diﬀerence is likely attributed to the presence of fractures in basal shear zones caused by excessive shear stress; 4. The main bodies of MTDs could serve as good seal units after their emplacement to hinder vertical ﬂuid migration.

tering and shear compaction during MTD emplacement; 3. The petrophysical character of the lowermost parts of MTDs (basal shear zones) are the opposite (i.e. they show lower resistivity, ve- locity, bulk density and higher porosity) from the main bodies of MTDs. This diﬀerence is likely attributed to the presence of fractures in basal shear zones caused by excessive shear stress; 4. The main bodies of MTDs could serve as good seal units after their emplacement to hinder vertical ﬂuid migration. However, ﬂuids could accumulate or laterally migrate along fractured basal shear zones; 5. Inclined sediments can fail under their own gravity without any triggers (e.g. earthquakes) with fractured zones, possibly charged by ﬂuid, acting as weak layers. In addition, fractured basal shear zone with high pore pressure represents an important geohazard when drilling. Full length article Mass transport deposits in confined environment, a case study from the Kaiping sag, Pearl River Mouth basin, northern south China sea margin Lushan Jiang , Xiwu Luan * College of Earth Science and Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, Shandong 266590, China A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords: Mass transport deposits Confined environment Compression High sedimentation rate Sea level change The Kaiping sag A B S T R A C T Mass transport deposits (MTDs) have been extensively discussed in the literature. The prevailing understanding characterizes MTDs as gravity-driven instability phenomena occurring on the seafloors. This study employs high- resolution 2D/3D seismic data integrated with well data to re-evaluate the genesis mechanisms of MTDs in the Kaiping sag, Zhu-II depression, Pearl River Mouth basin (PRMB), northern margin of the South China Sea (SCS). Key diagnostic features interpreted from seismic datasets reveal the absence of typical characteristics of con- ventional MTDs, including no distinct headwall scarps, and moats, and especially no onlapping structures of isochronous turbidite contourites that usually cover uneven MTD surfaces. In contrast, seismic evidence for a tectonic compression environment is observed, such as thrust fault reactivation, localized stratal folding, and chaotic thrust-related deformation patterns within the study interval-consistent with the post-spreading tectonic setting of the SCS. For the first time, this research concludes that these MTDs formed in a confined environment (rather than an open continental slope), and were induced by mega-earthquakes. The key contributing factors to their formation include: (1) tectonic compression after the cessation of SCS seafloor spreading (post-16 Ma), evidenced by seismic records of thrusting and folding; (2) elevated sedimentation rates since the peak uplift of the eastern Tibetan plateau (since 15 Ma), leading to thick unconsolidated sediments; (3) periods of higher sea level

the first time, this research concludes that these MTDs formed in a confined environment (rather than an open continental slope), and were induced by mega-earthquakes. The key contributing factors to their formation include: (1) tectonic compression after the cessation of SCS seafloor spreading (post-16 Ma), evidenced by seismic records of thrusting and folding; (2) elevated sedimentation rates since the peak uplift of the eastern Tibetan plateau (since 15 Ma), leading to thick unconsolidated sediments; (3) periods of higher sea level (favouring high pore-water pressure); and (4) mega-earthquakes triggered by the post-spreading compression. Collectively, these factors facilitated the liquefaction processes that drove MTDs development. 1. Introduction Mass transports, including slides, slumps, and debris flows, consti- tute critical gravity-driven sedimentary processes on continental slope, exhibiting well-documented variability in their movement mechanics and depositional characteristics (Murray and Renard, 1891; Varnes, 1958; Dott, 1963; Shanmugam, 2012, 2020; Palladino and Giordano, 2019; Alves, 2015; Deckers, 2015; Gee et al., 2007). Specifically, slides demonstrate consistent translational movement downslope with mini- mal internal deformation. Slumps, by contrast, involve rotational displacement along a concave-up surface, characterized by significant internal stratigraphic disruption. Debris flows, which originate from water-saturated slumps, behave as plastic laminar flows dominated by intergranular shear (Alves, 2015; Deckers, 2015; Gee et al., 2007). To date, nearly all globally reported MTDs- regardless of detection methods (e.g., side-scan sonar, multibeam echosounder, sub-bottom profiler), or morphological types (slide, slump, debris flow) −have been associated with open continental margin settings. In these environments, unconfined downslope movement is unimpeded by basin-bounding structures or overlying sediment loads (Lamarche et al., 2008; Strasser et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2013). The application of 3D seismic data has significantly improved our understanding of MTD geometry, spatial extent, and depositional genesis (Alves, 2010), while prompting re- visions to conventional MTD conceptual models (He et al.,

sonar, multibeam echosounder, sub-bottom profiler), or morphological types (slide, slump, debris flow) −have been associated with open continental margin settings. In these environments, unconfined downslope movement is unimpeded by basin-bounding structures or overlying sediment loads (Lamarche et al., 2008; Strasser et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2013). The application of 3D seismic data has significantly improved our understanding of MTD geometry, spatial extent, and depositional genesis (Alves, 2010), while prompting re- visions to conventional MTD conceptual models (He et al., 2013; Gong et al., 2014). Additionally, MTDs have gained substantial economic importance, as numerous deep-water petroleum reservoirs worldwide produce hydrocarbons from MTD-hosted sandstones (Shanmugam, 2012, 2020). Despite these advancements, a critical research gap re- mains: the formation mechanisms, diagnostic seismic features, and triggering factors of MTDs in confined environments (e.g., sedimentary soft layers bounded and buried by overlying strata) remain largely un- explored. Existing knowledge basically excludes such settings, thereby limiting our ability to fully understand formation processes and comprehensively characterize MTDs. Compression-induced uplift of Taiwan and Dongsha during this period is expected to generate mega-earthquakes in the sag region; (2) high sedimentation rates, driven by the peak uplift of the eastern Tibetan plateau (since 15 Ma), have deposited a thick unconsolidated stratum within the sag; and (3) periodic high sea-level stands have formed highly saturated argillaceous deposits in the sag, providing foundation for the development of MTDs (Dai et al., 1998; Pang et al., 1995). The pri- mary objectives of this study are: (1) to identify the diagnostic seismic features of MTDs in the Kaiping sag by integrating 2D/3D seismic and well data, and to compare these features with those of conventional open-environment MTDs; (2) to evaluate the roles of key controlling factors (tectonic compression, sedimentation rate, sea-level change, and mega-earthquakes) on MTD development; and (3) to extend the MTD conceptual model to include confined environments, thereby addressing an existing gap in MTD research. 2. Geological setting The SCS basin is floored primarily by oceanic crust at central and by rifted continental crusts at margins, and by continent-ocean boundary (COB) crust in between (Zhang et al., 2021a, 2024; Zhang and Luan, 2012) (Fig. 1). The oceanic SCS basin is complicated by extinct spreading ridges, transform faults, and seamounts (Yan et al., 2006; Xu et al., 2012). The continental margins underwent rifting, sedimentation, and deformation in response to multiple tectonic processes: the collision, subduction of the Philippine plate to the Eurasian plate, the

primarily by oceanic crust at central and by rifted continental crusts at margins, and by continent-ocean boundary (COB) crust in between (Zhang et al., 2021a, 2024; Zhang and Luan, 2012) (Fig. 1). The oceanic SCS basin is complicated by extinct spreading ridges, transform faults, and seamounts (Yan et al., 2006; Xu et al., 2012). The continental margins underwent rifting, sedimentation, and deformation in response to multiple tectonic processes: the collision, subduction of the Philippine plate to the Eurasian plate, the uplift associated with of the Himalayan orogeny (Ding et al., 2022), the spreading of the oceanic SCS basin itself, and subsequent post-spreading activities (Lu et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2014; Larsen et al., 2018). These processed collectively evolved the region into the present-day wide rifting system (Corti et al., 2003; England, 1983; Buck, 1991; Benes and Davy, 1996; Brun et al., 1994). Their various ages related to oceanic spreading, continental rifting and post spreading volcanic and magmatic activities are determined by the Ocean Discovery Program (ODP) Expedition 184 (Wang et al., 2000), and the International Ocean Dis- covery Program (IODP) Expedition 349, 367/368 (Expedition 349 Sci- entists, 2014; Expedition 367/368 Scientists, 2018; Larsen et al., 2018), and by industry wells at marginal basins (Fig. 1). The East Sub-basin started opening at ~ 30 Ma (Zhang et al., 2024) stopped around 15 Ma, and the Southwest Sub-basin initiated at ~ 23.6 Ma and terminated at about 16 Ma (Li et al., 2015). The Luzon arc is believed to be formed at about 15 Ma (Briais et al., 1993; Yang et al., 1996). The Taiwan orogeny started to buildup due to the westward movement of the Luzon arc and its oblique collision with the Eurasian continental margin (Teng, 1990). The Cenozoic magmatism in the region can be divided into three stages:

around 15 Ma, and the Southwest Sub-basin initiated at ~ 23.6 Ma and terminated at about 16 Ma (Li et al., 2015). The Luzon arc is believed to be formed at about 15 Ma (Briais et al., 1993; Yang et al., 1996). The Taiwan orogeny started to buildup due to the westward movement of the Luzon arc and its oblique collision with the Eurasian continental margin (Teng, 1990). The Cenozoic magmatism in the region can be divided into three stages: the Paleocene-Eocene pre-spreading stage (>33 Ma), the Oligocene- middle Miocene spreading stage (33–15 Ma), and the late Miocene- Quaternary post-spreading stage (<15 Ma) (Yan et al., 2006). The pre- spreading magmatism predominantly occurred on the northern margin of the SCS (Xu et al., 2012). The magmatism contemporaneous with seafloor spreading was very weak in the margins mainly occurring at the fault intersections within the extensional sags (Yan et al., 2006). The post-spreading magmatism is strong along the lower slope of the northern margin of the SCS and in the central basin (Lüdmann and Wong, 1999; Lüdmann et al., 2001; Yan et al., 2001). The most known major seamounts in the central SCS basin are believed to be emplaced after the seafloor spreading and all younger than 15 Ma (Wang et al., 1985; Kudrass et al., 1986; Yan et al., 2008; Expedition 349 Scientists, 2014). Most of these seamounts are along, or to the north of, the relict spreading center and are composed mainly of alkali basalts with sub- ordinate tholeiites that display oceanic island basalt-type (OIB-type) geochemical characteristics (Xu et al., 2012) (Fig. 1). (consisting of the Dongsha uplift, the Panyu low uplift, and the Shenhu uplift), the central depression belt (consisting of the Zhu-II depression (comprising the Baiyun sag, Kaiping sag, Shunde sag, and the Yunkai low uplift) and the Chaoshan depression), the southern uplift belt and the southern depression belt (Pang et al., 1995; Sun et al., 2008; Xie et al., 2009; Shi et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2024; Li et al., 2025; Guan et al., 2016) (Fig. 1). The tectonic evolution of the PRMB can be divided into rifting, depression and compression stages (Clift and Lin, 2001; Pang et al., 1995, 2006). The rifting stage experienced the Shenhu movement, the first and the second episodes of the Zhuqiong movement lasting from the Late Cretaceous to the Early Oligocene (~53–30 Ma) (Liu et al., 2015; Jiang et al., 2018). The depression stage experienced the Nanhai movement and the Baiyun movement lasting from the Middle Oligocene to the Early-Middle Miocene (30–10.5 Ma) coeval with the spreading of the SCS basin and the global sea-level rising (Shi et al., 2014). The compression stage experienced the Dongsha movement (since 10.5 Ma), causing thrust and compression deformation, accompanied by magmatic activity (Jiang et al., 2018) (Fig. 2). Generally, the sag (e.g. the Kaiping sag) of the PRMB has a Mesozoic igneous basement overlain by Cenozoic sedi- mentary unit (Luan, 2024). From bottom to top, these include the Eocene Wenchang and Enping Formations of mainly lacustrine mud- stones, the Oligocene Zhuhai Formation of mainly marine delta sand- stones, the Miocene Zhujiang, Hanjiang, and Yuehai Formations, the Pliocene Wanshan Formation, and the Quaternary deposits of mainly marine mudstones (Shi et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2024; Li, et al., 2025; Guan et al., 2016) (Fig. 2). From bottom to top, these include the Eocene Wenchang and Enping Formations of mainly lacustrine mud- stones, the Oligocene Zhuhai Formation of mainly marine delta sand- stones, the Miocene Zhujiang, Hanjiang, and Yuehai Formations, the Pliocene Wanshan Formation, and the Quaternary deposits of mainly marine mudstones (Shi et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2024; Li, et al., 2025; Guan et al., 2016) (Fig. 2). As already revealed by the industry wells, the Wenchang Formation of semi-deep lacustrine mudstones and the Enping Formation of coal- bearing lacustrine mudstone are crucial hydrocarbon source rocks (Pang et al., 1995, 2006; Wang et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2022). The Zhuhai Formation (32–23.8 Ma) of giant neritic shelf delta deposits is charac- terized by southward-enhanced thermal subsidence along the southern slope of the Shenhu-Panyu low uplift (Liu et al., 2011). The Baiyun movement (23.8–10.5 Ma) was associated with the southward shift of the mid-ocean ridge in the SCS (Hall, 2002; Zhou et al., 2015). The movement cause rapid basement subsidence and sedimentary environ- ments shifting from shallow-water shelf (Zhuhai Formation) to deep- water (Zhujiang, Hanjiang and Yuehai Formations) on the PRMB (Pang et al., 2007; Wang et al., 2012) (Fig. 2). During the Upper Zhu- jiang Formation (21–16 Ma), marine transgression submerged the cen- tral uplift (the Panyu low uplift and the Shenhu uplift). A shallow water deltaic sedimentary system developed in the Upper Zhujiang Formation, characterized by multiple reservoir-seal assemblages comprising delta sands, and transgressive mudstones. The Dongsha movement was characterized by rejuvenated normal faults as preserved through the offsetting of the Wanshan Formation and the Quaternary-aged strata (Zhou et al., 2015). In reaction to third − order relative drops in sea level, sandy sedimentary sequences emerged above the third − order sequence boundaries, namely SB21, SB17.5, SB16.5, SB15.5, SB13.8, SB12.5, and SB10.5 (Wang et al., 2024). Journal of Asian Earth Sciences 298 (2026) 106924 3 Miocene-Pliocene boundary indicates that a dramatic tectono- stratigraphic event occurred around the SCS at about 5 Ma, coeval with major tectonic events such as the uplift of the Dongsha uplift and Taiwan orogen (Luan et al., 2011), and the reversal in strike-slip movement along the Red-River fault (Zhu et al., 2009). The Kaiping sag is located on the southwest of the Zhu-II depression (Figs. 1, 3). Lying in center of the Zhu-II depression of the PRMB, the NE- ENE trending Kaiping sag consists of a series of NE-ENE faulted half- grabens and grabens, with an area of about 6200 km2, and sediment thickness exceeding 5 km (Dai et al., 1998; Pang et al., 1995). The Cenozoic fills in the Kaiping sag can be subdivided into non-marine syn- rift and overlying marine post-rift mega-sequences separated by a basin- wide T70 unconformity (Dai et al., 1998) (Fig. 2). The Kaiping sag is tectonically bounded by uplifted blocks, creating a closed depositional system that restricts lateral sediment dispersal. The northern boundary: Shenhu uplift, a major structural high that blocks northward sediment transport and acts as a source of clastic material during uplift events (Guan et al., 2016); The southwestern boundary: Kaiping low uplift, a fault-bounded ridge that limits sediment escape to the southwest and controls the sag’s southern depocenter (Dai et al., 1998); The eastern boundary: Yunkai low uplift, which isolates the sag from the adjacent Baiyun sag and focuses sediment accumulation in the sag’s central area (Wang et al., 2018) (Figs. 1, 3). This boundary configuration traps sediments delivered by the Pearl River system and eroded from sur- rounding uplifts, preventing the downslope dispersal typical of open continental margins. Instead, sediments accumulate vertically within the sag, building thick, continuous successions-essential for forming the

controls the sag’s southern depocenter (Dai et al., 1998); The eastern boundary: Yunkai low uplift, which isolates the sag from the adjacent Baiyun sag and focuses sediment accumulation in the sag’s central area (Wang et al., 2018) (Figs. 1, 3). This boundary configuration traps sediments delivered by the Pearl River system and eroded from sur- rounding uplifts, preventing the downslope dispersal typical of open continental margins. Instead, sediments accumulate vertically within the sag, building thick, continuous successions-essential for forming the “overlying strata load” that defines the confined MTD environment. The Kaiping sag experienced elevated sedimentation rates from the Middle Miocene onwards (~15 Ma), coinciding with the peak uplift of the eastern Tibetan plateau (Royden et al., 2008; Ding et al., 2022). This uplift enhanced sediment supply to the PRMB, with the Kaiping sag acting as a primary depocenter. High sedimentation rates prevented sufficient compaction and cementation of sediments, maintaining thick unconsolidated succession. Critically, this high porosity and high pore- water pressure succession facilitate fluidization when triggered by tec- tonic events, thus providing foundation for the confined MTD development. 3. Data and methodology 3.1. Seismic database Three − dimensional (3D) seismic data spanning approximately 2500 km2 within the Kaiping sag were employed (Fig. 3). The 3D seismic data were acquired using 8 streamers each of 6000 m long in a group interval of 12.5 m and in a cable distance equally of 100 m. The streamers were towed 7–9 m below the sea surface. Two 5085 in3-air- gun arrays were deployed 6 m beneath the sea surface directly ahead of steamers and fired crosswise in a shot interval of 50 m. The minimum offset (distance between shot and first receiver) is 150 m. Seismic data underwent standard pre-stack processing to optimize signal quality and imaging. Key steps encompassed: navigation and amplitude

interval of 12.5 m and in a cable distance equally of 100 m. The streamers were towed 7–9 m below the sea surface. Two 5085 in3-air- gun arrays were deployed 6 m beneath the sea surface directly ahead of steamers and fired crosswise in a shot interval of 50 m. The minimum offset (distance between shot and first receiver) is 150 m. Seismic data underwent standard pre-stack processing to optimize signal quality and imaging. Key steps encompassed: navigation and amplitude corrections (tidal, true amplitude recovery with exponential gain and spherical divergence compensation); noise attenuation (f-k velocity filtering for low-velocity noise, random noise suppression, and demultiple tech- niques including gapped deconvolution post-stack); velocity analysis for normal moveout correction; and final stacking. Third-order seismic sequences Combining seismic interpretation with planktonic foraminifera and nannoplankton biostratigraphy, Qin (1996) determined second and third-order relative sea-level change across the study area (Fig. 2). Correlated by wireline logs and cores, Pang et al. (2006) divided the strata 23.8–8.2 Ma into eight sequences (Zhou et al., 2015). The se- quences, according to their basal boundaries SB23.8–SB10.5, are called SQ23.8, SQ21, SQ17.5 and SQ16.5 (Zhujiang Formation); SQ15.5, SQ13.8 and SQ12.5 (Hanjiang Formation); and SQ10.5 (Lower Yuehai Formation) (Fig. 2). The eight sequence boundaries are mainly marked by truncation and toplap terminations of seismic reflectors (Wang et al., 2012; Zhou et al., 2015), and within the lowstand systems tracts in response to third-order globe sea-level falls (Fig. 2), and all sandy in lithology (Chen et al., 2015; He et al., 2020). Well data has now confirmed that sandy sequences formed in sequences SQ23.8 and SQ21, which are the main hydrocarbon-bearing intervals (Wang et al., 2012; Shi et al., 2014; Chen et al., 2015), while muddy sequences formed in sequences SQ17.5–SQ10.5 (Zhou et al., 2015), which correspond to an overall rapid rise of 2nd-order relative sea level (Fig. 2). 3.3. Seismic attributes Seismic attribute maps extracted from 3D seismic volume often reveal many notable characteristics that are not readily apparent from seismic cross − sections or temporal structural maps (Bull et al., 2009; Hart, 1999). To delineate MTD geometry and internal character, mul- tiple seismic attributes were extracted from the 3D volume. Coherence facilitated mapping discontinuities (faults, stratigraphic edges) and identifying chaotic facies associated with deformation (Deckers, 2015). Chaotic sections on coherence maps are seen as low coherence marked usually by dark, mottled patterns (Alves, 2015; Gamboa and Alves, 2015; Hart, 1999). Chaotic and disrupted seismic facies are easily discriminated from these maps (Bull et al., 2009). Seismic attribute of Root-Mean-Square (RMS) aided in recognizing the lateral boundary of sand and mud, leveraging the typically higher amplitude response of sands versus muds. (Bull et al., 2009; Luan and Lunt, 2022). Variance and dip attributes provided additional constraints on structural complexity and paleo-slope gradients within the deformed intervals, crucial for characterizing the MTD morphology and transport direction (Bull et al., 2009; Luan and Lunt, 2022; Banerjee et al., 2020; Bai et al., 2021). 3.4. GR serves as a pri- mary lithological discriminator, consistenting with conventional log- ging principles, with relatively high value in mud-dominated intervals and low value in sand-rich intervals. SP exhibits an inverse response to GR for lithological identification. In sand-rich intervals, SP values shift toward right hand due to ion migration in porous, permeable sands; in mud-dominated intervals, SP values return to left hand (Guan et al., 2016; Luan and Lunt, 2022). DT and ZDEN exhibit similar response to GR in the study interval: higher values correspond to mud-rich strata (denser matrix), while lower values are associated with sand-rich strata. Regional seismic profile interpretation The area of SCS experienced the Mesozoic volcanic arc or volcanic forearc (Li et al., 2018) to the Cenozoic rifting that resulted in the lithospheric breakup and finally seafloor spreading (Taylor and Hayes, 1983; Sun et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019). The Mesozoic meta-sedi- mentary composition (Larsen et al., 2018), volcanic arc composition (Cui et al., 2021; Shao et al., 2017), the strongly mid-lower crust anisotropy (Pang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2020a, b), and the Mesozoic thrust faults (Ye et al., 2018) of the northern margin of SCS are studied by either drilling wells and/or seismic data, but some questions regarding its pre-breakup evolution still remain open (Sun et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021a, b; Zhao et al., 2020) (Fig. 4). Both Moho and Tg (top of basement), are fairly well recognizable by high-amplitude reflectors along regional seismic profiles across the study area (Fig. 4). Moho at 10 s TWT at the coastal area goes up to 8.5 s TWT to the oceanic basin. Tg is controlled mainly by major normal faults that bound the tilted conti- nental blocks. Confined between Moho and Tg, the continental blocks thinned generally oceanward and tilted by normal faults forming the rifted accommodation spaces for the subsequent sedimentation, namely from north to south the Northern Depression, the Central Depression and the Southern

profiles across the study area (Fig. 4). Moho at 10 s TWT at the coastal area goes up to 8.5 s TWT to the oceanic basin. Tg is controlled mainly by major normal faults that bound the tilted conti- nental blocks. Confined between Moho and Tg, the continental blocks thinned generally oceanward and tilted by normal faults forming the rifted accommodation spaces for the subsequent sedimentation, namely from north to south the Northern Depression, the Central Depression and the Southern Depression (Figs. 1, 4). The terrestrial Wenchang and Enping Formations (~65–30 Ma, Tg-T70) filled within the rift space coincide with the rifting stage (Figs. 2, 4). The ODP/IODP drilling results at Sites 1501 (Larsen et al., 2018), 1505 (Jian et al., 2018), 1148 (Wang et al., 2000) and 1435 (Li et al., 2015) confirmed the ages, lithologies, and major hiatuses from Tg upward. Oceanic crust has successfully been cored at Sites U1500 (Stock et al., 2018) and U1503 (Childress and The Expedition 368X Scientists, 2019) indicating the onset of oceanic spreading at 30 Ma (Figs. 1, 2). Both sites recovered pillow basalt with a MORB composition. These basalts are over lain by post-rift sediments (T70 and above) of deep marine pelagic sediments. The sedimentary environments changed upwards from lacustrine/paralic/subaerial Wenchang and Enping Formations to bathyal marine formations at T70. Seafloor spreading in the SCS occurred between 30–16 Ma, corre- sponding to the marine sedimentation between T70 and T40 (Briais et al., 1993; Taylor and Hayes, 1983; Cullen et al., 2010; Ogg, 2020) (Figs. 2, 4). The Kaiping sag belongs to the Central Depression belt (Fig. 1). spreading in the SCS occurred between 30–16 Ma, corre- sponding to the marine sedimentation between T70 and T40 (Briais et al., 1993; Taylor and Hayes, 1983; Cullen et al., 2010; Ogg, 2020) (Figs. 2, 4). The Kaiping sag belongs to the Central Depression belt (Fig. 1). The study area encompasses most of the Kaiping sag and part of the Shenhu uplift to the north, Kaiping low uplift to the southwest, Shunde sag to the south, and Yunkai low uplift to the east (Figs. 1, 3, 5). The Kaiping sag evolved through the same stages of tectonic movement as detailed above. The Zhuqiong movement was characterized by extreme exten- sion in response to supra-detachment faults along the northern margin of SCS (Wang et al., 2018), resulting in the hydrocarbon-rich Wenchang and Enping Formations (~65–32 Ma, Tg-T70) in half-graben (Pang et al., 2006) (Figs. 2, 4). The Nanhai movement was characterized by southward-enhanced thermal subsidence along the southern margin of the Shenhu uplift, with giant neritic shelf delta Zhuhai Formation (30–23.8 Ma, T70-T60; Liu et al., 2011) (Figs. 2, 4). The Baiyun move- ment was associated with the southward shift of the mid-ocean ridge in the SCS (Hall, 2002; Zhou et al., 2015), and the rapid basement subsi- dence and sedimentary environments shifting from shallow-water shelf to deep-water (23.8–16.5 Ma, Zhujiang (T60-T40); Pang et al., 2007; Wang et al., 2012) (Figs. 2, 4). The deep-water environment continued through the post-spreading thermal subsidence period until the Dongsha movement (16.5–10.5 Ma, T40-T32 Hanjiang Formation, 10.5–5.3 Ma, T32-T30, Yuehai Formation) and corresponding high system tracts mainly deep-water muds were deposited (Fig. 5). Two MTDs have been identified in the Kaiping Sag: MTD-1, a chaotic interval between T40 and T35 within the lower Hanjiang Formation (15.5–13.8 Ma), which corresponds to the 3rd-order high sea level sequence SQ15.5; and MTD-2, a chaotic interval between T32 and T31 within the lower Yuehai Formation (10.5–8.2 Ma), associated with the 3rd-order high sea level sequence SQ10.5. Both intervals correspond to the deep-water mud-dominated highstand systems tracts (HST) documented in Figs. 2 and 5. Critically, post-spreading compressive tectonism- initiated ~ 16 Ma after SCS seafloor spreading cessation, intensified by the Dongsha movement since 10.5 Ma and T20 uplift event- provided both the “precondition” and “trigger” for these MTDs. First, compression-driven Shenhu uplift created gentle slopes within the Kaiping sag. Concurrently, high sedimentation rates, driven by eastern Tibetan plateau uplift since 15 Ma, accumulated thick unconsolidated mudstones (≥2 km) on

correspond to the deep-water mud-dominated highstand systems tracts (HST) documented in Figs. 2 and 5. Critically, post-spreading compressive tectonism- initiated ~ 16 Ma after SCS seafloor spreading cessation, intensified by the Dongsha movement since 10.5 Ma and T20 uplift event- provided both the “precondition” and “trigger” for these MTDs. First, compression-driven Shenhu uplift created gentle slopes within the Kaiping sag. Concurrently, high sedimentation rates, driven by eastern Tibetan plateau uplift since 15 Ma, accumulated thick unconsolidated mudstones (≥2 km) on these slopes, establishing the material precon- dition for instability. Additionally, compression-induced three docu- mented mega-earthquakes (mge-1 at T35 ~ 13.8 Ma, mge-2 at T31 ~ 8.6 Ma, and mge-3 in the Quaternary; Figs. 6, 7), served as the direct trigger for fluidization of the unconsolidated slopes, ultimately forming the confined MTDs analyzed herein. 4.2. Tectonic restoration Since the initiation of oceanic seafloor spreading, the SCS basin has experienced the process of subsidence, while the northern SCS margin transitioned from a terrestrial environment (Tg-T70) to a marine setting (T70-upward) (Fig. 4). This transition was likely driven by compression associated with oceanic spreading, leading to extensive squeezing of the seismic sequence between T70 (30 Ma) and T60 (23.8 Ma). This interval ehibits chaotic facies throughout the entire sequence along the north- –south profile (Chen et al., 2017; Barckhausen et al., 2014; Ningthoujam and Pe, 2020) (Fig. 6d). By T60 (23.8 Ma), the rift basins in the northern SCS were completely filled, and the embryonic form of the northern continental shelf and slope began to develop (Fig. 4). The Kaiping sag is located at the slope break of the continental shelf (Figs. 1, 3). Concur- rently, the Zhujiang Formation, which overlies T60, developed parallel, horizontal sequences during the middle ocean ridge jump from the East- subbasin to the Central basin (Guan et al.,

and Pe, 2020) (Fig. 6d). By T60 (23.8 Ma), the rift basins in the northern SCS were completely filled, and the embryonic form of the northern continental shelf and slope began to develop (Fig. 4). The Kaiping sag is located at the slope break of the continental shelf (Figs. 1, 3). Concur- rently, the Zhujiang Formation, which overlies T60, developed parallel, horizontal sequences during the middle ocean ridge jump from the East- subbasin to the Central basin (Guan et al., 2021) (Figs. 1, 4, 5, 6d). To constrain the timing and intensity of post-spreading deformation, tectonic restoration was performed using horizon flattening (on the T40 and T35 reflection horizons) combined with stratigraphic thickness balancing-a standard approach for quantifying paleo-formation in rifted margins (Zhang et al., 2020a, b; Luan et al., 2024) (Fig. 6). This method allowed us to “remove” the effects of post-T40, T35 subsidence and thrusting, revealing the original geometry of pre-T40, T35 strata and associated structures. When flattening the T40 horizon, prominent thrust and compression features vanished (Fig. 6c), indicating that the distinct thrust zones and compression zones within T60-T40 were caused by event after T40 (Fig. 6c). The same applies to T35. Com- pressions of various intensities, forms and phases (14.1–13.8 Ma, T40- T35; 11–6 Ma, T32-T30; Zhang et al., 2020a, b) have been observed in the SCS basin and its northern margin since the cessation of the oceanic spreading at 16.5 Ma (Yan et al., 2008; Zhao et al., 2018; Pourhiet et al., 2018) (Fig. 7). The onset of compressions coincided with the post- spreading volcanisms along the fossil spreading ridge of the SCS (Zhao et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2020a, b), and is attributed to the NNW- directed convergence of the Taiwan–Luzon arc (Yan et al., 2008; Zhang et al., 2020a, b), as well

SCS basin and its northern margin since the cessation of the oceanic spreading at 16.5 Ma (Yan et al., 2008; Zhao et al., 2018; Pourhiet et al., 2018) (Fig. 7). The onset of compressions coincided with the post- spreading volcanisms along the fossil spreading ridge of the SCS (Zhao et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2020a, b), and is attributed to the NNW- directed convergence of the Taiwan–Luzon arc (Yan et al., 2008; Zhang et al., 2020a, b), as well as far-field compression from the Hi- malayan orogeny (Qu et al., 2025; Ding et al., 2020; Song et al., 2024) (Fig. 7). The northern margin of the SCS exhibited the highest sedimentation rate during the T60-T40 interval (Figs. 4, 5, 6, 7), with a 10-km-thick sedimentary volume accumulated rapidly across the continental shelf and slope-coinciding with the formation of the steep topographic gra- dients in the Kaiping sag. The high sedimentation rates and elevated pore-water pressures fostered the slope instability potential. Strati- graphic and structural evidence, supported by restored profiles, infers a mega-earthquake event (mge-1) at T35 (13.8 Ma, coeval with the development of a new subduction of Java Trench and new volcanic arc in southern Java (Luan et al., 2024). The seismic criteria used to distinguish seismogenic defor- mation from other mechanisms (e.g., gravity-driven sliding) include: (1) abrupt, discrete deformation zones: MTD intervals contain narrow, sharply bounded zones of chaotic reflectors, lacking the gradual, wide- spread strain associated with protracted deformation (e.g., Fig. 6c shows a confined shear zone beneath the allochthonous wedge, not distributed layer-parallel slippage); (2) syn-deformational stratigraphic relation- ships: Deformed units are immediately overlain by conformable, un- disturbed strata (e.g., the allochthonous wedge at mge-1 is capped by continuous T35–T32 reflections), indicating rapid, episodic emplace- ment rather than slow, incremental deformation; (3) association with thrust fault reactivation: Each inferred mega-earthquake coincides with large (~20 ms in TWT) thrust offsets (e.g., mge-1 aligns with a thrust zone active at ~ 13.8 Ma, Fig. 6c), consistent with tectonic earthquakes correlating with fault slip in rifted margins (Yan et al., 2008; Luan et al., 2024). 5. Discussion 5.1. MTD in a confined environment For the past decades, continental margins provide an ideal setting for the mass transport deposits (MTDs). MTDs represent the failure, dislodgement, and downslope movement and deposition of sediment under the influence of gravity (Shanmugam et al., 1994; Shanmugam, 2020). After failure, the sediment moves downslope under gravity exhibiting extreme variability in mechanics (Shanmugam, 2020), ranging from solid blocks of kilometre-size coherence translation with or without rotation (Varnes, 1978) to transportation of small-size particles in debris flows or in turbidity flows (Cruden, 2003). According to Shanmugam’s (2020) recent classification, MTDs consist of three basic types: (1) slides, characterized by coherent translation without rotation; (2) slumps, involving coherent translation with rotation; and (3) debris flow. Notably, turbidity flows are excluded from this classification (Shanmugam, 2020; Dott, 1963). Typically, MTDs exhibit a headwall area featuring

Notably, turbidity flows are excluded from this classification (Shanmugam, 2020; Dott, 1963). Typically, MTDs exhibit a headwall area featuring a headwall scar, and in some cases, a moat (Yang et al., Fig. 11. Most MTDs slide along an upward-concave glide plane with an uneven surface, which is subsequently overlain by turbidite contourite displaying onlapping sequence structures (Kiswaka et al., 2025). The chaotic facies in MTD-1 and MTD-2 were examined using seismic attribute technology (Fig. 8). In our case, however, we observe both headwall and toe areas, but without headwall scarps and headwall moats, and also without toe area thrust pattern (Kiswaka et al., 2024; Jing et al., 2022) (Figs. 8, 9). The chaotic facies suggest debris or turbidity flow activity, characterized by rafted blocks suspended within the flows (Fig. 9). A critical distinction from conventional MTDs lies in the absence of the characteristic uneven surface typically associated with overlying turbidite contourites (Kiswaka et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2024) (Fig. 9). The chaotic facies suggest debris or turbidity flow activity, characterized by rafted blocks suspended within the flows (Fig. 9). A critical distinction from conventional MTDs lies in the absence of the characteristic uneven surface typically associated with overlying turbidite contourites (Kiswaka et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2024) (Fig. 9). We interpret this feature as indicative of in-situ liquefaction within a confined space (where strata were already overlain by overburden), rather than a submarine slide occurring on an open seafloor. Along the downslope direction (Figs. 9d, e), the base of the chaotic facies exhibits a rugged texture, contrasting with the typical smooth, concave-upward glide planes over which the MDTs usually move. Across the slope, the base of the chaotic facies displays a morphology (Fig. 8) resembling river channels; however, these channels are filled with chaotic facies rather than turbidite contourites, and their density is anomalously high (10 river channels per 25 km in this study vs 1 channel per 25 km in South China Sea slope context, Yang et al., 2024). In the continental slope region, we interpret these features as grooves, which represent the incision caused by the limited transport of the in-situ liq- uefied materials along the shear base and specify the orientation of the liquefied mass migration (Bull et al., 2009; Gee et al., 2007) (Figs. 8, 9). 5.2. Trigger mechanism MTDs generate primarily due to three factors: reduced support at the toe of the continental slope, excessive loading, and elevated pore-water pressure. They are generally governed by gravitational instability. Trigger mechanism MTDs generate primarily due to three factors: reduced support at the toe of the continental slope, excessive loading, and elevated pore-water pressure. They are generally governed by gravitational instability. Several factors have been proposed as potential triggers for seafloor gravitational instabilities, including high sedimentation rate (Hampton et al., 1996; Kuhlmann et al., 2017; Leynaud et al., 2007), sea level fluctuations (Smith et al., 2013; Urlaub et al., 2013), gas hydrate dissociation (Lee, 2009; Maslin et al., 2004), and steeper slope gradients (Owen et al., 2007; Urlaub et al., 2015). The steep slope gradients over 10˚have been reported for the landslide scarps area, and about 2˚for the slope gradients next to the headwall area in the northern continental margin of the SCS (Yang et al., 2024). For most of the MDTs in the sub- arctic mid-Norwegian margin, for almost its entire length, a gradient reaching 1˚is believed as the threshold value to trigger the gravity instability (Jing et al., 2022; Canals et al., 2004; He and Zhong, 2015). The Kaiping sag exhibits a gentle slope gradient of ~ 0.3–0.6◦, as evi- denced by seismic profiles (Fig. 10). This gradient is significantly lower than the quantitative thresholds for static slope stability (5-10◦, Cruden, 2003), and also falls below the threshold value reported for the sub- arctic mid-Norwegian margin (1˚, Jing et al., 2022; Canals et al., 2004; He and Zhong, 2015). The low slope gradient (~0.3–0.6◦) in the Kaiping sag directly contradicts the prevailing understanding that MTDs form via gravity-driven sliding on steep slopes (Shanmugam, 2012, 2020, 2021). Instead, our study proposes a hypothesis that dynamic triggers, such as mega-earthquakes, induce the chaotic facies

the quantitative thresholds for static slope stability (5-10◦, Cruden, 2003), and also falls below the threshold value reported for the sub- arctic mid-Norwegian margin (1˚, Jing et al., 2022; Canals et al., 2004; He and Zhong, 2015). The low slope gradient (~0.3–0.6◦) in the Kaiping sag directly contradicts the prevailing understanding that MTDs form via gravity-driven sliding on steep slopes (Shanmugam, 2012, 2020, 2021). Instead, our study proposes a hypothesis that dynamic triggers, such as mega-earthquakes, induce the chaotic facies and the subsequent limited material migration. Mega-earthquakes generate dynamic shocks that liquefy unconsolidated sediments, enabling chaotic facies genera- tion regardless of whether slopes are gentle or steep. Gravity-driven mechanisms rely primarily on slope angle, while our hypothesis high- light the crucial role of earthquake shock in the formation of the chaotic facies within confined environments. Earthquakes are recognized as the primary cause of liquefaction in submarine unconsolidated layers (Du et al., 2025). Earthquake induced liquefaction was first reported in 1960 s (Ambraseys and Sarma, 1969; Seed, 1979; Sims, 1973, 1975). Hance (2003) reported a database of 534 submarine mass liquefaction events with over 40 % attributed to earthquakes. In the Mediterranean Sea, earthquakes account for nearly half of the 696 recorded submarine liquefaction (Urgeles and Camer- lenghi, 2013). Liquefaction is associated with the increasing in pore pressure generated by earthquake shocks and the rising of buried low- density materials. Phenomena relate to liquefaction after earthquakes including geyser-like fountains, mud volcanoes and sediment eruption (Obermeier, 1996). Only sufficiently large earthquakes can induce liquefaction and fluidization effects (Owen and Moretti, 2008). Liquefaction is unlikely in earthquake below magnitude 5, while stronger quakes may cause it over a broader area (Allen, 1986; Ambraseys, 1988; Galli, 2000). Deeper layer lique- faction by subsequent earthquake shock has not been reported, it is important to note that for the liquefaction of these deeply buried layers to occur, they must remain unconsolidated (Davenport et al., 1994; Owen and Moretti, 2008). ODP/IODP expeditions in the SCS have revealed significant depth of unconsolidated rock volumes, which unexpected before. IODP Expedi- tion 349 (2014) drilled into the eastern and southwestern sub-basins of the SCS, penetrating ~ 1,000 m of sediment to reach basaltic basement rocks. Unconsolidated sediments, primarily siliceous ooze and carbon- ate deposits, extended to depths of ~ 600 m due to high sedimentation rates (~8 cm/kyr) following seafloor spreading (~16 Ma, T40 in this study). In the eastern sub-basin, hydrothermal alteration zones showed unconsolidated sediments intermixed with epidote veins at depths of ~ 900 m, where active fluid circulation prevented cementation (Expedition 349 Scientists, 2014). IODP Expeditions 367/368 (2017–2018) focused on the northern SCS margin to study continental breakup mechanisms. In

basement rocks. Unconsolidated sediments, primarily siliceous ooze and carbon- ate deposits, extended to depths of ~ 600 m due to high sedimentation rates (~8 cm/kyr) following seafloor spreading (~16 Ma, T40 in this study). In the eastern sub-basin, hydrothermal alteration zones showed unconsolidated sediments intermixed with epidote veins at depths of ~ 900 m, where active fluid circulation prevented cementation (Expedition 349 Scientists, 2014). IODP Expeditions 367/368 (2017–2018) focused on the northern SCS margin to study continental breakup mechanisms. In the Liwan sag (ultra-thinned continental margin), unconsolidated sediments reached ~ 800 m, attributed to low geothermal gradients (<30 ◦C/km) and rapid post-rift subsidence (since T70 in this paper) (Sun et al., 2018). ODP Leg 184 (1999) drilled un- consolidated clays and siliceous ooze in the central SCS at depth of ~ 300–400 m below seafloor. This ~ 400 m-thick layer was identified as an early Miocene soft stratum with high organic content, which may have delayed the diagenesis of the stratum (Wang et al., 2000). IODP 349 drilled submarine fan deposits in the southwestern sub-basin of SCS exhibited unconsolidated layers up to ~ 1,000 m, driven by over- pressure systems from tectonic compression near the Manila Trench (Expedition 349 Scientists, 2014). Drilling well A within our 3D seismic survey revealed the presence of ’soft’ lithology, as indicated by both cuttings analysis and core samples obtained from depths exceeding 2000 m (Fig. 11). Cuttings collected during the drilling process provided initial insights into the lithological composition and diagenetic charac- teristic at various intervals, showing a predominance of fine-grained sand and mud that were poorly cemented and soft. Subsequent core recovered at ~ 2000 m further confirmed these observations. The li- thology is dark grey silty mudstone. For the prediction of the submarine unconsolidated layer liquefaction, however, earth- quakes have the nature of randomness and unpredictability, on the other hand, the vibration generated by earthquakes acts on all strata within the propagation range of seismic waves. In this way, it should be meaningful to predict which stratum may liquefy during the earthquake. (Du et al., 2025; Hance, 2003; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013). For the prediction of the submarine unconsolidated layer liquefaction, however, earth- quakes have the nature of randomness and unpredictability, on the other hand, the vibration generated by earthquakes acts on all strata within the propagation range of seismic waves. In this way, it should be meaningful to predict which stratum may liquefy during the earthquake. Generally speaking, the prerequisite for submarine unconsolidated layer liquefaction is high sedimentation rate and high pore-water content (Du et al., 2025; Hance, 2003; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013). Sun et al. (2018) reported that four Quaternary submarine landslides along the northern margin of SCS all occurred during the high sea level period. These events are interpreted to be favored by high sea levels, elevated pore-water content, and a lower sand-mud ratio, which promote me- chanical sliding. Seismic data and ODP/IODP records from the northern SCS slope reveal extensive Quaternary landslides (~1,035 km3), pri- marily influenced by tectonic overpressure (earthquake-triggered) rather than deltaic collapse (gravity driven). The determined ages of the four landslides- ~1.59 Ma, 0.79 Ma, 0.54 Ma and 0.19 Ma −all fall within high sea level periods (Sun et al., 2018) (Fig. 12). Notably, the two deformed strata (T40-T35 and T32-T31) discussed in this study are align with high sea level periods (Fig. 12). The increased GR logging values observed at MTD-2 and MTD-1 correspond well with the soft, poorly cemented argillaceous lithology identified in the drilling cuttings (Fig. 11). The Tibetan plateau is the Earth’s broadest and highest orogenic system, with a mean elevation of ~ 5 km. The uplift of the plateau profoundly influenced the sedimentary supply to Asian basins including the PRMB (Fig. 1). Geological data indicate that India began colliding with Asia 65–60 Ma ago (Ding et al., 2022). The general perceptions are that the Tibet grew outward from the Indus-Tsangpo suture zone (ITSZ) since the Eocene (Ding et al., 2022). However, phases of regional and rapid surface uplift during the past 20 Ma (Ding et al., 2022). Further- more, the majority of surface uplift in eastern Tibet is widely considered to have occurred at < 15 Ma (Royden et al., 2008) (Fig. 12). This time period coincides with the deposition of the deformed strata T40-T35 and T32-T31, characterized by their high sedimentation rate (Figs. 7, 12). Under elevated stress conditions (e.g. ultra-high pore-pressure or earthquake triggering), submarine unconsolidated layer undergo liquefaction (Du et al., 2025; Hance, 2003; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013). The literature identifies key indicators of large-scale liquefaction on continental margins, including pockmarks, mud diapirs, and tectonic uplift (Alves, 2010; Canals et al., 2004; Gee et al., 2007; Lamarche et al., 2008) (Fig. 11). Such large-scale liquefaction significantly enhances the mobility of submarine unconsolidated layers, leading to their subse- quent fluidization (Deckers, 2015; Martínez et al., 2005; Gong et al., 2014; Strasser et al., 2011). Pockmarks initiated development in the stratum above T31, progressively migrating upward toward structural highs and concentrating to form pockmark groups between T31 and T30 (Figs. 13, 14). et al., 2008) (Fig. 11). Such large-scale liquefaction significantly enhances the mobility of submarine unconsolidated layers, leading to their subse- quent fluidization (Deckers, 2015; Martínez et al., 2005; Gong et al., 2014; Strasser et al., 2011). Pockmarks initiated development in the stratum above T31, progressively migrating upward toward structural highs and concentrating to form pockmark groups between T31 and T30 (Figs. 13, 14). The mge-3 earthquake generated the formation of the diapir struc- tures at T10 time (diagnosed by the onlapping at T10, as illustrated in Fig. 14). This event likely caused liquefaction of previously deposited strata, facilitating upward fluid migration via diapiric structures and associated thrust faults. The pockmark cluster observed between T31 and T30 (Fig. 14), concentrated on the structure high (Fig. 13b), and the gas zones accumulated at T10 (Fig. 13a) can be reasonably attributed to the combined effects of liquefaction by mega-earthquake, and subse- quent fluid migration by folding and uplift, and compressional thrusting (Du et al., 2025; Hance, 2003; Urgeles and Camerlenghi, 2013). The development of the diapiric structure may provide evidence that the entire stratum may remain in an unconsolidated state (Fig. 11). The diapiric structures penetrate the entire sediment column and extend to the seafloor, indicating that the fluid activity through these structures may persist to the present day following their formation (Figs. 13, 14). 6. Conclusions Integrated analysis incorporating detailed seismic interpretation, well data analysis, tectonic restoration, seismic attributes, and slope stability assessment of the Kaiping sag MTDs yields new insights into their triggering mechanisms: 1. Tectonic compression established mainly after the cessation of the SCS oceanic spreading (after 16 Ma) play a fundamental role in slope activities of north margin of SCS. Mega-earthquakes identified dur- ing the compression serve as triggering role for the development of MTD-2 and MTD-1. This hypothesis

Conclusions Integrated analysis incorporating detailed seismic interpretation, well data analysis, tectonic restoration, seismic attributes, and slope stability assessment of the Kaiping sag MTDs yields new insights into their triggering mechanisms: 1. Tectonic compression established mainly after the cessation of the SCS oceanic spreading (after 16 Ma) play a fundamental role in slope activities of north margin of SCS. Mega-earthquakes identified dur- ing the compression serve as triggering role for the development of MTD-2 and MTD-1. This hypothesis differs from traditional MTDs in driven forces and in spatial unconfined environment. Fig. 14. The eastern Tibetan plateau attained its maximum elevation approximately 15 Ma ago, thereby providing substantial sediment supply to the Kaiping sag, which has remained largely unconsoli- dated since that period. Beside the higher sedimentation rate, both the MTD-1 and −2 developed during a period of higher sea level, which meet the prerequisite of high porosity. 3. The presence of pockmarks and diapir structures confirms the compression background and liquefaction, further supporting the formation of the MTDs in a confined environment.
